metadata.explanation: A single, clearly diagnosed fix in one function is a textbook case for direct execution; plan mode and Explore-subagent surveys are overhead better reserved for tasks with real architectural ambiguity or multi-file scope.

cards.prompt: What is the primary reason plan mode is recommended before committing to changes on a task with multiple valid implementation approaches?
cards.answer: It enables safe exploration and comparison of designs before any code changes are made, avoiding costly rework.

metadata.subdomain: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
choices: It enables safe exploration and comparison of designs before any code changes are made, avoiding costly rework.
It automatically selects the fastest-running approach by silently benchmarking each candidate option directly in the production environment.
It disables all write tools entirely so that absolutely no file can ever be modified for the remainder of the session.
It requires a second engineer's formal sign-off before any plan mode session is permitted to end or transition to execution.
metadata.explanation: Plan mode's value is letting Claude explore and design without prematurely committing to an approach, avoiding expensive rework; it doesn't disable tools entirely, nor does it involve production benchmarking or mandatory sign-off.

cards.prompt: A migration task requires surveying roughly 200 files to catalog every usage of a deprecated API before a plan can be drafted. Doing this discovery inline risks filling up the main conversation's context. What's the recommended approach?
cards.answer: Delegate discovery to an Explore subagent, which isolates the verbose search output and returns a condensed summary.

metadata.subdomain: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
choices: Read all 200 files directly into the main conversation at once, trusting confidently that nothing important ends up missed.
Delegate discovery to an Explore subagent, which isolates the verbose search output and returns a condensed summary.
Skip discovery entirely and simply let plan mode infer every usage without ever reading a single file.
Switch straight to direct execution immediately, fixing each usage one at a time as it happens to be discovered.
metadata.explanation: The Explore subagent exists precisely to isolate verbose discovery work and prevent context window exhaustion, returning only the useful summary; reading all 200 files inline would blow up context, and skipping discovery undermines the plan's accuracy.

cards.prompt: A team is migrating from one HTTP client library to another across the codebase. They've used plan mode to investigate call sites and settle on a migration strategy. What is the appropriate next step for the mechanical, well-understood parts of the change?
cards.answer: Switch to direct execution to implement the already-planned changes, now that the design decisions are settled.

metadata.subdomain: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
choices: Re-enter plan mode again for every individual file edit, even though the overall approach was already fully agreed on.
Discard the plan entirely and let Claude re-explore the whole codebase again from scratch before making a single edit.
Switch to direct execution to implement the already-planned changes, now that the design decisions are settled.
Require a brand-new Explore subagent invocation before every single individual file change made during implementation.
metadata.explanation: Combining plan mode for investigation with direct execution for the settled implementation is the documented pattern -- once the approach is decided, re-planning or re-exploring every file adds unnecessary overhead.

cards.prompt: Before writing any code, a team needs to choose between SQS and Kafka for a new event pipeline, each implying very different deployment and operations requirements. How should Claude Code be used at this stage?
cards.answer: Plan mode, to evaluate the trade-offs of each infrastructure approach before any implementation begins.

metadata.subdomain: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
choices: Direct execution, implementing the SQS approach first and reverting later if it turns out to be wrong.
A skill invoked with allowed-tools restricted to Read only, since infrastructure decisions don't require any exploration.
A path-specific rule scoped to the infra/ directory to force a specific choice automatically.
Plan mode, to evaluate the trade-offs of each infrastructure approach before any implementation begins.
metadata.explanation: Choosing between architecturally different infrastructure approaches is exactly the multiple-valid-approaches case plan mode is meant for; implementing one option live and reverting later risks the costly rework plan mode is designed to prevent.

cards.prompt: A product manager asks for one additional validation check: ensure a discount code field cannot be blank before submission, in an existing form-handling function. What's the appropriate approach?
cards.answer: Direct execution, since this is a simple, well-scoped addition to one function.

metadata.subdomain: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
choices: Direct execution, since this is a simple, well-scoped addition to one function.
Plan mode, treating it as a multi-file architectural change requiring design comparisons.
A dedicated Explore subagent survey of the entire form-handling module before making any edit.
A new custom skill with context: fork to isolate the validation logic from the main session.
metadata.explanation: A single, clearly-scoped validation addition is the canonical direct-execution case; invoking plan mode, an Explore subagent, or a forked skill for something this small is unnecessary process overhead.

cards.prompt: Which of the following best describes when plan mode should be used, according to Claude Code best practices?
cards.answer: For complex tasks with large-scale changes, multiple valid approaches, or architectural decisions across many files.

metadata.subdomain: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
choices: For absolutely every single code change whatsoever, regardless of size, applied as a blanket, one-size-fits-all safety policy.
For complex tasks with large-scale changes, multiple valid approaches, or architectural decisions across many files.
Only when the change happens to involve modifying the CLAUDE.md configuration file itself, and never otherwise.
Only when running Claude Code inside an automated CI pipeline rather than in an interactive terminal session.
metadata.explanation: Plan mode's purpose is tied to complexity and architectural stakes, not blanket caution or the specific file being touched; using it for every trivial change, or reserving it only for CI or CLAUDE.md edits, misrepresents its intended use.

cards.prompt: A team needs to upgrade a widely-used library to a new major version with breaking API changes affecting more than 45 files across the codebase. What's the recommended first move?
cards.answer: Enter plan mode to map out the scope of breaking changes and design a migration approach first.

metadata.subdomain: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
choices: Start editing files alphabetically using direct execution, fixing each breakage individually as it surfaces.
Downgrade the library instead, since any change affecting 45+ files should always be avoided outright.
Enter plan mode to map out the scope of breaking changes and design a migration approach first.
Write a single .claude/rules/ file describing the new API and expect all 45+ files to update themselves.
metadata.explanation: A breaking, cross-cutting change of this scale is squarely the large-scale, multi-file case plan mode addresses, letting the team design the migration strategy before touching code; jumping straight to alphabetical direct execution risks the exact costly rework plan mode prevents.

cards.prompt: A CI failure produces a clear, one-line stack trace pointing to an off-by-one error inside a single loop in one file. A junior engineer suggests entering plan mode to investigate architectural alternatives before fixing it. Is this the right call?
cards.answer: No -- this is a simple, well-scoped bug with a clear cause, so direct execution fits better than plan mode.

metadata.subdomain: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
choices: Yes -- any bug found via a CI pipeline should always go through plan mode first, regardless of its actual scope.
Yes -- off-by-one errors always indicate some deeper underlying architectural issue that requires a full redesign.
No -- but only because plan mode technically cannot ever be invoked for bugs that were found in CI log output.
No -- this is a simple, well-scoped bug with a clear cause, so direct execution fits better than plan mode.
metadata.explanation: A clear-cause, single-file bug is the direct-execution case, not a candidate for architectural exploration; plan mode isn't barred from CI-sourced bugs in general, it's simply unnecessary overhead for one this well-scoped.

cards.prompt: A developer's prose description asking Claude to 'normalize phone numbers to a standard format' produces inconsistent results across different attempts. What's the most effective way to fix this?
cards.answer: Provide 2-3 concrete input/output examples showing exactly how specific phone numbers should be transformed.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: Provide 2-3 concrete input/output examples showing exactly how specific phone numbers should be transformed.
Repeat the exact same prose instruction three or four more times within the same message purely for emphasis.
Remove the instruction from the prompt and let Claude infer the format from the codebase's variable names.
Switch to plan mode so that Claude designs the entire phone-number normalization algorithm architecturally first.
metadata.explanation: Concrete input/output examples are the documented, effective way to communicate transformations that prose alone renders ambiguous; repeating the same words or invoking plan mode doesn't resolve the underlying ambiguity.

cards.prompt: A team wants Claude Code to implement a new fuzzy-matching scoring function and converge on correct behavior systematically rather than guessing. What iteration technique fits best?
cards.answer: Write a test suite covering expected behavior and edge cases first, then iterate by sharing test failures with Claude.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: Ask Claude to write the function once and accept it without any test coverage, since fuzzy matching is inherently subjective.
Write a test suite covering expected behavior and edge cases first, then iterate by sharing test failures with Claude.
Have Claude write the tests and the implementation in the same message with no separation between them.
Skip iteration and use the interview pattern instead, which replaces the need for any test suite.
metadata.explanation: Test-driven iteration -- writing tests first, then feeding back failures -- is the documented technique for progressive improvement toward well-defined expected behavior; the interview pattern serves a different purpose, surfacing unknown considerations, not replacing tests.

cards.prompt: Before implementing a new caching layer for an endpoint, a developer unfamiliar with caching wants Claude to surface considerations like invalidation strategy and failure modes they might not have thought of. What technique should they use?
cards.answer: The interview pattern -- have Claude ask clarifying questions before implementation begins.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: The test-driven iteration pattern, since writing failing tests always reveals invalidation strategies automatically.
Plan mode alone, without any additional prompting, since plan mode inherently asks the developer questions.
The interview pattern -- have Claude ask clarifying questions before implementation begins.
Providing 2-3 concrete input/output examples of cache hits and misses.
metadata.explanation: The interview pattern -- Claude asking questions to surface unanticipated design considerations -- is specifically suited to unfamiliar domains like cache invalidation strategy; concrete I/O examples help clarify transformations, not surface unknown unknowns.

cards.prompt: A data migration script keeps mishandling null values from a legacy export, and repeated prose instructions like 'handle nulls gracefully' haven't fixed it. What's the most effective next step?
cards.answer: Provide specific test cases with example input rows containing nulls and the exact expected output for each.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: Add the word "gracefully" in bold to the existing prose instruction and try again.
Switch the entire migration to plan mode so Claude designs a completely new script from scratch.
Remove all mentions of null handling from the prompt and let Claude discover the issue via CI failures later.
Provide specific test cases with example input rows containing nulls and the exact expected output for each.
metadata.explanation: Concrete example input/output pairs, especially for tricky edge cases like nulls, are the documented fix when prose descriptions produce inconsistent handling; simply emphasizing the same ambiguous wording doesn't add new information.

cards.prompt: A review turns up three issues: two are a race condition and a caching bug that both touch the same lock and clearly interact, and a third is an unrelated linting issue in a completely different file. How should these be communicated to Claude for fixing?
cards.answer: Describe the race condition and caching bug together in one message since they interact, and handle the linting issue separately.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: Describe the race condition and caching bug together in one message since they interact, and handle the linting issue separately.
List every one of the three issues in three completely separate messages, one at a time, regardless of whether they actually interact.
Combine all three unrelated issues into a single message solely in order to reduce the total number of messages sent.
Ignore the linting issue entirely going forward, since only genuinely interacting issues are ever worth raising with Claude.
metadata.explanation: The rule is to bundle interacting problems into one message, since fixing them in isolation risks conflicting changes, while unrelated issues can be handled independently; grouping everything together indiscriminately, or splitting interacting issues apart, both undercut that logic.

cards.prompt: A team is implementing a new leaderboard sort that must correctly handle ties, empty input, and complete within 200ms for 10,000 rows. What should be written before asking Claude to implement it?
cards.answer: A test suite covering expected behavior, edge cases like ties and empty input, and the 200ms performance requirement.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: A single example of correct sorted output with no edge cases, since performance can be checked manually afterward.
A test suite covering expected behavior, edge cases like ties and empty input, and the 200ms performance requirement.
A CLAUDE.md rule simply stating "the sort must be fast," with absolutely no accompanying test coverage whatsoever.
A skill with allowed-tools restricted to Read only, so that Claude is physically unable to modify the sort function at all.
metadata.explanation: Test-driven iteration calls for tests covering expected behavior, edge cases, and performance requirements up front, so failures can drive refinement; a vague CLAUDE.md rule or a Read-only skill gives Claude nothing concrete to iterate against.

cards.prompt: A team about to build its first-ever single-sign-on integration wants to surface potential failure modes -- like token expiry races and session hijacking risks -- before any code is written. What approach fits best?
cards.answer: Use the interview pattern, letting Claude ask questions that surface considerations the team hasn't anticipated.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: Jump straight to test-driven iteration, since writing tests first always reveals security failure modes.
Use direct execution with no upfront discussion since SSO integrations are simple, well-scoped changes.
Use the interview pattern, letting Claude ask questions that surface considerations the team hasn't anticipated.
Rely solely on concrete input/output examples of successful logins, without discussing any potential failure modes upfront.
metadata.explanation: The interview pattern is designed for exactly this -- surfacing unanticipated considerations in an unfamiliar domain before implementation -- whereas test suites and I/O examples are better suited to clarifying already-understood transformations, not eliciting unknown risks.

cards.prompt: Two completely unrelated issues are found: a typo in an error message string in module A, and a missing database index in a migration in module B. Neither affects the other. What's the recommended way to have Claude address them?
cards.answer: Fix them sequentially in separate iterations, since they are independent problems that don't interact.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: Combine both into a single detailed message because any two issues found in the same review must be reported together.
Wait until a third issue appears so there are enough issues to justify batching them.
Address only the higher-severity one and silently drop the other since they don't interact.
Fix them sequentially in separate iterations, since they are independent problems that don't interact.
metadata.explanation: The guidance is to batch interacting issues together but handle independent problems sequentially, keeping each fix focused and easy to verify; batching unrelated issues just because they were found together adds unnecessary complexity, and dropping one silently ignores real work.

cards.prompt: An instruction to 'format dates consistently across the report' has produced three different date formats across separate Claude Code runs. What is the most effective fix?
cards.answer: Supply 2-3 concrete examples showing a specific input date and its exact required output format.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: Supply 2-3 concrete examples showing a specific input date and its exact required output format.
Add the phrase "please be consistent this time" to the next prompt.
Let the interview pattern replace the instruction entirely, since date formatting is an unfamiliar domain.
Split the single instruction into ten near-identical restatements of the same request.
metadata.explanation: Concrete examples remove the ambiguity that causes inconsistent interpretation of a prose rule; pleading for consistency or restating the same vague instruction repeatedly doesn't add the missing information Claude needs.

cards.prompt: Claude's first attempt at a new behavior-symbol-code parser fails four of the twelve tests in the suite the team wrote up front. What is the correct next step in a test-driven iteration workflow?
cards.answer: Share the specific failing test output with Claude so it can refine its implementation against those cases.

metadata.subdomain: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
choices: Discard the test suite and rewrite the parser requirements as a single paragraph of prose instead.
Share the specific failing test output with Claude so it can refine its implementation against those cases.
Accept the four failures as unimportant edge cases that don't really matter, since eight of the tests already pass.
Re-enter plan mode to redesign the entire codebase architecture in response to four failing unit tests.
metadata.explanation: The core of test-driven iteration is feeding actual test failures back to Claude to drive progressive improvement; discarding the tests, ignoring failures, or escalating to a full architectural re-plan over four unit-test failures all abandon that tight feedback loop.

cards.prompt: A nightly CI job that invokes Claude Code to generate release notes keeps hanging indefinitely instead of completing. What CLI flag is most likely missing?
cards.answer: The -p (or --print) flag, which runs Claude Code non-interactively instead of waiting for interactive input.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: The --output-format json flag, which is apparently required for any CI invocation to ever terminate at all.
The --json-schema flag, which reportedly forces Claude Code to skip all of its normal tool calls entirely.
The -p (or --print) flag, which runs Claude Code non-interactively instead of waiting for interactive input.
The context: fork frontmatter option, which supposedly must be set directly inside the CI pipeline's YAML configuration file.
metadata.explanation: -p/--print is exactly what prevents Claude Code from waiting on interactive input in automated contexts; --output-format json controls output shape, not whether the process hangs, and context: fork is a skill-level setting, not a CI mechanism.

cards.prompt: A CI pipeline needs to parse Claude Code's review findings programmatically in order to post them as individual inline PR comments. What CLI flags enable this?
cards.answer: --output-format json combined with --json-schema, producing structured, machine-parseable output.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: -p alone, since running in non-interactive mode automatically returns perfectly valid JSON output every time.
--allowed-tools=Read, since restricting the available tools apparently forces fully structured output.
A .claude/rules/ file with a paths field that matches the specific files changed in the pull request.
--output-format json combined with --json-schema, producing structured, machine-parseable output.
metadata.explanation: --output-format json together with --json-schema is what enforces the structured, parseable output CI needs to build automated comments; -p only prevents interactive hangs, it doesn't change output formatting on its own.

cards.prompt: A CI-invoked Claude Code review keeps flagging stylistic nitpicks the team doesn't enforce, while missing violations of fixture conventions the team does care about. What's the most direct fix?
cards.answer: Document the team's actual testing standards, fixture conventions, and review criteria in CLAUDE.md.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: Document the team's actual testing standards, fixture conventions, and review criteria in CLAUDE.md.
Increase the --json-schema strictness setting so that Claude automatically infers the team's real priorities on its own.
Remove the -p flag entirely so that the CI job runs fully interactively and a human can step in to correct it live.
Add many more unit tests to the codebase so that Claude eventually stops needing any instructions at all.
metadata.explanation: CLAUDE.md is the documented mechanism for giving CI-invoked Claude Code project context like testing standards and review criteria; removing -p would break the automated, non-interactive nature of CI entirely.

cards.prompt: A CI setup has the same Claude Code session that generated a PR's code also review that PR's diff before merge, in order to save an extra invocation. What's the concern with this approach?
cards.answer: Self-review is less effective than an independent instance, since it shares the same context and assumptions that produced the code.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: Nothing at all -- session context isolation only ever matters for skills, never for CI-based code review steps.
Self-review is less effective than an independent instance, since it shares the same context and assumptions that produced the code.
The exact same session technically cannot be invoked a second time, so the review step would simply fail outright.
Reviewing within the exact same session reportedly and consistently doubles the token cost compared to using two entirely separate independent sessions.
metadata.explanation: The guide specifically calls out that a session reviewing its own generated code is less effective than an independent review instance because it shares the same blind spots and assumptions; the same session absolutely can be invoked again, that's not the actual issue.

cards.prompt: A GitHub Actions workflow calling Claude Code to summarize a PR diff freezes the runner and only stops after it times out. What is the most likely missing piece in the invocation?
cards.answer: The -p (--print) flag, running in non-interactive mode so the process never waits for input that will never arrive.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: A brand-new .claude/commands/ entry specifically defining the PR summarization behavior end to end.
A directory-level CLAUDE.md file placed directly inside the .github/workflows/ directory itself.
The -p (--print) flag, running in non-interactive mode so the process never waits for input that will never arrive.
The interview pattern, so that Claude proactively asks the CI runner clarifying questions before proceeding at all.
metadata.explanation: Hanging CI invocations waiting on input are the classic symptom of missing -p/--print; a directory CLAUDE.md or the interview pattern don't address non-interactive execution at all.

cards.prompt: An automated Claude Code review re-run after new commits keeps re-posting the same three issues that were already flagged in the previous run's PR comments. What should the pipeline do differently?
cards.answer: Include the prior review findings in context and instruct Claude to report only new or still-unaddressed issues.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: Disable --output-format json so duplicate detection happens automatically.
Re-run the review in the exact same session that generated the original code, since it will remember its own prior comments.
Stop running reviews on any PR that has already received one comment, regardless of new commits.
Include the prior review findings in context and instruct Claude to report only new or still-unaddressed issues.
metadata.explanation: Explicitly feeding prior findings into context and instructing Claude to report only new/unresolved issues is the documented fix for duplicate comments; disabling JSON output has nothing to do with deduplication, and skipping review entirely after new commits would miss genuinely new issues.

cards.prompt: A CI-triggered test-generation step keeps suggesting new tests that duplicate scenarios already covered in the module's existing test file. What should be changed in the invocation?
cards.answer: Provide the existing test files in context so Claude avoids duplicating scenarios the suite covers.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: Provide the existing test files in context so Claude avoids duplicating scenarios the suite covers.
Turn off the --json-schema flag entirely so Claude has much more freedom to write duplicate tests intentionally.
Run the test generation step inside the very same session that will later go on to review the resulting PR.
Add a context: fork option to the CI YAML configuration file specifically in order to prevent duplication.
metadata.explanation: Giving Claude visibility into the existing test file is the documented way to prevent redundant test generation; context: fork is a skill frontmatter option, not a CI configuration mechanism, so it doesn't apply here.

cards.prompt: Automated test generation keeps producing low-value tests, like asserting a simple getter returns the value that was just set, while ignoring the project's existing fixture factories. What should be improved?
cards.answer: Document testing standards, valuable-test criteria, and available fixtures in CLAUDE.md so generation quality improves.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: Increase the --json-schema strictness setting so that low-value tests get automatically rejected at parse time.
Document testing standards, valuable-test criteria, and available fixtures in CLAUDE.md so generation quality improves.
Switch the test-generation step from -p mode to interactive mode so a human types out every test manually.
Remove the existing fixture factories entirely so that Claude is always forced to write fully self-contained tests instead of reusing them.
metadata.explanation: CLAUDE.md documenting testing standards, valuable-test criteria, and fixtures is exactly how the guide addresses low-value, fixture-ignorant test generation; --json-schema only governs output structure, not test quality, and removing fixtures would make things worse.

cards.prompt: Which statement accurately describes running Claude Code with the -p (--print) flag in a CI pipeline?
cards.answer: It runs Claude Code in non-interactive mode, suited to automated pipelines that can't respond to prompts.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: It forcibly puts Claude Code into plan mode on every single invocation, regardless of how complex the task actually is.
It is only compatible with Azure Functions deployments, not AWS Lambda or local CI runners.
It runs Claude Code in non-interactive mode, suited to automated pipelines that can't respond to prompts.
It automatically restricts Claude Code to strictly read-only tools for the entire duration of the run.
metadata.explanation: -p/--print is specifically about non-interactive execution suited to automation; it has no relationship to plan mode, cloud provider, or tool permission restrictions, which are separate, independently-configured concerns.

cards.prompt: A team is building an automated PR-review bot using Claude Code and wants: (1) it to run without hanging on input, (2) structured output it can parse into inline comments, (3) awareness of the team's review criteria, and (4) to avoid the accuracy loss of a session reviewing its own generated code. Which combination of practices satisfies all four goals?
cards.answer: Run with -p and --output-format json/--json-schema, document criteria in CLAUDE.md, and review from a separate session than the generator.

metadata.subdomain: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
choices: Run interactively without -p, skip CLAUDE.md entirely, and always reuse the exact same code-generation session for review purposes to save time.
Use --json-schema alone with no -p flag at all, and rely on the interview pattern instead of CLAUDE.md for review criteria.
Run with -p only, embed the review criteria in commit messages instead of CLAUDE.md, and reuse the code-generation session to reduce overall cost.
Run with -p and --output-format json/--json-schema, document criteria in CLAUDE.md, and review from a separate session than the generator.
metadata.explanation: All four documented CI practices -- -p for non-interactive execution, --output-format json/--json-schema for structured output, CLAUDE.md for project context, and an independent review session to avoid self-review bias -- are needed together; each distractor drops or inverts one of these.

cards.prompt: You're building a code-review bot and tell it to 'flag comments only when the claimed behavior contradicts the actual code behavior' instead of 'check that comments are accurate.' Why is this more likely to reduce false positives?
cards.answer: It gives the model a categorical, checkable condition instead of a vague standard, so stylistic nitpicks are excluded by definition.

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: It gives the model a categorical, checkable condition instead of a vague standard, so stylistic nitpicks are excluded by definition.
It instructs the model to be more conservative when uncertain, the same vague 'be conservative' framing the guide says fails to change what gets flagged.
It reduces the number of tokens the model needs to process per comment, shrinking the prompt so review runs faster and cheaper.
It forces the model to use extended thinking before flagging any comment, adding a reasoning step before every judgment.
metadata.explanation: Explicit, checkable criteria narrow what counts as a violation, unlike 'be conservative,' which doesn't define what to actually check.

cards.prompt: A PR-automation team adds 'be conservative and only report high-confidence findings' to their review prompt after seeing too many false positives. After a week, false positive rates haven't changed. What is the most likely explanation from the study guide's perspective?
cards.answer: Confidence-based instructions like 'be conservative' don't supply categorical criteria, so they don't reliably change what gets flagged.

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: Confidence-based instructions like 'be conservative' don't supply categorical criteria, so they don't reliably change what gets flagged.
The model ignored the instruction because it was placed after the examples in the prompt, rather than at the very start of the system prompt.
Confidence-based filtering only works when combined with a lower temperature setting, which the team apparently never adjusted.
The team needed to add more few-shot examples of low-confidence findings to teach the model what 'conservative' means.
metadata.explanation: The guide states general instructions like 'be conservative' fail to improve precision compared to specific categorical criteria; the fix is defining exact criteria, not asking for more caution.

cards.prompt: A document-QA pipeline reports both 'formatting inconsistencies' (40% false positive rate) and 'missing required signatures' (2% false positive rate). Reviewers start ignoring both categories entirely. What does this illustrate?
cards.answer: High false-positive categories can erode trust in accurate categories, causing reviewers to discount reliable findings too.

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: High false-positive categories can erode trust in accurate categories, causing reviewers to discount reliable findings too.
The two categories should be merged into a single severity level to simplify triage, since reviewers already treat them the same way.
Reviewers should apply the same confidence threshold to every category regardless of its historical accuracy.
The pipeline needs additional retries on the missing-signature category specifically, since low accuracy always stems from insufficient retries.
metadata.explanation: The guide notes false positive rates in one category can undermine confidence even in genuinely accurate categories -- the fix is addressing the noisy category, not lumping them together.

cards.prompt: You're designing review criteria for an automated linter-plus-LLM code review tool. Which approach best matches the guide's recommendation for what to report versus skip?
cards.answer: Define which issue types to report (bugs, security) versus skip (style, idioms) explicitly, rather than filtering by self-reported confidence.

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: Define which issue types to report (bugs, security) versus skip (style, idioms) explicitly, rather than filtering by self-reported confidence.
Report every issue the model finds and let a human reviewer filter by confidence score afterward, since manual triage scales fine for any volume.
Ask the model to only report issues it is more than 90% confident about, regardless of issue category or severity.
Skip all style-related feedback entirely and only ever report compiler-level errors, since style issues are never worth flagging.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends specific criteria defining reportable vs skippable categories rather than confidence-based filtering, which doesn't reliably separate real issues from noise.

cards.prompt: Your automated null-safety checker generates so many false positives that engineers have started ignoring its Slack alerts entirely, including for a separate, highly accurate memory-leak category it also reports. What's the recommended immediate action?
cards.answer: Temporarily disable the null-safety category while refining its prompt, to preserve trust in the memory-leak category.

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: Temporarily disable the null-safety category while refining its prompt, to preserve trust in the memory-leak category.
Lower the temperature setting across all categories to reduce randomness, since determinism alone fixes false-positive rates.
Merge null-safety and memory-leak findings into one alert so reviewers see them together and can triage both at once.
Add a 'be more conservative' instruction to the null-safety prompt and keep it running, trusting that phrasing alone will cut false positives.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends temporarily disabling high false-positive categories to restore trust while the prompt is improved, rather than continuing to erode confidence in unrelated categories.

cards.prompt: A security-review tool needs to classify findings as Critical, Major, or Minor. Reviewers currently disagree on classification for the same finding. What should the prompt add to fix this?
cards.answer: Explicit severity criteria for each level accompanied by concrete code examples illustrating each severity.

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: Explicit severity criteria for each level accompanied by concrete code examples illustrating each severity.
A single overall confidence score attached to each finding instead of a distinct severity label for each level.
An instruction telling the model to 'use its best judgment' on severity, trusting consistency to emerge naturally over time.
A rule that all findings default to Minor unless a human manually escalates them to a higher severity level.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends defining explicit severity criteria with concrete examples per level to achieve consistent classification, rather than relying on generic judgment instructions.

cards.prompt: An invoice-extraction prompt currently says 'check that dates are correct.' Extraction quality is inconsistent. Which replacement instruction best follows the guide's principle on explicit criteria?
cards.answer: 'Flag an invoice date if it falls before the stated billing period start or after the invoice's due date.'

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: 'Flag an invoice date if it falls before the stated billing period start or after the invoice's due date.'
'Be careful and only flag dates you are highly confident are wrong, using your best judgment on each one.'
'Check dates conservatively and skip ones you're unsure about, relying on your own sense of caution.'
'Report all dates found in the document without evaluation, letting a human decide later which are wrong.'
metadata.explanation: A categorical, checkable rule (before start / after due date) is explicit criteria; 'be careful'/'conservatively' phrasing repeats the vague-instruction anti-pattern the guide warns against.

cards.prompt: A vulnerability-scanning assistant is told: 'Only report a finding if you are very confident it's a real vulnerability.' Reports remain inconsistent in precision. What is the underlying issue?
cards.answer: Confidence-based filtering doesn't substitute for categorical criteria describing what counts as a reportable vulnerability.

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: Confidence-based filtering doesn't substitute for categorical criteria describing what counts as a reportable vulnerability.
The model needs a higher tool_choice setting to enforce stricter confidence scoring across every category of finding it reports.
The prompt is missing a retry-with-error-feedback loop that would catch these inconsistent precision issues.
Confidence filtering only works for extraction tasks, not for review or classification tasks like this one.
metadata.explanation: As with 'be conservative,' confidence-based instructions don't define what to check, so they fail to improve precision the way explicit categorical criteria do.

cards.prompt: A static-analysis-plus-LLM tool reports 'unused variable' (very low false-positive rate) and 'possible race condition' (high false-positive rate) in the same output feed. Engineers begin dismissing unused-variable findings too, even though those are accurate. What principle explains this?
cards.answer: High false-positive rates in one category can undermine trust in other, more accurate categories reported alongside it.

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: High false-positive rates in one category can undermine trust in other, more accurate categories reported alongside it.
Unused-variable findings are inherently less important than race-condition findings, so dismissal is appropriate.
The tool should increase its confidence threshold uniformly across all categories to fix this, regardless of each category's actual accuracy.
This is expected behavior and requires no prompt or reporting changes, since some dismissal is always unavoidable.
metadata.explanation: The guide explicitly calls out that noisy categories can drag down trust in accurate ones -- the fix is isolating/improving the noisy category, not accepting blanket dismissal.

cards.prompt: A legal-contract review assistant labels risk as High/Medium/Low, but two reviewers looking at the same clause often disagree on the label the model chose. What is the most effective prompt fix per the guide?
cards.answer: Provide explicit criteria for each risk level paired with concrete example clauses illustrating each one.

metadata.subdomain: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
choices: Provide explicit criteria for each risk level paired with concrete example clauses illustrating each one.
Ask the model to always default to the higher risk label whenever it is uncertain about a clause's true risk.
Instruct the model to be conservative about assigning High risk, trusting cautious phrasing to produce consistent labels.
Remove the risk-level field entirely and just report clause text, leaving classification to manual reviewer judgment.
metadata.explanation: Concrete, example-anchored criteria per severity level produce consistent classification; vague 'be conservative' instructions are the anti-pattern the guide warns against.

cards.prompt: A support-ticket triage bot has detailed written instructions but still produces inconsistently formatted routing decisions. What technique does the guide identify as most effective for fixing this?
cards.answer: Adding 2-4 few-shot examples showing the desired input-to-output mapping.

metadata.subdomain: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
choices: Adding 2-4 few-shot examples showing the desired input-to-output mapping.
Rewriting the instructions in even more detail with additional edge cases.
Lowering the model's temperature to reduce output variance.
Splitting the task across two sequential API calls.
metadata.explanation: The guide states few-shot examples are the most effective technique for consistent, actionable output when detailed instructions alone produce inconsistent results.

cards.prompt: A coding agent must decide whether an ambiguous user request should call the 'search_docs' tool or the 'search_code' tool. Both are plausible for many requests. How should the prompt help the model handle this?
cards.answer: Include few-shot examples of ambiguous requests with reasoning for why one tool was chosen over the other.

metadata.subdomain: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
choices: Include few-shot examples of ambiguous requests with reasoning for why one tool was chosen over the other.
Add a rule that always prefers 'search_code' when in doubt, regardless of what the user's request actually describes.
Instruct the model to ask the user to choose a tool every time any ambiguity arises, no matter how minor.
Remove one of the two tools so no ambiguity can occur, even though both are genuinely useful for different requests.
metadata.explanation: Few-shot examples that show reasoning for a chosen action over plausible alternatives are the guide's recommended way to teach ambiguous-case handling, like tool selection.

cards.prompt: A test-coverage bot must judge whether a new PR's tests adequately cover a changed function versus only covering a narrow branch. Plain written rules produce inconsistent judgments across similar PRs. What addition would most help per the guide?
cards.answer: Few-shot examples showing branch-level coverage gaps and how they should be judged.

metadata.subdomain: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
choices: Few-shot examples showing branch-level coverage gaps and how they should be judged.
A single line reminding the model to 'consider all branches' before finalizing its judgment.
Increasing the max_tokens parameter for the review response so more detail can be written.
Switching from tool use to plain text output so judgments read more naturally.
metadata.explanation: The guide specifically cites branch-level test coverage gaps as an ambiguous case few-shot examples help the model handle consistently.

cards.prompt: After adding 5 few-shot examples to a document-summarization prompt, the model correctly handles a document structure never seen in any example. What does this demonstrate about few-shot prompting?
cards.answer: Few-shot examples let the model generalize judgment to novel patterns, not just replicate the cases shown.

metadata.subdomain: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
choices: Few-shot examples let the model generalize judgment to novel patterns, not just replicate the cases shown.
The examples were memorized verbatim and coincidentally happened to match this new, unseen document structure.
This only happens when extended thinking is also enabled alongside the few-shot examples in the prompt.
Few-shot examples work only for classification tasks, not summarization tasks like this document pipeline.
metadata.explanation: A key benefit the guide highlights is that few-shot examples enable generalization beyond the specific patterns demonstrated, rather than only handling pre-specified cases.

cards.prompt: An extraction pipeline pulling measurements from informal field-inspection notes ('about 3 ft', 'roughly waist-high') is hallucinating precise numeric values not stated in the source. What technique does the guide recommend to reduce this?
cards.answer: Few-shot examples showing how to handle informal or approximate measurements during extraction.

metadata.subdomain: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
choices: Few-shot examples showing how to handle informal or approximate measurements during extraction.
Increasing the temperature so the model is less deterministic and produces more varied numeric estimates.
Removing the measurement field from the schema entirely, since informal values can't be usefully captured.
Requiring the model to always round measurements to the nearest whole number regardless of source phrasing.
metadata.explanation: The guide identifies few-shot examples as effective for reducing hallucination in extraction tasks, specifically calling out handling of informal measurements as an example use case.

cards.prompt: You're building a security-finding classifier and want to add few-shot examples. How many targeted examples does the guide suggest are typically enough for an ambiguous scenario?

cards.prompt: A code-review bot's output is inconsistent in structure -- sometimes it includes a suggested fix, sometimes not, and severity labels vary in wording. What should the few-shot examples specifically demonstrate to fix this?
cards.answer: The exact desired output format: location, issue description, severity, and suggested fix, for each example.

choices: The exact desired output format: location, issue description, severity, and suggested fix, for each example.

cards.prompt: A code-review bot repeatedly flags a common local idiom (e.g., a project's custom logging wrapper) as a 'possible bug' even though it's an accepted pattern in this codebase. What few-shot strategy addresses this while still catching genuine bugs?
cards.answer: Include examples contrasting this pattern with genuinely similar issues, so the model generalizes rather than memorizing one case.

metadata.subdomain: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
choices: Include examples contrasting this pattern with genuinely similar issues, so the model generalizes rather than memorizing one case.
Add a rule that disables bug-flagging for any code using a wrapper function, regardless of what that code actually does.
Instruct the model to only flag issues in files it has not seen before, ignoring previously reviewed files entirely.
Remove the bug-detection category entirely from the review prompt, since one false positive means the category isn't worth keeping.
metadata.explanation: Few-shot examples that contrast acceptable patterns against genuine issues reduce false positives while still letting the model generalize to catch real problems -- a blanket suppression rule wouldn't generalize.

cards.prompt: A research-paper extraction pipeline handles papers that cite sources inline versus papers with a separate bibliography, and some embed methodology details in results sections rather than a dedicated methods section. Extraction accuracy varies widely by document structure. What should be added to the prompt?

choices: Few-shot examples demonstrating correct extraction across both citation styles and methodology placements.

cards.prompt: An invoice-extraction schema has a required 'tax_id' field, but many source invoices from small vendors simply omit it in different ways (blank, 'N/A', omitted line). The pipeline keeps returning empty or null values inconsistently, and sometimes fabricates plausible-looking IDs. What fix does the guide recommend beyond schema changes?

metadata.subdomain: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
choices: Add few-shot examples covering the varied vendor formats, including how to represent a genuinely missing tax_id.
Increase the retry count so the model eventually finds a tax_id, even in invoices that never listed one.
Make the tax_id field required and reject any extraction lacking it, forcing every vendor to supply one.
Ask the model to guess a plausible tax_id based on the vendor's country and its typical numbering conventions used there.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends few-shot examples covering varied formats to address empty/null extraction of required fields -- pairing this with optional/nullable schema design, not forcing guesses or stricter requirements.

cards.prompt: An engineering team is choosing between free-text JSON parsing (asking the model to output JSON in its response text) and tool use with a JSON schema for a contract-metadata extraction pipeline. Which approach does the guide identify as most reliable for guaranteed schema compliance?
cards.answer: Tool use with a JSON schema, since it eliminates JSON syntax errors entirely.

metadata.subdomain: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
choices: Tool use with a JSON schema, since it eliminates JSON syntax errors entirely.
Free-text JSON, since it allows more natural language explanation alongside the data.
Free-text JSON with a regex validator applied after generation.
Tool use without a schema, relying on the tool name alone to imply structure.
metadata.explanation: The guide identifies tool_use with JSON schemas as the most reliable way to guarantee schema-compliant output, eliminating JSON syntax errors that free-text parsing is prone to.

cards.prompt: A developer is configuring tool_choice for an extraction API call and needs the model to definitely invoke a tool, but is fine letting the model pick which of several available tools fits best. Which setting matches this requirement?
cards.answer: tool_choice: "any" -- the model must call a tool but can choose which one.

metadata.subdomain: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
choices: tool_choice: "any" -- the model must call a tool but can choose which one.
tool_choice: "auto" -- the model may return text instead of calling a tool.
A forced tool_choice naming one specific tool.
Omitting tool_choice entirely so the model decides whether to use tools at all.
metadata.explanation: 'any' forces a tool call but leaves tool selection to the model, unlike 'auto' (may skip tools) or a forced named tool (locks in which one).

cards.prompt: An invoicing pipeline uses tool_use with a strict JSON schema for line-item extraction. The output is always valid JSON matching the schema, but a reviewer notices the extracted line items don't sum to the extracted total. What does this indicate?
cards.answer: Strict schemas eliminate JSON syntax errors but don't prevent semantic errors like values that don't add up.

metadata.subdomain: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
choices: Strict schemas eliminate JSON syntax errors but don't prevent semantic errors like values that don't add up.
The schema definition itself must be malformed, since a genuinely valid schema always guarantees correct totals.
Tool use should be abandoned in favor of free-text extraction for numeric fields, since schemas clearly aren't working.
This can only be fixed by lowering the model's temperature to 0 so its outputs become fully deterministic.
metadata.explanation: The guide explicitly notes tool use guarantees syntactic validity, not semantic correctness -- catching sum mismatches requires separate validation logic.

cards.prompt: A ticket-categorization schema has a 'category' enum field with a fixed list of categories. New, previously unseen ticket types keep getting forced into the closest existing category, losing information. What schema design principle addresses this?
cards.answer: Add an 'other' enum value paired with a free-text detail field so extensible categories aren't forced into a bad fit.

metadata.subdomain: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
choices: Add an 'other' enum value paired with a free-text detail field so extensible categories aren't forced into a bad fit.
Make the category field a required string with no enum constraint at all, discarding structure entirely and losing validation.
Remove the category field and classify tickets manually going forward, abandoning automation for this step.
Expand the enum to include every conceivable category up front, so nothing new could ever appear.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends enum fields with an 'other' + detail string pattern specifically to handle extensible categories that don't fit predefined values.

cards.prompt: You're building a resume-parsing feature and want the LLM to return structured candidate data (name, skills, experience) reliably. How should this be implemented using tool use?
cards.answer: Define an extraction tool with a JSON schema for the candidate data, and read the result from the tool_use block.

metadata.subdomain: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
choices: Define an extraction tool with a JSON schema for the candidate data, and read the result from the tool_use block.
Ask the model to output the resume data as a markdown table and parse it afterward with a hand-written regex.
Define the tool with no input schema at all, and parse whatever loosely-structured text the model returns inside it.
Use tool_choice: "auto" so the model can skip the tool entirely whenever the resume already looks well-formatted.
metadata.explanation: Defining an extraction tool with a JSON schema as its parameters, then reading the resulting tool_use block, is the standard structured-extraction pattern for guaranteed schema compliance.

cards.prompt: A document pipeline handles incoming files that could be invoices, purchase orders, or shipping manifests, and the type isn't known until the model reads the content. Three separate extraction tools exist, one per document type. Which tool_choice setting should be used?
cards.answer: tool_choice: "any," so the model must call one of the extraction tools, selecting whichever matches the document.

metadata.subdomain: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
choices: tool_choice: "any," so the model must call one of the extraction tools, selecting whichever matches the document.
tool_choice: "auto," so the model can respond with plain text instead if it's unsure of the document type.
A forced tool_choice naming the invoice-extraction tool as the default, even when the document isn't actually an invoice.
No tool_choice parameter at all, relying entirely on the model's system prompt to describe the document types.
metadata.explanation: When multiple extraction schemas exist and the document type is unknown ahead of time, 'any' guarantees a structured call while letting the model choose the matching tool.

cards.prompt: A pipeline first extracts core metadata from a contract, then runs a separate enrichment step that depends on that metadata. The team wants to guarantee the extract_metadata tool always runs first, before any enrichment tool is considered. How should tool_choice be configured for that first call?
cards.answer: Force the specific tool with tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"}.

metadata.subdomain: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
choices: Force the specific tool with tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"}.
Use tool_choice: "any" so the model can pick between metadata extraction and enrichment freely.
Use tool_choice: "auto" and trust the model to sequence the steps correctly on its own.
Provide both tools in the same call with no tool_choice constraint and let the model decide order.
metadata.explanation: Forcing a specific named tool guarantees that exact tool runs, which is the right way to ensure a required extraction step precedes downstream steps, unlike 'any' or 'auto' which leave the choice open.

cards.prompt: An extraction schema for building-inspection reports includes a 'permit_number' field. Many older inspection reports genuinely don't reference a permit number at all. Extractions are currently fabricating plausible-looking permit numbers to satisfy a required field. What's the fix?
cards.answer: Make 'permit_number' optional/nullable so the model can indicate absence instead of inventing a value.

metadata.subdomain: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
choices: Make 'permit_number' optional/nullable so the model can indicate absence instead of inventing a value.
Keep the field required but add a retry loop that runs until a valid-looking number is eventually produced.
Make the field required and instruct the model to leave it blank whenever it is unsure of the true value.
Remove the field from the schema entirely and track permit numbers in a separate manual spreadsheet instead.
metadata.explanation: Designing fields as optional/nullable when the source may not contain the information prevents the fabrication that required fields tend to induce.

cards.prompt: A dispute-classification tool has an enum field for 'dispute_reason' with several known categories, but some customer complaints are genuinely ambiguous and don't clearly match any single category. What schema addition handles this cleanly?
cards.answer: Add 'unclear' for genuinely ambiguous cases, plus 'other' with a detail field for cases outside existing categories.

metadata.subdomain: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
choices: Add 'unclear' for genuinely ambiguous cases, plus 'other' with a detail field for cases outside existing categories.
Require the model to always pick the closest matching category, even when the true reason is genuinely ambiguous or unclear.
Remove the enum constraint entirely and let 'dispute_reason' be free text in every single case.
Add a numeric confidence score field instead of handling ambiguity directly within the enum structure itself.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends adding 'unclear' for genuinely ambiguous cases and 'other' + detail for extensible categorization, both of which avoid forcing a bad categorical fit.

cards.prompt: A pipeline extracts dates and phone numbers from scanned forms where source formatting is wildly inconsistent (MM/DD/YYYY, DD-MM-YY, spelled-out dates, phone numbers with or without country codes). The JSON schema strictly types these as strings. What should be added alongside the schema to handle this?

choices: Format normalization rules in the prompt describing how to convert inconsistent source formats to one output format.

Nothing -- strict schemas alone are sufficient to normalize formatting differences without any extra prompt guidance.
A retry loop that keeps resubmitting the same request until the source format happens to match the desired one.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends including format normalization rules in the prompt alongside the strict schema, since the schema enforces structure/type but not the specific formatting conventions of the values.

cards.prompt: An extraction pipeline's output fails schema validation because a date field contains free text instead of an ISO date. What retry strategy does the guide recommend?

Retry with the exact same prompt left unchanged, hoping the model succeeds on a second random sample.
Retry with a completely different, simplified schema that removes the field that failed validation.
Skip retrying entirely and immediately escalate every failed extraction straight to manual review.
metadata.explanation: Retry-with-error-feedback means appending the specific validation error to guide correction, rather than blind retries or immediately abandoning automation.

cards.prompt: A financial-report extraction pipeline is retrying a document because the schema validator flagged a missing 'auditor_name' field. Three retries in a row still return null. Investigation shows the source PDF genuinely never mentions an auditor. What should the team conclude?

The schema's 'auditor_name' field should be switched from optional to required, forcing the model to supply a value.
The temperature should be increased so the model considers less-likely completions and eventually names an auditor.
metadata.explanation: The guide is explicit that retries can't recover information that isn't in the source at all -- this is a case for leaving the field null, not further retries.

cards.prompt: A code-review bot's security-finding schema doesn't currently record what code construct triggered each finding, making it hard to tell why developers keep dismissing findings in one category. What field addition would enable that analysis?
cards.answer: A 'detected_pattern' field capturing which specific code construct triggered the finding.

metadata.subdomain: 4.4 Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality
choices: A 'detected_pattern' field capturing which specific code construct triggered the finding.

A boolean 'is_valid' field the model sets itself, based purely on its own self-reported confidence.

metadata.explanation: The guide recommends a detected_pattern field specifically so dismissal patterns can be systematically analyzed by pattern, not general-purpose notes or self-assessed validity.

cards.prompt: A team debating validation strategy notes that their tool_use-based extraction never fails on malformed JSON but sometimes returns a shipping weight in the 'volume' field. How should this failure be categorized?
cards.answer: It's a semantic validation error -- the field placement is wrong even though the JSON syntax itself is valid.

metadata.subdomain: 4.4 Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality
choices: It's a semantic validation error -- the field placement is wrong even though the JSON syntax itself is valid.
It's a schema syntax error that tool use failed to prevent, meaning tool use doesn't actually work as advertised.
It's evidence that tool use should be replaced entirely with free-text JSON parsing going forward.
It's an unavoidable error that no validation layer, human or automated, can ever hope to catch.
metadata.explanation: The guide distinguishes semantic errors (like wrong field placement) -- which tool use does not prevent -- from schema syntax errors, which tool use does eliminate.

cards.prompt: An extraction pipeline's structured output fails a semantic validation check (line items don't sum to the stated total). How should the follow-up correction request be constructed?
cards.answer: Include the original document, the failed extraction, and the specific validation error, so the model can self-correct.

metadata.subdomain: 4.4 Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality
choices: Include the original document, the failed extraction, and the specific validation error, so the model can self-correct.
Send only the validation error message with no document or prior extraction attached, to keep the retry prompt as short as possible.
Send only the original document again with no reference at all to the prior failed extraction attempt.
Regenerate from scratch with a higher temperature and no reference whatsoever to the previous failure.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends follow-up requests containing the original document, the failed extraction, and the specific errors, giving the model the context needed to self-correct.

cards.prompt: Two extraction failures occur in the same week: (1) a total field was formatted as '$1,200' instead of a number, and (2) a required 'contract_end_date' simply isn't mentioned anywhere in a 40-page source contract. Which failure is a good candidate for an automatic retry?
cards.answer: The '$1,200' formatting failure, since it's a structural/format mismatch the model can likely fix on retry.

metadata.subdomain: 4.4 Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality
choices: The '$1,200' formatting failure, since it's a structural/format mismatch the model can likely fix on retry.
The missing contract_end_date failure, since retries reliably surface information the model initially missed.
Both failures equally, since retry effectiveness doesn't depend on the type of error.
Neither failure, since retries should never be used for extraction pipelines.
metadata.explanation: The guide draws a clear line: format/structural mismatches are good retry candidates, but retries can't manufacture information the source document never contained.

cards.prompt: Developers keep dismissing 'potential null-pointer' findings from a review bot, but the team has no way to see if the dismissals cluster around a specific coding pattern. What structured-output change would let them analyze this?
cards.answer: Add a detected_pattern field to each finding recording the specific construct that triggered it.

metadata.subdomain: 4.4 Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality
choices: Add a detected_pattern field to each finding recording the specific construct that triggered it.
Add a free-text summary field describing the whole file instead of the specific finding that triggered it.
Remove the null-pointer category from the tool entirely, since a few false positives means it isn't worth keeping.
Ask the model to rate its own finding as 'high' or 'low' priority with no further supporting detail at all.
metadata.explanation: A detected_pattern field lets the team correlate dismissals with specific triggering constructs, directly enabling the systematic analysis the guide describes.

cards.prompt: An invoice-extraction schema wants to catch cases where the invoice's stated total doesn't match what the line items actually add up to. What schema design implements this self-correction check?
cards.answer: Extract both a 'calculated_total' (summed from line items) and a 'stated_total' (as printed) to flag mismatches automatically.

metadata.subdomain: 4.4 Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality
choices: Extract both a 'calculated_total' (summed from line items) and a 'stated_total' (as printed) to flag mismatches automatically.
Extract only the 'stated_total' field and trust it as ground truth without ever cross-checking it against the line items at all.
Extract only the sum of the line items and discard the printed total on the invoice entirely.
Add a free-text 'comments' field where the model can mention if something about the totals seemed off.
metadata.explanation: The guide specifically recommends extracting both calculated_total and stated_total to flag discrepancies, rather than trusting one value or relying on unstructured commentary.

cards.prompt: A contract-extraction pipeline sometimes encounters contracts where the cover page lists one effective date and a clause in the body lists a different one. What structured field should be added to flag this for review?
cards.answer: A 'conflict_detected' boolean field indicating the source document contains inconsistent data on this point.

metadata.subdomain: 4.4 Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality
choices: A 'conflict_detected' boolean field indicating the source document contains inconsistent data on this point.
A 'confidence' float field describing how sure the model is about the extracted date overall, without noting the conflict.
A rule to always prefer whichever date appears first in the document, with no flag raised at all.
A retry loop that keeps extracting the contract over and over until both dates happen to match.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends conflict_detected booleans specifically for surfacing inconsistent source data, rather than silently picking one value or retrying against unresolvable ambiguity.

cards.prompt: A PO-extraction tool call returns an object where 'quantity' is a string ('twelve') instead of the required integer type per the schema. What's the appropriate next step per the guide's retry design?
cards.answer: Retry the call, appending the specific type-validation error so the model reformats 'quantity' as an integer.

metadata.subdomain: 4.4 Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality
choices: Retry the call, appending the specific type-validation error so the model reformats 'quantity' as an integer.
Accept the string value as-is and convert it manually downstream every single time this occurs.
Discard the entire extraction and mark the whole document as permanently unprocessable.
Switch the 'quantity' field to a free-text string type entirely, just to avoid the validation error altogether.
metadata.explanation: This is a format/structural error, a good candidate for retry-with-error-feedback, where the specific validation error is appended to guide correction -- not something to abandon or patch around by loosening the schema.

cards.prompt: A team is evaluating the Message Batches API for a nightly document-processing job and wants to understand its trade-offs. Which statement correctly describes it?
cards.answer: It offers roughly 50% cost savings with processing completed within 24 hours, but no guaranteed latency SLA.

choices: It offers roughly 50% cost savings with processing completed within 24 hours, but no guaranteed latency SLA.
It offers 50% cost savings with a guaranteed, contractually binding 1-hour turnaround SLA regardless of load.
It costs the same as the synchronous API but supports much larger context windows for each request.
It guarantees results within 24 hours at the full price of the synchronous API, with no discount.
metadata.explanation: The Message Batches API trades a soft, non-guaranteed latency window (up to 24 hours) for roughly 50% cost savings -- it doesn't promise a specific SLA.

cards.prompt: A team is deciding which of two workloads should use the Message Batches API: (1) a pre-merge PR check that blocks developers from merging until it completes, or (2) a weekly compliance audit report. Which is appropriate for batch processing?
cards.answer: The weekly compliance audit report, since it's latency-tolerant and non-blocking.

choices: The weekly compliance audit report, since it's latency-tolerant and non-blocking.
The pre-merge PR check, since batch processing is cheaper and merges aren't time-sensitive.
Both workloads equally, since the Batches API works identically to the synchronous API for latency-sensitive tasks.
Neither -- batch processing should only be used for real-time chat applications.
metadata.explanation: The guide is explicit that batch processing suits non-blocking, latency-tolerant workloads like weekly audits, while blocking workflows like pre-merge checks need synchronous calls with predictable latency.

cards.prompt: A team wants to run an agentic workflow inside a single Message Batches API request, where the model calls a tool, receives the tool's result, and then continues reasoning within that same request. Is this supported?
cards.answer: No -- the batch API does not support multi-turn tool calling within a single request.

choices: No -- the batch API does not support multi-turn tool calling within a single request.
Yes, as long as tool_choice is explicitly set to "any" for every request in the batch.
Yes, but only for requests that stay under a 10,000-token combined limit per call.
Yes, this is actually the primary use case the Batches API was specifically designed for.
metadata.explanation: The guide states the batch API does not support executing tools mid-request and returning results within that same request -- multi-turn tool loops need the synchronous API.

cards.prompt: A batch job submits 5,000 document-extraction requests overnight. The next morning, engineers need to match each response back to the specific source document it came from. What mechanism enables this?
cards.answer: The custom_id field included in each request, which is echoed back with its corresponding response.

choices: The order in which responses are returned, which is always assumed to match submission order exactly.
The custom_id field included in each request, which is echoed back with its corresponding response.
A manual lookup against request timestamps recorded at the moment of submission.
The model's own restatement of the document's filename somewhere in the response text.
metadata.explanation: custom_id fields are designed specifically for correlating batch request/response pairs reliably, rather than relying on timestamps or response ordering.

cards.prompt: An engineering team is building a PR bot that must block merges until its review completes, and separately, a nightly job that regenerates test suites for the whole codebase. How should they match API approach to each workload?
cards.answer: Use the synchronous API for the blocking PR check and the batch API for the nightly test-generation job.

choices: Use the synchronous API for the blocking PR check and the batch API for the nightly test-generation job.
Use the batch API for both, since it's cheaper regardless of latency needs.
Use the synchronous API for both, since batch processing is only for image generation.
Use the batch API for the PR check and synchronous for the nightly job, to save on merge-blocking costs.
metadata.explanation: Latency-sensitive, blocking work needs the synchronous API's predictable turnaround, while latency-tolerant nightly batch work benefits from the Batches API's cost savings.

cards.prompt: A team must guarantee a 30-hour SLA for delivering extraction results, using a batch API with up to a 24-hour processing window. How should they schedule submissions to meet this SLA?
cards.answer: Submit batches every 4 hours or more often, so the worst case (24h processing plus wait for the next window) fits inside 30 hours.

choices: Submit batches every 4 hours or more often, so the worst case (24h processing plus wait for the next window) fits inside 30 hours.
Submit one batch per day, since the 24-hour processing window already comfortably fits inside the 30-hour SLA.
Submit as infrequently as possible to maximize cost savings, since the SLA supposedly has no bearing on submission frequency at all.
Submit every 30 hours, exactly matching the SLA window, so timing lines up perfectly with the deadline.
metadata.explanation: Because batch processing can take up to 24 hours, submission frequency must be tight enough (e.g., every ~4 hours) that the worst-case document doesn't exceed the 30-hour SLA once submission delay is added.

cards.prompt: A 10,000-document overnight batch job returns with 40 failed requests, several of which failed because the source document exceeded the context window. What's the recommended remediation?
cards.answer: Resubmit only the failed documents, identified by custom_id, chunking the oversized ones before resubmitting.

choices: Resubmit only the failed documents, identified by custom_id, chunking the oversized ones before resubmitting.
Resubmit the entire 10,000-document batch completely unmodified and simply hope for a different outcome this time.
Discard the failed documents permanently, since batch failures can supposedly never be retried once they occur.
Switch the entire remaining pipeline over to the synchronous API for all future runs going forward.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends identifying failures via custom_id and resubmitting only those documents with appropriate fixes, like chunking oversized ones -- not resubmitting everything or abandoning batch processing.

cards.prompt: Before running a 50,000-invoice batch extraction job, a team wants to minimize costly resubmissions. What should they do first?
cards.answer: Refine the extraction prompt on a small representative sample to maximize first-pass success before the full run.

choices: Refine the extraction prompt on a small representative sample to maximize first-pass success before the full run.
Submit the full 50,000-invoice batch immediately, to gather the largest possible dataset for later analysis.
