Over a multi-issue conversation, context fills up quickly. What should be done before the tool result enters the conversation history?
cards.answer: Trim the tool output to only the return-relevant fields (status, amount, date, item, tracking) before it accumulates in context.

metadata.subdomain: 5.1 Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
choices: Pass the full 40-field response into context every time, since removing fields might hide something relevant later.
Trim the tool output to only the return-relevant fields (status, amount, date, item, tracking) before it accumulates in context.
Summarize the entire 40-field tool response into a single paragraph after every call, regardless of which fields the case actually needs.
Cache the full response outside context and never reference any of its fields again, even the ones needed to process the return.
metadata.explanation: Trimming verbose tool outputs to only relevant fields keeps the case facts precise while avoiding disproportionate token cost; passing everything through unfiltered is the specific anti-pattern of 40+ fields when only 5 matter.

cards.prompt: To reduce token usage, an engineer configures a customer-facing agent to send only the customer's most recent message to the API on each turn, discarding earlier turns. Customers begin reporting that the agent contradicts things it said minutes earlier and asks for information already provided. What is the most likely explanation?
cards.answer: Omitting prior conversation history from each request breaks coherence, since full history must be passed for the model to stay consistent.

metadata.subdomain: 5.1 Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
choices: The model's reasoning ability has degraded due to cumulative fatigue over the course of the long session, unrelated to what's sent in each request.
The agent needs a larger, more capable model to compensate for the conversation history being dropped from each request.
Omitting prior conversation history from each request breaks coherence, since full history must be passed for the model to stay consistent.
The customer is providing genuinely inconsistent information across turns, rather than the agent losing track of prior context.
metadata.explanation: The guide notes that passing complete conversation history in subsequent requests is necessary for coherence; dropping it, not model fatigue or user inconsistency, causes contradictions and repeated questions.

cards.prompt: A customer opens a chat to resolve a billing dispute, then a shipping delay, then a return, all in one session. The agent's running summary blends details from all three issues, and it starts applying the return policy to the shipping delay question. What restructuring would prevent this cross-contamination?
cards.answer: Extract and persist each issue's structured data (IDs, amounts, statuses) into separate context layers per issue rather than one blended running summary.

metadata.subdomain: 5.1 Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
choices: Ask the customer to start a brand-new chat session for each separate issue, rather than letting one agent track billing, shipping, and returns together at all.
Extract and persist each issue's structured data (IDs, amounts, statuses) into separate context layers per issue rather than one blended running summary.
Increase how often the running summary is regenerated, so the single blended summary simply stays shorter overall.
Have the agent guess which issue a policy applies to based on whichever issue was mentioned most recently in the conversation.
metadata.explanation: Persisting structured, per-issue data in separate layers keeps facts from bleeding across issues; a single blended summary causes the cross-contamination described, and guessing by recency is an unreliable heuristic.

cards.prompt: A market-research pipeline has subagents scrape competitor pricing pages and return findings as plain prose paragraphs. When the synthesis agent combines them, it cannot tell which price came from which date or source, producing a report with unverifiable numbers. What should the subagents' output format require?
cards.answer: Structured metadata alongside each finding, such as source location, date, and methodological context, so downstream synthesis can verify and attribute claims.

metadata.subdomain: 5.1 Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
choices: Longer prose paragraphs with more descriptive adjectives about each price point, without any added source or date information.
A single aggregated number representing the average price across all sources, discarding which source or date each price came from.
Structured metadata alongside each finding, such as source location, date, and methodological context, so downstream synthesis can verify and attribute claims.
A confidence percentage attached to the overall report as a whole, with no other structural change to how the individual price findings are recorded or sourced.
metadata.explanation: Requiring subagents to attach dates, sources, and methodology as structured metadata supports accurate downstream synthesis, exactly as the guide specifies; prose-only output or a single aggregate number strips away the attribution needed to verify claims.

cards.prompt: A coordinator agent has a small context budget and calls three upstream research subagents. Each subagent currently returns its full chain-of-thought reasoning plus raw source text, which quickly exhausts the coordinator's budget across three calls. What change to the upstream agents best fixes this?
cards.answer: Modify the upstream agents to return structured data, such as key facts, citations, and relevance scores, instead of verbose content and reasoning chains.

metadata.subdomain: 5.1 Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
choices: Have the coordinator read only the first subagent's output in full and simply ignore the other two subagents' research findings entirely, losing that coverage.
Modify the upstream agents to return structured data, such as key facts, citations, and relevance scores, instead of verbose content and reasoning chains.
Increase the coordinator's context window budget substantially so it can accommodate the verbose upstream output as-is.
Have each subagent write its own final answer directly for the user, bypassing the coordinator's synthesis step entirely.
metadata.explanation: Returning structured facts and citations instead of full reasoning chains directly addresses limited downstream context budgets, as the guide recommends; expanding the window or dropping subagents' output entirely sacrifices either cost control or coverage.

cards.prompt: In an invoice-extraction review tool, a reviewer chats with an assistant across many turns about a disputed invoice, referencing a $12,450.00 total and a July 2nd due date early on. Later, the assistant's running summary reads 'a moderate invoice amount was due around early July,' and the reviewer catches the imprecision before approving a correction. What should the tool have done differently?
cards.answer: Kept the exact total and due date in a persistent fact block referenced alongside the summary, instead of letting them degrade into prose.

metadata.subdomain: 5.1 Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
choices: Skipped summarization entirely and replayed the entire raw transcript on every single turn, regardless of the added token cost.
Rounded the numbers before summarizing them, on the mistaken assumption that exact cents rarely matter to a reviewer who is about to approve a correction.
Kept the exact total and due date in a persistent fact block referenced alongside the summary, instead of letting them degrade into prose.
Asked the reviewer to re-type the invoice total from memory each time the assistant needed to reference it again later in the review.
metadata.explanation: This is the same progressive-summarization risk from a different angle: precise transactional facts must live in a structured block outside the summarized narrative, not be re-derived from vague prose or re-entered manually by the user.

cards.prompt: A support agent is handling a case where the customer has not asked for a human, the request fits within documented policy, and the agent is making steady progress toward a resolution. Under what circumstance should the agent escalate to a human?
cards.answer: Only when the customer explicitly requests a human, the policy has a gap or exception for the situation, or the agent cannot make further progress.

metadata.subdomain: 5.2 Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
choices: Whenever the case involves more than two back-and-forth messages, regardless of whether progress is being made.
Only when the customer explicitly requests a human, the policy has a gap or exception for the situation, or the agent cannot make further progress.
Whenever the customer's message contains negative or frustrated language, even if the underlying issue is simple and the agent could resolve it on its own.
Whenever the agent's self-reported confidence in its own answer happens to drop below an arbitrary, never-validated 80% threshold.
metadata.explanation: The guide names three legitimate triggers: explicit request, policy gap or exception, and inability to progress, not message count, sentiment, or self-reported confidence, which are explicitly called unreliable proxies.

cards.prompt: Two customers each contact support with a billing error. Customer A says, 'I want to speak to a manager right now.' Customer B says, 'This billing error is annoying, can someone fix it?' How should the agent's response differ?
cards.answer: Customer A should be escalated immediately for explicitly requesting a human; Customer B should be resolved directly, since there's no explicit escalation demand.

metadata.subdomain: 5.2 Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
choices: Both should be escalated immediately to a human agent, since billing errors of any kind always warrant human involvement and handling.
Neither should be escalated; both requests should be resolved autonomously by the agent, since billing errors are considered routine.
Customer A should be escalated immediately for explicitly requesting a human; Customer B should be resolved directly, since there's no explicit escalation demand.
Customer B should be escalated first because their tone suggests greater frustration, while Customer A's explicit human request can simply wait its turn in the queue.
metadata.explanation: The guide distinguishes honoring an explicit demand for a human immediately from offering direct resolution when the issue is straightforward; escalating based on which customer sounds more frustrated is the sentiment-based anti-pattern.

cards.prompt: A customer's very first message is, 'Please transfer me to a human agent, I don't want to deal with a bot.' The agent has tools available that could likely resolve the issue in seconds. What should the agent do?
cards.answer: Honor the explicit request for a human immediately, without first attempting investigation or resolution.

metadata.subdomain: 5.2 Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
choices: Run its diagnostic tools first to gather context so the human agent receives a fully investigated case, then escalate.
Attempt a quick resolution using its own tools first, and only escalate to a human if that initial attempt happens to fail.
Honor the explicit request for a human immediately, without first attempting investigation or resolution.
Ask the customer to confirm their request two more times before escalating, to avoid unnecessary handoffs.
metadata.explanation: The guide is explicit that an unambiguous request for a human should be honored immediately rather than delayed by investigation; running tools first or requiring repeated confirmation both needlessly stall a clear, explicit request.

cards.prompt: A customer writes, 'I'm so frustrated, this is the third time I've had to explain this,' but does not ask for a human, and the issue, a duplicate charge, is one the agent can resolve directly. What is the best response pattern?
cards.answer: Acknowledge the frustration, offer to resolve the duplicate charge directly, and escalate only if the customer reiterates a preference for a human.

metadata.subdomain: 5.2 Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
choices: Escalate immediately to a human agent because the customer's frustrated tone alone signals that the case must be complex.
Ignore the emotional content entirely and process the duplicate-charge refund mechanically, without any human-sounding acknowledgment of how the customer feels.
Acknowledge the frustration, offer to resolve the duplicate charge directly, and escalate only if the customer reiterates a preference for a human.
Ask the customer to rate their frustration on a scale of 1 to 10 before the agent decides how to proceed with the case.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends acknowledging frustration while offering resolution within the agent's capability, escalating only on reiteration; treating frustration itself as an automatic escalation trigger is the sentiment-based anti-pattern.

cards.prompt: An engineering team configures their support agent to escalate any case where the customer's message scores above a sentiment-negativity threshold, or where the model's own confidence score in its answer is below 70%. After launch, straightforward cases with mildly annoyed phrasing keep getting escalated unnecessarily, while some genuinely complex policy-gap cases pass through with high self-reported confidence. What does this reveal?
cards.answer: Sentiment-based escalation and self-reported confidence are unreliable proxies for case complexity, since neither reflects whether policy covers the situation.

metadata.subdomain: 5.2 Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
choices: The sentiment and confidence thresholds simply need to be tuned to slightly different numeric values, and the underlying escalation logic itself is otherwise fine.
Sentiment-based escalation and self-reported confidence are unreliable proxies for case complexity, since neither reflects whether policy covers the situation.
The model needs additional training data on customer emotions specifically to improve its sentiment-detection accuracy.
The issue is unrelated to escalation logic entirely and is instead a separate, pre-existing tool-calling reliability problem.
metadata.explanation: The guide explicitly warns that sentiment and self-reported confidence are unreliable proxies for complexity; the failure pattern described is a design flaw in the trigger itself, not something fixable by retuning thresholds or improving sentiment detection.

cards.prompt: An agent looks up a customer by name and phone number, and the account-lookup tool returns three matching accounts with similar names in the same city. What should the agent do?
cards.answer: Ask the customer for an additional identifier, such as order number, email, or billing zip code, to disambiguate rather than guessing.

metadata.subdomain: 5.2 Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
choices: Select the account with the most recent activity on file, assuming that recency alone reliably identifies which customer is actually calling in right now.
Select the account with the highest lifetime order value, simply assuming it must belong to the more important, higher-priority customer.
Ask the customer for an additional identifier, such as order number, email, or billing zip code, to disambiguate rather than guessing.
Proceed with the first account returned by the tool, since lookup tools typically rank their results by relevance already.
metadata.explanation: The guide requires requesting additional identifiers when multiple matches occur rather than choosing based on heuristics like recency, value, or return order; any of those heuristics risks acting on the wrong customer's account.

cards.prompt: A customer asks a retail support agent to match a competitor's lower price. The written policy only addresses price adjustments when the retailer's own site drops a price later, and says nothing about competitor pricing. The agent can technically issue a partial refund using the same discount tool used for own-site adjustments. What should it do?
cards.answer: Escalate the request, since the policy is silent on competitor price matching rather than explicitly addressing it.

metadata.subdomain: 5.2 Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
choices: Apply the discount using the own-site adjustment tool, since the mechanism is already technically capable of issuing it directly.
Deny the request outright, since the policy doesn't explicitly authorize competitor price matching.
Escalate the request, since the policy is silent on competitor price matching rather than explicitly addressing it.
Approve or deny based on how large the price difference is, using the agent's own judgment of what seems fair.
metadata.explanation: The guide identifies policy gaps or silence as an escalation trigger distinct from a policy that explicitly forbids something; using an unrelated tool to approximate an answer, flatly denying, or judging by size all substitute agent guesswork for a legitimate policy decision.

cards.prompt: A team wants their support agent to reliably distinguish cases it should resolve autonomously from cases it should escalate, beyond a one-line policy statement. What is the most effective way to encode this in the system prompt?
cards.answer: Add explicit escalation criteria along with few-shot examples in the system prompt that demonstrate escalate-versus-resolve decisions.

metadata.subdomain: 5.2 Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
choices: Add a single vague sentence instructing the agent to simply 'use good judgment' about when a case should be escalated.
Add explicit escalation criteria along with few-shot examples in the system prompt that demonstrate escalate-versus-resolve decisions.
Remove escalation guidance from the prompt and rely entirely on a separate classifier model to route cases.
Instruct the agent to escalate every case once its conversation crosses a certain length threshold, regardless of what the case is actually about.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends explicit criteria plus few-shot examples to teach the escalate/resolve boundary; vague instructions, an unrelated classifier, or a length threshold don't capture the nuanced judgment few-shot examples convey.

cards.prompt: A coordinator agent in a research pipeline dispatches a subagent to query a database. The subagent fails and reports back only 'query failed.' What information should the subagent have included to let the coordinator make a good recovery decision?
cards.answer: The failure type, the query that was attempted, any partial results obtained, and possible alternative approaches.

metadata.subdomain: 5.3 Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
choices: Nothing more is needed; the coordinator should simply retry the exact same query again without any additional context.
The failure type, the query that was attempted, any partial results obtained, and possible alternative approaches.
A generic apology message formatted for display to the end user, with no technical detail about what actually failed.
A recommendation that the entire research workflow be aborted immediately, without any detail about what was attempted or why.
metadata.explanation: Structured error context, including failure type, attempted query, partial results, and alternatives, is what enables intelligent coordinator recovery; a bare failure message or a blanket abort recommendation both withhold the context needed for a good decision.

cards.prompt: A subagent queries an inventory API for a rarely-stocked item. In one case the API times out after 30 seconds; in another, the API responds normally but returns zero matching items. How should these two outcomes be reported to the coordinator?
cards.answer: The timeout should be reported as an access failure that may warrant a retry, while the zero-match response should be reported as a valid, successful query with no matches.

metadata.subdomain: 5.3 Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
choices: Both should be reported identically as 'no results found,' on the assumption that the end result looks exactly the same to the coordinator either way, in every single case.
The timeout should be reported as a valid empty result, and the zero-match response should be reported as an access failure needing retry.
The timeout should be reported as an access failure that may warrant a retry, while the zero-match response should be reported as a valid, successful query with no matches.
Both should be reported as errors requiring an automatic retry, since neither call returned any usable inventory data for the coordinator to act on.
metadata.explanation: The guide draws a clear line between access failures like timeouts, which may need retry, and valid empty results, a successful query with no matches; collapsing that distinction either wastes retries on a legitimately empty result or hides a real access problem.

cards.prompt: A subagent's error-handling code catches every possible failure, including timeouts, malformed queries, permission errors, and empty results, and reports all of them to the coordinator as the single status string 'search unavailable.' What is the main problem with this design?
cards.answer: Generic statuses like this hide context the coordinator needs to pick a recovery strategy, such as retrying, using an alternative source, or proceeding with partial coverage.

metadata.subdomain: 5.3 Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
choices: The string 'search unavailable' is too long overall and will waste tokens inside the coordinator's already-limited context budget across many repeated calls each session, every time.
Generic statuses like this hide context the coordinator needs to pick a recovery strategy, such as retrying, using an alternative source, or proceeding with partial coverage.
The coordinator will misinterpret the string 'search unavailable' as a successful result and proceed as though real data had actually been found.
This design is fine as long as every subagent uses the exact same generic status string consistently across the whole pipeline every time.
metadata.explanation: The guide specifically calls out generic statuses like 'search unavailable' as hiding context the coordinator needs; the problem isn't string length or misinterpretation risk, it's the loss of information needed to decide how to recover.

cards.prompt: A multi-agent pipeline experiences a failure in one of five parallel subagents. The team is deciding between two designs: Design A has the failing subagent silently return an empty result set that looks like a normal successful search; Design B has the coordinator immediately abort the entire pipeline the moment any single subagent fails. What is the issue with these two designs?
cards.answer: Both are anti-patterns: faking success hides real problems, and aborting everything over one failure is too fragile; propagate the failure with context instead.

metadata.subdomain: 5.3 Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
choices: Design A is correct because it keeps the whole pipeline running smoothly without interruption; Design B should be avoided as too disruptive.
Design B is correct because failing fast prevents bad data from silently spreading downstream to other agents; Design A should be avoided entirely as too risky to trust.
Both are anti-patterns: faking success hides real problems, and aborting everything over one failure is too fragile; propagate the failure with context instead.
Both designs are acceptable as long as the failure is logged somewhere in a file for an engineer to review sometime later on, at their convenience.
metadata.explanation: The guide names both silently suppressing errors and terminating entire workflows on single failures as anti-patterns; the fix is structured error propagation that lets the coordinator make an informed recovery decision, not picking one flawed extreme over the other.

cards.prompt: A subagent responsible for fetching pricing data hits a rate limit after retrieving results for 3 of 5 requested products. What should its response to the coordinator include?
cards.answer: A failure type (rate limit), the specific requests attempted, the 3 partial results obtained, and a suggested alternative such as retrying after backoff.

metadata.subdomain: 5.3 Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
choices: Only the 3 successful results, silently omitting any mention that the other 2 products were never retrieved at all.
A failure type (rate limit), the specific requests attempted, the 3 partial results obtained, and a suggested alternative such as retrying after backoff.
A single boolean flag indicating overall success or failure, with no detail about which specific requests succeeded.
An error message instructing the coordinator to abandon the entire pricing task altogether, rather than make any use of the partial results already gathered.
metadata.explanation: This is the concrete skill of returning structured error context, type, attempted work, partial results, and alternatives, which enables recovery; omitting the failure, collapsing it to a boolean, or recommending abandonment all discard usable information.

cards.prompt: A customer-lookup subagent is being redesigned so its error reporting is more useful. It currently returns the same 'no data' response whether the lookup service was unreachable or whether the service responded and simply found no matching customer. What change should the redesign make?
cards.answer: Distinguish an access failure, such as an unreachable service, from a valid empty result, such as no match found, so the coordinator can judge whether a retry helps.

metadata.subdomain: 5.3 Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
choices: Report both cases the same way but include a timestamp, so at least the coordinator can see roughly when each lookup attempt happened to occur during that particular session.
Distinguish an access failure, such as an unreachable service, from a valid empty result, such as no match found, so the coordinator can judge whether a retry helps.
Change all 'no data' responses to raise a hard exception that immediately stops the calling agent from proceeding any further at all.
Remove error reporting from the lookup subagent entirely and let the coordinator infer its status from response latency instead.
metadata.explanation: Separating access failures from genuinely empty results is the specific skill needed so the coordinator only retries when retrying could help; adding a timestamp without separating the cases, or inferring status from latency, doesn't give the coordinator a reliable signal.

cards.prompt: A subagent occasionally hits a transient network blip when calling an external API, which usually succeeds on a second attempt. How should the subagent handle this, versus a persistent authentication failure it cannot fix on its own?
cards.answer: Retry the transient blip locally, but propagate the persistent authentication failure upward with what was attempted, since the subagent can't resolve it itself.

metadata.subdomain: 5.3 Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
choices: Propagate every failure, whether transient or persistent, up to the coordinator every time so it always has full visibility.
Retry the transient network blip locally and resolve it silently, but also silently swallow the persistent authentication failure to avoid bothering the coordinator.
Retry the transient blip locally, but propagate the persistent authentication failure upward with what was attempted, since the subagent can't resolve it itself.
Never attempt any local recovery at all; always propagate both transient and persistent failures to the coordinator immediately.
metadata.explanation: The guide favors local recovery for transient, resolvable failures, propagating only failures the subagent genuinely cannot fix, with context attached; propagating everything adds noise, while silently swallowing an unresolvable failure hides a real problem from the coordinator.

cards.prompt: A research pipeline could not access two of eight planned sources due to paywalls, but successfully synthesized findings from the remaining six. How should the final synthesis report reflect this?
cards.answer: Include explicit coverage annotations noting which findings the six accessible sources support well, and which topic areas have gaps from the two unavailable sources.

metadata.subdomain: 5.3 Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
choices: Present the six-source findings as fully comprehensive and complete, without ever mentioning that two sources were inaccessible.
Refuse to produce any report at all, on the grounds that a source set missing even two of eight planned sources is far too incomplete to synthesize responsibly at all.
Include explicit coverage annotations noting which findings the six accessible sources support well, and which topic areas have gaps from the two unavailable sources.
Replace the missing sources' expected content with placeholder text generated by the model itself, in order to fill in the apparent gaps.
metadata.explanation: Structuring output with coverage annotations honestly represents what is and isn't well-supported, per the guide; presenting partial coverage as complete, refusing entirely, or fabricating placeholder content are all worse than transparent annotation.

cards.prompt: During a long codebase exploration session, an agent initially gives precise answers referencing specific classes like OrderValidator and RefundProcessor. Three hours into the session, it starts answering questions about the refund flow by describing 'typical patterns you'd see in an e-commerce refund system' instead of naming the actual classes it found earlier. What does this behavior indicate?
cards.answer: This is context degradation in an extended session; the model has started substituting generic reasoning for the specific, previously-discovered facts.

metadata.subdomain: 5.4 Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration
choices: The codebase itself must have changed significantly mid-session, which surely invalidated all of the agent's earlier findings about the refund flow entirely.
This is context degradation in an extended session; the model has started substituting generic reasoning for the specific, previously-discovered facts.
The agent has silently switched over to a different, less capable underlying model partway through the long session without telling anyone.
The user's questions have gradually become too vague over time for the agent to answer with any specificity at all, regardless of what it discovered earlier.
metadata.explanation: The guide describes this exact symptom, models in long sessions drifting toward describing 'typical patterns' instead of citing specific classes discovered earlier, as context degradation, not a model swap, codebase change, or vague questioning.

cards.prompt: A team is designing an agent that explores a large monorepo across a session likely to span many context resets. They want a way for the agent to reliably recall specific findings, such as 'the payment webhook handler is in services/billing/webhook.py,' even after context has been compacted or reset. What mechanism serves this purpose?
cards.answer: Persisting key findings to a scratchpad file that the agent writes during exploration and can reference across context boundaries.

metadata.subdomain: 5.4 Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration
choices: Increasing the model's context window substantially so that context resets during long codebase exploration sessions never happen at all.
Relying on the model to remember the finding on its own, simply because it was important enough to be memorable at the time.
Persisting key findings to a scratchpad file that the agent writes during exploration and can reference across context boundaries.
Re-running the entire exploration from scratch after every single context reset, just to rediscover the same finding it already had.
metadata.explanation: Scratchpad files are the guide's recommended mechanism for persisting findings across context boundaries; hoping the model remembers, enlarging the window indefinitely, or repeating full exploration are unreliable or wasteful substitutes.

cards.prompt: An agent exploring an unfamiliar codebase discovers that authentication logic is split across three files and writes this down. Two hours later, a follow-up question requires recalling exactly which three files. What should the agent do to answer accurately, given intervening context churn?
cards.answer: Reference the scratchpad file where it recorded the earlier finding, rather than relying on its in-context memory of the discovery.

metadata.subdomain: 5.4 Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration
choices: Re-derive the answer from scratch by re-reading the entire authentication codebase again, hoping to rediscover the same three files.
Reference the scratchpad file where it recorded the earlier finding, rather than relying on its in-context memory of the discovery.
Give its best guess based on common authentication file-naming conventions, without checking any earlier recorded finding.
Ask the user to remind it which three files were relevant, since the agent no longer has that information available.
metadata.explanation: The skill here is actively referencing a maintained scratchpad file for prior findings to counteract context degradation, rather than re-deriving everything, guessing from convention, or offloading recall onto the user.

cards.prompt: A main agent needs to understand how a legacy authorization system works across dozens of files with deeply nested logic. Reading every file directly would flood the main agent's context with verbose, low-level detail. What approach lets the main agent maintain high-level understanding while still getting thorough coverage?
cards.answer: Spawn subagents to investigate specific sub-questions, such as tracing the permission-check call chain, while the main agent coordinates at a high level.

metadata.subdomain: 5.4 Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration
choices: Read every file directly into the main agent's context in full every single time it's needed, accepting the resulting token cost as simply unavoidable overhead.
Spawn subagents to investigate specific sub-questions, such as tracing the permission-check call chain, while the main agent coordinates at a high level.
Skip detailed investigation entirely and answer the question based only on the files' names, without reading contents.
Have a single subagent read the entire codebase directly and return its full raw exploration transcript back verbatim, unfiltered.
metadata.explanation: Delegating specific investigative questions to subagents isolates verbose exploration output while the main agent retains high-level coordination, exactly as the guide describes; reading everything directly or having one subagent dump a full raw transcript defeats the purpose of delegation.

cards.prompt: A long-running codebase migration involves several coordinated agents, and the process needs to survive a crash partway through without losing all progress. How should this be designed for reliable recovery?
cards.answer: Each agent exports its state to a known location, and the coordinator loads a manifest of that state on resume, injecting it into agent prompts to resume work.

metadata.subdomain: 5.4 Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration
choices: Each agent keeps its progress only in its own in-memory context, so the whole multi-agent migration restarts from step one after any crash whatsoever, with nothing saved.
Each agent exports its state to a known location, and the coordinator loads a manifest of that state on resume, injecting it into agent prompts to resume work.
Only the coordinator's state is saved; individual agents are expected to re-derive their prior work from scratch after a crash.
State is saved as a single unstructured log file with no defined schema, to be parsed manually by an engineer after any crash.
metadata.explanation: Structured state exports plus a coordinator-loaded manifest is the guide's crash-recovery design; restarting from scratch, saving only coordinator state, or using an unstructured log all fail to reliably restore multi-agent progress.

cards.prompt: An exploration is organized into phases: phase one maps the module structure, phase two traces data flow within modules identified in phase one. How should the transition between phases be handled to preserve useful context for phase two's subagents?
cards.answer: Summarize the key findings from phase one and inject that summary into the initial context of phase two's subagents.

metadata.subdomain: 5.4 Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration
choices: Give phase two's subagents the full raw, unfiltered output of every phase one subagent, without any summarization at all.
Give phase two's subagents no information from phase one at all, so they explore with a completely fresh perspective.
Summarize the key findings from phase one and inject that summary into the initial context of phase two's subagents.
Have phase two's subagents simply ask the user to describe what phase one already found, instead of using the agent's own results.
metadata.explanation: Summarizing key findings from one phase and injecting them into the next phase's initial context is the recommended handoff pattern; passing raw unfiltered output floods context, while passing nothing or relying on the user loses the value of phase one entirely.

cards.prompt: During an extended codebase exploration session, the agent's context is filling up with verbose file-read output and tool call logs from earlier discovery steps, even though the important conclusions have already been captured. What is an appropriate lightweight action to take at this point?
cards.answer: Use /compact to reduce context usage, since the verbose discovery output is no longer needed once key findings are captured.

metadata.subdomain: 5.4 Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration
choices: End the session and lose all accumulated understanding, starting a brand-new session for any further work.
Use /compact to reduce context usage, since the verbose discovery output is no longer needed once key findings are captured.
Continue accumulating all tool output indefinitely, since context limits are only a soft guideline.
Manually delete random earlier messages from the conversation without any regard for their content or importance to the case.
metadata.explanation: /compact is the guide's named tool for reducing context usage from verbose discovery output during extended sessions; ending the session, ignoring limits, or deleting messages arbitrarily are all worse than a targeted compaction once findings are already captured.

cards.prompt: An invoice-extraction system reports 97% overall field accuracy across all document types, and the team uses this figure to justify reducing human review. What risk does relying on this single aggregate number carry?
cards.answer: The aggregate figure may mask much worse performance on specific document types or fields, even though the overall number looks strong.

metadata.subdomain: 5.5 Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
choices: None; a 97% aggregate accuracy figure is high enough on its own to justify full automation, regardless of how it breaks down by segment or field.
The aggregate figure may mask much worse performance on specific document types or fields, even though the overall number looks strong.
The only real risk here is that the 97% figure was measured on too small a sample size to be statistically meaningful.
The aggregate number is likely inflated due to rounding, and the true number is probably lower across the board.
metadata.explanation: The guide specifically warns that an aggregate figure like 97% can hide poor performance on particular document types or fields; the risk is about masked variation, not sample size or generic rounding error.

cards.prompt: A document-extraction team wants an ongoing way to catch new, previously unseen error patterns in extractions the model reports as high-confidence, without reviewing every single one manually. What sampling approach addresses this?
cards.answer: Stratified random sampling of high-confidence extractions across document types and fields, to measure error rates and surface novel patterns.

metadata.subdomain: 5.5 Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
choices: Reviewing only the extractions the model flags as low-confidence, on the assumption that high-confidence ones are unlikely to contain any errors at all.
Reviewing a random sample of all extractions regardless of confidence level, drawn uniformly without regard to document type.
Stratified random sampling of high-confidence extractions across document types and fields, to measure error rates and surface novel patterns.
Reviewing only the most recently processed extractions each week, regardless of their document type or confidence level.
metadata.explanation: Stratified random sampling of high-confidence extractions is the guide's recommended approach for catching novel error patterns that wouldn't be found by only reviewing low-confidence cases; uniform sampling or recency-based sampling can miss segment-specific issues that stratification is designed to surface.

cards.prompt: A team building an insurance-claims extraction pipeline wants to implement ongoing quality monitoring now that most extractions are marked high-confidence and skip manual review. What should they build?
cards.answer: A process that periodically pulls a stratified random sample of high-confidence extractions, across document types and fields, for manual error-rate measurement.

metadata.subdomain: 5.5 Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
choices: A dashboard that only tracks the overall percentage of extractions marked high-confidence over time, without ever sampling a single one of them for accuracy at all.
A process that periodically pulls a stratified random sample of high-confidence extractions, across document types and fields, for manual error-rate measurement.
A rule that automatically re-reviews any extraction older than 30 days, regardless of its confidence score or document type.
A system that only reviews extractions that customers themselves flag as incorrect, with no other systematic sampling.
metadata.explanation: This operationalizes stratified sampling as an ongoing monitoring process, matching the guide's recommended skill; tracking only the high-confidence percentage, age-based re-review, or relying solely on customer complaints all fail to systematically catch quiet error patterns.

cards.prompt: A team wants to route extracted fields to human review based on how likely the model is to have gotten them wrong. They have the model emit a confidence score per field. What is required before these scores can be trusted to drive review routing decisions?
cards.answer: The scores must be calibrated against labeled validation sets so a given value reflects the field's true error likelihood.

metadata.subdomain: 5.5 Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
choices: Nothing further is needed; a raw model-reported confidence score is inherently meaningful on its own and can be used directly for routing.
The scores must be calibrated against labeled validation sets so a given value reflects the field's true error likelihood.
The scores should be replaced by a single document-level confidence score instead of field-level scores.
The scores only need to be normalized onto a consistent 0-100 scale in order to become usable for routing decisions.
metadata.explanation: Field-level confidence scores need calibration against labeled validation data to be meaningful for routing, per the guide; treating raw scores as trustworthy, collapsing to document-level, or merely rescaling doesn't establish that a given score actually predicts error likelihood.

cards.prompt: An extraction system currently sends every field to human review regardless of the model's certainty, creating a large reviewer backlog. The team wants to reduce unnecessary review while catching real errors. What should they implement?
cards.answer: Have the model output field-level confidence scores, then calibrate review thresholds against labeled validation sets so only uncertain fields go to reviewers.

metadata.subdomain: 5.5 Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
choices: Have the model output field-level confidence scores, then calibrate review thresholds against labeled validation sets so only uncertain fields go to reviewers.
Reduce reviewer workload by having reviewers only spot-check a single randomly chosen document from each batch, ignoring the rest entirely.
Set a single fixed threshold arbitrarily, such as always reviewing fields below 50% confidence, without any validation at all against real-world labeled outcomes.
Stop collecting confidence scores altogether and instead route documents to review based purely on how long they are.
metadata.explanation: The correct approach ties field confidence scores to thresholds calibrated against labeled data; an arbitrary uncalibrated threshold, document length as a proxy, or coarse batch spot-checking don't reliably target the fields actually likely to be wrong.

cards.prompt: Before removing human review from a growing share of extractions, a team wants to be confident automation is safe across the board, not just on average. What should they do first?
cards.answer: Analyze accuracy broken down by document type and field to confirm consistent performance across all segments before reducing human review.

metadata.subdomain: 5.5 Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
choices: Trust the overall aggregate accuracy metric on its own, since it already reflects performance across every document type and case.
Analyze accuracy broken down by document type and field to confirm consistent performance across all segments before reducing human review.
Survey a small handful of customers about their overall satisfaction with recent extractions, rather than measuring accuracy directly across segments.
Reduce review incrementally by a fixed percentage each and every month regardless of what the measured accuracy actually shows.
metadata.explanation: Segment-level validation by document type and field is required to confirm automation is safe everywhere, not just on average, per the guide; relying on the aggregate metric, customer sentiment, or a schedule-based reduction ignores exactly the variation that matters.

cards.prompt: A reviewer team has limited capacity and cannot review every extraction. Some documents have internally contradictory source values, such as two different totals listed, and some fields have low model confidence. How should review capacity be allocated?
cards.answer: Route low-confidence or ambiguous/contradictory extractions to human review, prioritizing them given limited reviewer capacity.

metadata.subdomain: 5.5 Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
choices: Spread review evenly across all extractions regardless of confidence or ambiguity, giving each one the same fixed amount of reviewer attention.
Review only the extractions belonging to the largest customers, regardless of the model's confidence or any ambiguity.
Route low-confidence or ambiguous/contradictory extractions to human review, prioritizing them given limited reviewer capacity.
Skip review of ambiguous documents entirely since resolving contradictions is not a human reviewer's job.
metadata.explanation: Directing scarce reviewer capacity toward low-confidence and ambiguous/contradictory cases is the guide's recommended prioritization; spreading attention evenly, prioritizing by customer size, or skipping ambiguous documents all misallocate limited review resources away from where errors are most likely.

cards.prompt: A literature-review agent reads ten medical studies and produces a single condensed paragraph stating 'multiple studies suggest a moderate effect.' A reader later asks which specific studies support this and cannot tell from the report. What went wrong?
cards.answer: Source attribution was lost during the summarization step, since findings were compressed without preserving which claims came from which sources.

metadata.subdomain: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis
choices: The agent should have used more than ten studies overall, since a larger sample would have strengthened the claim.
Source attribution was lost during the summarization step, since findings were compressed without preserving which claims came from which sources.
The reader should have read all ten original medical studies directly themselves in full detail, instead of relying on the condensed report at all.
The claim itself is likely false or fabricated, simply because it cannot currently be traced back to a specific source.
metadata.explanation: This is the guide's named risk of losing source attribution during compression; the fix is preserving claim-source mappings, not adding more studies, bypassing the report, or assuming the claim is false.

cards.prompt: A legal-research pipeline has subagents read case law and return prose summaries of relevant holdings. The synthesis agent downstream needs to cite specific cases for each claim in the final memo but cannot recover which subagent finding came from which case. What should the subagents' output format include instead?
cards.answer: Structured claim-source mappings, such as case name, citation, and excerpt, tied to each finding and preserved for the synthesis agent.

metadata.subdomain: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis
choices: A longer prose summary containing more descriptive detail about the reasoning behind each case's holding.
Structured claim-source mappings, such as case name, citation, and excerpt, tied to each finding and preserved for the synthesis agent.
A single ranked list of the most important cases overall, without ever linking any specific claim in the memo back to a particular case.
A confidence score for how well-established each holding is overall, with no source information of any kind attached to it.
metadata.explanation: Structured claim-source mappings that downstream agents preserve through synthesis is the specific skill needed here; more prose, an unlinked ranking, or a bare confidence score all still fail to connect claims back to their sources.

cards.prompt: A synthesis agent combining findings from many sources needs to preserve which conclusions are broadly agreed upon versus which are debated, including the original researchers' own caveats. How should the final report be structured?
cards.answer: Use explicit sections distinguishing well-established findings from contested ones, preserving each source's original characterization and methodology.

metadata.subdomain: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis
choices: Blend all findings into one single continuous narrative throughout the whole report, smoothing over any disagreements between sources purely for readability.
Report only the findings that every single source agrees on, and omit any contested or debated findings entirely.
Use explicit sections distinguishing well-established findings from contested ones, preserving each source's original characterization and methodology.
Present the contested findings first in the report, since disagreements tend to be the most interesting to readers.
metadata.explanation: The guide calls for explicit structural separation between well-established and contested findings while preserving sources' own framing; blending everything into one narrative or dropping contested findings entirely erases exactly the distinction the report needs to convey.

cards.prompt: Two credible industry reports give different figures for the same market's size, one says $4.2B, the other $5.1B, and both use defensible methodologies. A synthesis agent must include a market-size figure in its report. What should it do?
cards.answer: Include both figures, explicitly annotated with their respective sources, rather than arbitrarily selecting or blending one value.

metadata.subdomain: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis
choices: Average the two conflicting figures together and present $4.65B as the report's single unified number.
Pick whichever figure comes from the more recognizable or prestigious source, and simply present only that one as the report's number.
Include both figures, explicitly annotated with their respective sources, rather than arbitrarily selecting or blending one value.
Omit the market-size figure from the report entirely, simply to avoid any appearance of disagreement between sources.
metadata.explanation: The guide requires annotating conflicting statistics with source attribution rather than arbitrarily resolving the conflict; averaging, picking by source prestige, and omitting the figure all discard information the reader needs to judge the conflict themselves.

cards.prompt: A subagent analyzing financial documents finds that two source filings report different revenue figures for the same quarter. The subagent is not positioned to know which filing is authoritative. What should it do with its output?
cards.answer: Complete its analysis with both conflicting values included and annotated, leaving reconciliation to the coordinator rather than resolving it itself.

metadata.subdomain: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis
choices: Silently use the higher of the two conflicting figures, on the assumption that investors generally prefer seeing optimistic revenue numbers reported.
Refuse to complete any part of its analysis until a human manually resolves which of the two figures is correct.
Complete its analysis with both conflicting values included and annotated, leaving reconciliation to the coordinator rather than resolving it itself.
Pick whichever revenue figure happens to appear first among the source documents it processed, without checking the other filing.
metadata.explanation: The subagent should surface both values with clear annotation and defer reconciliation to the coordinator, per the guide; silently choosing one value by any arbitrary rule, or halting entirely, both remove the coordinator's ability to make an informed decision.

cards.prompt: A synthesis agent combines a 2019 industry report stating 12% market share with a 2024 report stating 8% market share for the same company, and flags this as a contradiction requiring resolution. What is the actual issue, and what would have prevented the false alarm?
cards.answer: This likely reflects legitimate change over time, not a contradiction; requiring collection dates in each source's output would have made the gap clear upfront.

metadata.subdomain: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis
choices: This is a genuine contradiction between the two reports, and the agent was correct to flag it as an error needing resolution.
The two reports likely used different market-size denominators entirely, so the fix is to standardize how market share gets calculated across every source used.
This likely reflects legitimate change over time, not a contradiction; requiring collection dates in each source's output would have made the gap clear upfront.
One of the two reports must simply be unreliable or wrong, so the agent should discard the older of the two figures.
metadata.explanation: The guide warns that temporal differences can be misread as contradictions without collection dates attached; the fix is requiring dates in structured output, not assuming one source is wrong or that a denominator mismatch is the cause without evidence.

cards.prompt: A synthesis report needs to present quarterly financial figures, breaking news about a regulatory change, and a list of technical implementation findings, all gathered from different subagents. How should the final report present these different kinds of content?
cards.answer: Render each content type in the form suited to it — financial data as tables, news as prose, technical findings as lists — rather than forcing one uniform format.

metadata.subdomain: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis
choices: Convert everything into a single uniform prose narrative throughout, purely for the sake of consistency across the whole report.
Convert everything into bullet-point lists across the board, since lists are generally considered the easiest format for readers to scan quickly regardless of content.
Render each content type in the form suited to it — financial data as tables, news as prose, technical findings as lists — rather than forcing one uniform format.
Convert everything into raw data tables, including the narrative news content, purely for the sake of a consistent visual style throughout.
metadata.explanation: The guide recommends rendering content in the format naturally suited to it, tables for financial data, prose for news, structured lists for technical findings; forcing any single uniform format across all content types obscures rather than clarifies the material.

cards.prompt: Which domain carries the single largest share of the exam?
cards.answer: Agentic Architecture and Orchestration

choices: Context Management and Reliability
Prompt Engineering and Structured Output
Agentic Architecture and Orchestration
Claude Code Configuration and Workflows
metadata.explanation: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration is 27% — the biggest slice and the most scenario-heavy. It's where your study should start.

cards.prompt: Roughly what combined weight do the top three domains cover?
cards.answer: About sixty-seven

choices: About forty percent
About sixty-seven
About eighty-eight
About fifty-two
metadata.explanation: 27 + 20 + 20 = 67%. Agentic, Claude Code, and Prompting together nearly reach the 72% pass line on their own — hence the study order.

cards.prompt: What scaled score do you need to pass the CCA-F?
cards.answer: Seven hundred twenty

choices: Seven hundred twenty
Six hundred eighty
Eight hundred fifty
Seven hundred fifty
metadata.explanation: 720 out of 1000. It's a scaled score, not a raw percent-correct — items are weighted. Aim well above it (850+) for a safe first-attempt pass.

cards.prompt: Which pair are the two SMALLEST domains by weight?
cards.answer: Tool Design with MCP and Context Management

choices: Prompt Engineering and Agentic Architecture
Claude Code Config and Prompt Engineering
Tool Design with MCP and Context Management
Agentic Architecture and Context Management
metadata.explanation: Tools/MCP (18%) and Context Management (15%) are the lightest — still ~20 questions combined, so not ignorable, just second in line.

cards.prompt: In the loop, whose job is it to actually execute the tool's code?
cards.answer: Your own code runs it

choices: The Claude model runs it
Your own code runs it
The API server runs it
The tool schema runs it
metadata.explanation: The model only emits a tool_use request matching the schema. YOUR code runs the function and returns a tool_result. The model never executes anything.

cards.prompt: What stop_reason signals that the model is requesting a tool call?
cards.answer: The value tool_use

choices: The value end_turn
The value max_tokens
The value tool_use
The value stop_seq
metadata.explanation: stop_reason: "tool_use" means the response contains one or more tool_use blocks you must fulfil. Anything else (usually end_turn) means it answered — exit the loop.

cards.prompt: After a tool call, what must you add to messages before the next request?
cards.answer: Both assistant then result

choices: Only the tool_result block
Only the assistant content
Both assistant then result
Neither, just re-ask it
metadata.explanation: Append BOTH: the assistant's turn (its tool_use request) AND a user turn with the tool_result. Skip the assistant turn and the API rejects the conversation as malformed.

cards.prompt: What most reliably guarantees the model returns schema-valid JSON?
cards.answer: A tool schema plus tool_choice

choices: Asking politely within the prompt
Setting the temperature down to zero
A tool schema plus tool_choice
Parsing prose with a regex
metadata.explanation: Forcing a tool via tool_choice makes the model's input conform to your input_schema. Prompt-only requests drift; this is structurally guaranteed.

cards.prompt: What does an eval fundamentally give you that ad-hoc testing does not?
cards.answer: An objective number to compare versions

choices: A faster model to run the prompt
An objective number to compare versions
A prompt that can never fail again
A way to remove the grader entirely
metadata.explanation: An eval scores outputs across a dataset and averages them, so you can compare prompt versions numerically. Ad-hoc testing gives you a vibe, not a metric.

cards.prompt: "Is the output valid, parseable JSON?" — which grader fits best?
cards.answer: A code grader validates it

choices: A human grader reviews it
A model grader judges it
A code grader validates it
No grader is needed here
metadata.explanation: Syntax validity is deterministic, so a code grader (json.loads → 10/0) is exact and free. Save the model grader for judgment calls like task-following.

cards.prompt: Why ask a model grader for reasoning BEFORE it outputs the score?
cards.answer: Without it, it defaults near six

choices: It makes the response much shorter
Without it, it defaults near six
It lets you skip the code grader
It forces the output into pure JSON
metadata.explanation: Reasoning first (strengths/weaknesses) forces genuine assessment. Skip it and the model lazily clusters everything around a middling ~6, so the eval can't discriminate.

cards.prompt: When iterating, what makes a prompt's eval score actually useful?
cards.answer: That you can move it by editing

choices: That the score is above eight
That the score never changes at all
That you can move it by editing
That the grader is always a human
metadata.explanation: The absolute value isn't good or bad. What matters is that changing the prompt moves the number — eval is the instrument you steer by, not a final verdict.

cards.prompt: Which opening line best follows the clear-and-direct technique?
cards.answer: Create a 30-minute workout plan for beginners

choices: Do you know much about workout routines?
Create a 30-minute workout plan for beginners
I was wondering about fitness and workouts
What sort of workout might be good for me?
metadata.explanation: A direct instruction led by an action verb (‘Create’) with a specific task beats any question or vague musing. The first line sets everything up.

cards.prompt: Claude keeps missing sarcasm in social posts. Best fix?
cards.answer: Give examples of sarcasm labeled negative

choices: Tell it to be more careful with sarcasm
Make the prompt longer with instructions
Give examples of sarcasm labeled negative
Ask it to guess when things are sarcastic
metadata.explanation: Sarcasm is a corner case — show it with input/output examples (multi-shot), don't tell it to try harder. Examples teach edge cases instructions can't.

cards.prompt: Which pair of specificity tools should you use, and when?
cards.answer: Guidelines always; process steps for complex tasks

choices: Process steps always; guidelines only rarely
Guidelines always; process steps for complex tasks
Only one of them, never combine the two
Guidelines for tone; process steps for length
metadata.explanation: Output guidelines belong in nearly every prompt (your consistency safety-net). Add process steps when the task needs multi-angle reasoning. Pros combine both.

cards.prompt: What is the main purpose of XML tags in a prompt?
cards.answer: To bound content and clarify its structure

choices: To cut the token count of the prompt
To speed up the model's processing time
To make the prompt look more professional
To bound content and clarify its structure
metadata.explanation: Descriptive tags (<athlete_information>) delimit interpolated data from instructions so Claude parses structure correctly — most valuable as prompts grow complex.

cards.prompt: What scaled score is required to pass the CCA-F exam?
cards.answer: A score of 720 / 1000

choices: A score of 650 / 1000
A score of 720 / 1000
A score of 800 / 1000
A score of 900 / 1000
metadata.explanation: 720 out of 1000 passes. Remember it's a scaled score (weighted), not a raw percentage of questions correct.

cards.prompt: In the agent loop, which stop_reason means Claude is requesting a tool?
cards.answer: The value tool_use

choices: The value end_turn
The value max_tokens
The value tool_use
The value stop_seq
metadata.explanation: stop_reason of tool_use means the response holds tool_use blocks you must fulfil and feed back. Anything else (usually end_turn) means it answered — exit the loop.

cards.prompt: Which single domain carries the largest share of the exam?
cards.answer: Agentic Architecture, 27%

choices: Prompt Engineering, at 20%
Tool Design and MCP, 18%
Claude Code Workflows, 20%
Agentic Architecture, 27%
metadata.explanation: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration is the biggest at 27%. With Claude Code (20%) and Prompting (20%), the top three are ~67% of the score.

cards.prompt: You need to check whether output is valid JSON. Which grader fits?
cards.answer: A code grader validates it

choices: A human grader reviews it
A model grader judges it
A code grader validates it
No grader is needed here
metadata.explanation: Syntax validity is deterministic, so a code grader (json.loads → 10/0) is exact and free. Reserve the model grader for judgment like task-following.

cards.prompt: Why ask a model grader for reasoning BEFORE the score?
cards.answer: Without it, it defaults near six

choices: It makes responses much shorter
Without it, it defaults near six
It lets you drop the code grader
It forces output into pure JSON
metadata.explanation: Reasoning first (strengths/weaknesses) forces real assessment. Skip it and the model clusters everything near a middling six, so the eval can't discriminate.

cards.prompt: What is the single defining line between an agent and a workflow?
cards.answer: Whether the model or code controls flow

choices: Whether the system calls any tools
Whether the model or code controls flow
Whether the output is JSON or prose
Whether more than one model is used
metadata.explanation: A workflow runs a developer-defined path; an agent lets the model direct its own steps and stopping point. Tools, JSON, and model count are all orthogonal to that split.

cards.prompt: A task splits into independent parts that can run at once. Best shape?
cards.answer: Orchestrator-workers with fan-out

choices: Prompt chaining across fixed steps
Evaluator-optimizer with a rubric
Orchestrator-workers with fan-out
A single autonomous tool loop
metadata.explanation: Independent, parallelizable subtasks call for orchestrator-workers: decompose, fan out to isolated contexts, synthesize. Wall-clock is the slowest worker, not the sum.

cards.prompt: Your 002 meal-plan eval (generate, grade, revise) is which topology?
cards.answer: Evaluator-optimizer reflection

choices: Routing by input category
Evaluator-optimizer reflection
Orchestrator-workers fan-out
Autonomous agent tool loop
metadata.explanation: Generate → score against a rubric → revise is the evaluator-optimizer loop. You ran it open-loop (you were the optimizer); build 06 closes it.

cards.prompt: Which trait most signals you need an autonomous agent, not a workflow?
cards.answer: The path can't be known upfront

choices: The output must be valid JSON
The steps are known in advance
The path can't be known upfront
The task uses exactly one tool
metadata.explanation: Autonomous agents earn their cost only when the control flow can't be predetermined. If the steps are knowable, a workflow is simpler, cheaper, and easier to test.

cards.prompt: Why give each orchestrator worker only its own subtask, not the whole goal?
cards.answer: Isolated context scales and focuses

choices: It makes the workers run slower
Isolated context scales and focuses
The API forbids sharing the goal
It guarantees valid JSON output
metadata.explanation: Isolated context keeps each worker's window small (the job can exceed one context) and its reasoning focused, with no cross-talk. Sharing everything reinflates context and invites drift.

cards.prompt: A task must extract numbers, normalize them, sort them, then render a table — each step needs the previous step's clean output. Best pattern?
cards.answer: Chain the four steps in sequence

choices: Parallelize the four steps as inputs
Chain the four steps in sequence
Route each step to a specialist
Ask one prompt to do all four
metadata.explanation: Each step depends on the last, so they must run in order — that's prompt-chaining. Parallel needs independent subtasks; one big prompt fumbles the multi-step transform.

cards.prompt: You must run the same impact analysis over four independent stakeholder groups, and you want all four back fast. Best pattern?
cards.answer: Parallelize one prompt over the inputs

choices: Chain them so each builds on prior
Route each to a different specialist
Parallelize one prompt over the inputs
Combine all four into one prompt
metadata.explanation: Independent subtasks you all want → parallelization (same prompt, many inputs, concurrently). It trades cost (N calls at once) to hide latency. No step depends on another, so chaining would only add delay.

cards.prompt: Your router keeps sending password-reset tickets to the billing specialist. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Improve the selector's classification prompt

choices: Add a fallback catch-all support route
Retry the routing call a few times
Improve the selector's classification prompt
Lengthen each specialist's own prompt
metadata.explanation: Routing quality is classifier quality — a mis-route means the selector prompt is weak. Fix the classification (the root cause); a fallback route or retries just bolt a layer onto a broken selector.

cards.prompt: What actually makes these three 'workflows' rather than 'agents'?
cards.answer: You define the control flow in code

choices: They call the model more than once
You define the control flow in code
They always emit structured output
They run all their steps at once
metadata.explanation: A workflow is a graph you draw; an agent is a loop the model drives. Here you wired the edges before runtime — the model only fills each node. Multiple calls and structured output aren't the distinction.

cards.prompt: Compared with a single prompt, what does prompt-chaining primarily trade away?
cards.answer: Added latency from serial steps

choices: Added latency from serial steps
All ability to return valid JSON
Lower accuracy on each subtask
Higher token cost per single call
metadata.explanation: Chaining runs steps in series, so latency stacks — that's the trade for higher accuracy on decomposable tasks. It doesn't lose JSON or accuracy; per-call cost is roughly unchanged.

cards.prompt: A coding task changes a different, unpredictable set of files each run — you cannot list the subtasks ahead of time. Best pattern?
cards.answer: Orchestrator-workers, subtasks at runtime

choices: Route to a fixed set of file handlers
Orchestrator-workers, subtasks at runtime
Parallelize a known list of file edits
Chain the file edits in a fixed order
metadata.explanation: Subtasks unknowable in advance → the orchestrator must generate them per input. Routing and parallel both assume a fixed, pre-known set of paths; a chain assumes a fixed order.

cards.prompt: A translation misses nuance first pass, a critic can name what to fix, and quality clearly improves with revision. Best pattern?
cards.answer: Evaluator-optimizer, loop until it passes

choices: Chain: translate, then polish, then ship
Evaluator-optimizer, loop until it passes
Parallel: translate three ways at once
Route to a specialist translator prompt
metadata.explanation: A clear quality signal plus gains from revision is the exact fit for evaluator-optimizer: generate, judge, refine, repeat until PASS. A chain runs a fixed number of steps and never loops back on a critique.

cards.prompt: Your evaluator-optimizer loop occasionally runs forever in production. Root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Add a maximum-iteration cap to the loop

choices: Switch the generator to a bigger model
Make the evaluator grade more leniently
Add a maximum-iteration cap to the loop
Retry the whole loop from the beginning
metadata.explanation: The cookbook exits only on PASS, so a generator that never satisfies the judge loops forever. A max-iteration cap is the structural fix; a bigger model or lenient judge just masks a missing stop guard.

cards.prompt: You want evaluator-optimizer but cannot clearly state what a 'good' output is. What does that tell you?
cards.answer: The pattern won't help — no quality signal

choices: Use a stronger model as the evaluator
The pattern won't help — no quality signal
Add more workers to the orchestrator step
Run more iterations to make up for it
metadata.explanation: The pattern depends on the evaluator applying real criteria. No articulable standard of 'good' means the judge can't judge and the loop is theater — no model size or iteration count rescues it.

cards.prompt: What actually separates orchestrator-workers from parallelization?
cards.answer: The orchestrator decides the subtasks itself

choices: The orchestrator decides the subtasks itself
Parallelization is always the faster option
The orchestrator is capped at two workers
Parallelization needs an evaluator to merge
metadata.explanation: Parallelization runs work you already divided; the orchestrator divides it for you at runtime, per input. That runtime decomposition — not speed, worker count, or merging — is the distinction.

cards.prompt: You're building a material designer app: users upload part images and get a recommendation from six material types. Your single prompt embeds detailed criteria for all six materials, and results are inconsistent — Claude seems to under-apply some criteria. What is the best fix?
cards.answer: Split into parallel requests, each evaluating the part against one material's specialized criteria, then aggregate the analyses in a final step

choices: Split into parallel requests, each evaluating the part against one material's specialized criteria, then aggregate the analyses in a final step
Add an instruction telling Claude to be thorough and consider every material's criteria equally before answering
Increase max_tokens so Claude has enough room to reason through all six materials' criteria in one response
Ask Claude to think step-by-step through the six materials in a fixed order within the same single request
metadata.explanation: Cramming all criteria into one request forces the model to juggle competing considerations at once. Parallelization gives each material a focused evaluation and an aggregation step makes the final call; 'be thorough' instructions and more tokens are band-aids on the overloaded single prompt.

cards.prompt: A teammate sketches a parallelization workflow on the whiteboard. Which sequence correctly describes the pattern?
cards.answer: Split one task into focused sub-tasks, run the sub-tasks simultaneously, then aggregate their results into a final decision

choices: Split one task into focused sub-tasks, run the sub-tasks simultaneously, then aggregate their results into a final decision
Run the full task once, ask a second model instance to critique it, then run the full task again with the critique
Classify the input first, then route it to exactly one specialized prompt that produces the final answer
Chain focused prompts sequentially so each step's output becomes the next step's input until a final answer emerges
metadata.explanation: Parallelization is split → run in parallel → aggregate. The routing option describes a routing workflow, the sequential option describes prompt chaining, and critique-and-retry is an evaluator-optimizer loop — all different patterns.

cards.prompt: While implementing parallel material evaluations, a developer insists every parallel branch must use an identical prompt so results stay comparable. What is the correct guidance?
cards.answer: Parallel sub-tasks may each use a specialized prompt, tool set, or evaluation criteria — they only need to contribute a distinct analysis the aggregator can compare

choices: Parallel sub-tasks may each use a specialized prompt, tool set, or evaluation criteria — they only need to contribute a distinct analysis the aggregator can compare
All branches must share one prompt template, varying only a material-name placeholder, or the aggregation step becomes invalid
Branches must be identical and any specialization should instead be moved into the final aggregation prompt
Specialized branches are only allowed when each branch also uses a different model to avoid shared bias
metadata.explanation: The sub-tasks in a parallelization workflow don't need to be identical — specialization per branch is the point. The aggregator compares the independent analyses; it doesn't require uniform prompts.

cards.prompt: In the material-designer example, why does evaluating one material per request outperform one mega-prompt holding all six materials' criteria?
cards.answer: Each request concentrates the model's attention on one aspect, avoiding the competing considerations that degrade analysis in a combined prompt

choices: Each request concentrates the model's attention on one aspect, avoiding the competing considerations that degrade analysis in a combined prompt
Splitting the work reduces total token usage because the criteria are shorter in each request
Parallel requests are processed by faster model instances, so each analysis gets more compute
It avoids context-window limits, which are the main reason mega-prompts fail for this task
metadata.explanation: The benefit is focused attention: one specialized evaluation per request reduces the cognitive load of balancing many criteria simultaneously. Total tokens actually go up across parallel calls, and the mega-prompt's problem here is attention, not the context limit or compute speed.

cards.prompt: Your parallelized material workflow performs well except the metal evaluation, which keeps overrating corrosion-prone parts. What does the parallel structure let you do?
