cards.answer: Refine and test the metal evaluation prompt independently, without touching the other materials' prompts

choices: Refine and test the metal evaluation prompt independently, without touching the other materials' prompts
Rewrite the aggregation prompt to down-weight the metal analysis whenever corrosion appears in the input
Regenerate all material prompts together so the criteria stay stylistically consistent across branches
Add a global instruction to every branch reminding the model to consider corrosion risk carefully
metadata.explanation: Independent optimization is a core benefit: each branch's prompt can be improved and tested in isolation. Down-weighting in the aggregator or adding global instructions papers over the one faulty branch instead of fixing it.

cards.prompt: Product asks you to add glass as a seventh material to the parallelized designer. What does this require?
cards.answer: Add one new parallel request with glass-specific criteria; existing branch prompts stay unchanged

choices: Add one new parallel request with glass-specific criteria; existing branch prompts stay unchanged
Rework the shared mega-prompt so glass criteria don't interfere with the other six materials' criteria
Re-tune every existing branch because adding a material changes the balance of the combined evaluation
Convert the workflow into a decision tree that eliminates materials one at a time to keep latency flat
metadata.explanation: Scalability is a key benefit of parallelization: new options are added as new independent branches without rewriting existing prompts or worrying about criteria interference. The other options reintroduce exactly the coupling the pattern removes.

cards.prompt: Which task is the best candidate for a parallelization workflow?
cards.answer: A decision that decomposes into independent evaluations across different domains of expertise, whose results are then compared

choices: A decision that decomposes into independent evaluations across different domains of expertise, whose results are then compared
A pipeline where each step's output is required as the input to the next step
A single-fact lookup that one small prompt already answers reliably
A task whose sub-steps must observe each other's intermediate reasoning to stay consistent
metadata.explanation: Parallelization fits when sub-tasks can operate independently and each contributes a distinct analysis. Sequentially dependent steps call for chaining, and tasks that are already simple gain nothing from the extra structure.

cards.prompt: All six material evaluations have returned in your parallelized workflow. What does the aggregation step do?
cards.answer: Sends the individual analyses back to the model with a request to compare them and produce the final material recommendation

choices: Sends the individual analyses back to the model with a request to compare them and produce the final material recommendation
Selects whichever branch produced the longest and most detailed analysis as the winner
Applies string-matching majority vote across the branch outputs without another model call
Returns the first branch response that arrived, since parallel workflows optimize for latency
metadata.explanation: Aggregation is itself a model step: the collected analyses are fed back to Claude to weigh and compare before the final recommendation. Length, arrival order, and naive voting ignore the content of the specialized analyses.

cards.prompt: A monorepo lead wants every engineer to inherit the same Bash permission allowlist the moment they clone the repo, with no manual setup per person. Which file should hold those settings?
cards.answer: .claude/settings.json, committed to the repo

choices: .claude/settings.json, committed to the repo
~/.claude/settings.json, synced via each engineer's dotfiles
.claude/settings.local.json, committed to the repo
A note in CLAUDE.md describing the desired permissions
metadata.explanation: Project-scoped settings.json is committed and shared automatically; settings.local.json is meant to be gitignored, and user settings don't travel with the repo clone.

cards.prompt: A contractor isn't following the team's 'always use pytest' convention even though it's documented, because they added the rule to ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md instead of the repo's memory file. Where should the rule move to fix this for everyone working in the repo?
cards.answer: ./CLAUDE.md at the project root, committed to the repo

choices: ./CLAUDE.md at the project root, committed to the repo
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, kept personal but synced via dotfiles
CLAUDE.local.md, gitignored in this repo
A pinned message at the start of every session
metadata.explanation: Project memory at ./CLAUDE.md is committed and shared with everyone in the repo; the user-level file only follows the individual who wrote it.

cards.prompt: A designer wrote a /changelog slash command they use for personal workflow only and don't want showing up for every engineer who clones the repo. Which location keeps it private to them but usable in any project?
cards.answer: ~/.claude/commands/changelog.md

choices: ~/.claude/commands/changelog.md
.claude/commands/changelog.md, committed
.claude/commands/changelog.local.md
Inside .mcp.json under a commands key
metadata.explanation: Slash commands only have project and personal scopes (no local variant); ~/.claude/commands is the personal, cross-project location.

cards.prompt: An engineer built a skill encoding a company-wide deployment checklist and wants it invocable by name in every repo they personally work in across the org, not just this one. Where does it belong?
cards.answer: ~/.claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md

choices: .claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md in this repo only
~/.claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md
.claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.local.md
A skills array inside .mcp.json
metadata.explanation: Skills follow the user-vs-project split like commands and agents; ~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md is personal and follows the engineer across all their projects.

cards.prompt: A team wants a 'database-migration-reviewer' subagent available to anyone who clones this specific repo, version-controlled alongside the code it reviews. Which path satisfies that?
cards.answer: .claude/agents/database-migration-reviewer.md, committed

choices: ~/.claude/agents/database-migration-reviewer.md, so it follows just this engineer
.claude/agents/database-migration-reviewer.md, committed
.claude/agents/database-migration-reviewer.local.md
A prose description inside CLAUDE.md
metadata.explanation: Subagents follow project/personal scope like commands and skills; the committed .claude/agents/*.md path ships with the repo for the whole team.

cards.prompt: One engineer wants a hook that pops a desktop notification on their own machine when a subagent finishes, without imposing it on teammates who share the repo. Where should this hook be configured?
cards.answer: .claude/settings.local.json's hooks section, gitignored

choices: .claude/settings.local.json's hooks section, gitignored
.claude/settings.json's hooks section, committed and shared with the whole team
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md as a written instruction
.mcp.json's hooks section
metadata.explanation: Hooks live inside settings.json files; settings.local.json is the gitignored, repo-scoped, personal-only variant, exactly matching 'just me, just here.'

cards.prompt: A new hire, expecting everything shared to live under .claude/, searches for the team's MCP server configuration in .claude/mcp.json and finds nothing there. Where does the shared, version-controlled MCP config actually live?
cards.answer: .mcp.json at the repository root, outside .claude/

choices: .claude/mcp/servers.json
.mcp.json at the repository root, outside .claude/
~/.claude.json, scoped to the project, since MCP config always defaults to a personal location
An mcpServers key inside .claude/settings.json
metadata.explanation: MCP is the named oddball in the scope map: the shared/project file is .mcp.json at repo root, not nested under .claude/ like everything else.

cards.prompt: A tech lead is teaching a new teammate the naming convention for config scope. Which summary matches the pattern used throughout the reference?
cards.answer: .claude/ in the repo is shared with the team, ~/.claude/ is personal and follows you everywhere, and *.local.* is this repo but private

choices: .claude/ in the repo is shared with the team, ~/.claude/ is personal and follows you everywhere, and *.local.* is this repo but private
.claude/ always means personal, and ~/.claude/ always means shared with the team
Any gitignored file automatically syncs to every teammate's machine the moment someone else runs git pull, regardless of directory
*.local.* files are committed but silently ignored by Claude Code at runtime
metadata.explanation: This is the stated pattern: repo .claude/ = shared, ~/.claude/ = personal across projects, *.local.* = this repo but gitignored/private.

cards.prompt: A developer finds a stale CLAUDE.local.md file sitting in an old feature branch and asks what scope it represents today. What's the accurate characterization?
cards.answer: A legacy local/gitignored project-memory file, scoped to this repo

choices: The current recommended way to store personal, repo-specific memory
A legacy local/gitignored project-memory file, scoped to this repo
The shared, committed project memory file that replaced CLAUDE.md
Equivalent to ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, applying across all projects
metadata.explanation: The scope map lists CLAUDE.local.md as the (legacy) local variant of project memory — repo-scoped, gitignored, and not the current primary mechanism.

cards.prompt: An enterprise admin has pushed a managed, system-level policy disabling a tool. A project's .claude/settings.local.json explicitly allows that same tool. What happens when it's invoked?
cards.answer: The managed policy wins — it overrides every other scope

choices: Claude asks the user to resolve the conflict
The local settings win, since local is most specific to the current work
The managed policy wins — it overrides every other scope
Whichever file was modified most recently wins
metadata.explanation: Managed/enterprise policy sits above Local, Project, and User in precedence and cannot be overridden by any of them.

cards.prompt: A repo's committed .claude/settings.json denies a Bash command, but one engineer's gitignored .claude/settings.local.json allows that same command. No managed policy is involved. Which setting applies for that engineer?
cards.answer: The local settings.local.json rule

choices: The local settings.local.json rule
The shared settings.json rule, since team-wide config always wins
Neither — Claude asks the engineer to confirm each time
Whichever rule appears first alphabetically in the merged config
metadata.explanation: Precedence is Managed > Local > Project > User — the counter-intuitive part is that the gitignored local file outranks the shared, committed project file.

cards.prompt: A contractor's personal ~/.claude/settings.json allows a tool that the project's committed .claude/settings.json denies, with no managed or local override present. Which one governs?
cards.answer: The project's settings.json, since project outranks user scope

choices: The project's settings.json, since project outranks user scope
The user's settings.json, since personal preferences take priority
Neither — Claude asks for confirmation since they conflict
The tool is allowed since at least one scope permits it
metadata.explanation: In the precedence order Managed > Local > Project > User, project settings beat user settings whenever there's no local or managed override in play.

cards.prompt: Ranking from highest to lowest precedence, which ordering of Managed, Local project, Project (shared), and User settings is correct?
cards.answer: Managed > Local project > Project (shared) > User

choices: Managed > Local project > Project (shared) > User
Managed > Project (shared) > Local project > User
Local project > Managed > Project (shared) > User
User > Project (shared) > Local project > Managed
metadata.explanation: The documented order is Managed > Local > Project > User; local project settings rank second, above the shared project file and user settings.

cards.prompt: A platform team asks which single scope truly cannot be overridden by anything else, including a developer's own local settings file. What's the answer?
cards.answer: Managed/enterprise policy, set at the system level

choices: .claude/settings.local.json, since local settings are evaluated last and always take final say
Managed/enterprise policy, set at the system level
.claude/settings.json
~/.claude/settings.json
metadata.explanation: Managed policy (plist/registry-level) is described as beating everything, with no scope able to override it.

cards.prompt: A junior engineer reasons: 'the team's shared settings.json denies rm -rf, but my personal ~/.claude/settings.json allows it — since these conflict, Claude must not know which I meant, so it'll just ask me before running the command.' Is this reasoning correct?
cards.answer: No — the action is denied outright: deny wins over allow, and project scope also outranks user scope, so there's no fallback to asking

choices: No — the action is denied outright: deny wins over allow, and project scope also outranks user scope, so there's no fallback to asking
Yes — whenever two scopes disagree about a rule, Claude is designed to pause the run and explicitly ask the user which one should apply
No — the action is allowed, because personal preferences override team defaults
Yes, but only when the conflicting rule involves a destructive command like rm
metadata.explanation: This is a named exam trap: resolution is deterministic, not a fallback to asking. Deny beats allow, and project beats user, so the command is simply denied.

cards.prompt: Which statement correctly describes what Local project scope (.claude/settings.local.json) beats and loses to in the precedence hierarchy?
cards.answer: It beats Project (shared) and User settings, but loses to Managed policy

choices: It beats Project (shared) and User settings, but loses to Managed policy
It beats only User settings, but loses to Project (shared) settings, which is why teams are cautioned against relying on it
It beats Managed policy but loses to Project (shared) settings
It beats nothing; all scopes are merged with equal weight
metadata.explanation: Local project ranks second overall: above Project and User, but below Managed, which nothing can override.

cards.prompt: A repo's permission config has a broad Bash(rm:*) rule set to deny, plus a narrower Bash(rm -rf ./tmp/*:*) rule set to allow, meant to let a cleanup script run. When that script executes rm -rf ./tmp/cache, what happens?
cards.answer: The command is denied — a broad deny beats a narrower allow that also matches

choices: The command is denied — a broad deny beats a narrower allow that also matches
The command is allowed, since the narrower tmp-directory rule is treated as a more precise, and therefore winning, match
Claude asks for confirmation, since two rules match the same command
The command is allowed in CI runs but denied on local machines
metadata.explanation: The reference is explicit: a broad deny beats a narrower allow that also matches, regardless of how specific the allow rule is.

cards.prompt: When a tool call matches multiple permission rules across the deny, ask, and allow categories, in what order does Claude Code evaluate them?
cards.answer: deny, then ask, then allow — first match wins

choices: allow, then ask, then deny — last match wins
deny, then ask, then allow — first match wins
ask, then deny, then allow — most specific match wins
Rules are evaluated alphabetically by pattern
metadata.explanation: Rules evaluate in the order deny -> ask -> allow, with the first match winning.

cards.prompt: A team wants git status commands to run without ever prompting, but wants a confirmation prompt before any git push, even though a broader rule would otherwise permit it silently. Which combination of permission types achieves this?
cards.answer: allow for Bash(git status:*), and ask for Bash(git push:*)

choices: allow for Bash(git status:*), and ask for Bash(git push:*)
allow for both commands, trusting Claude's judgment on push
deny for Bash(git push:*), and allow for Bash(git status:*)
ask for both git status and git push, to be safe
metadata.explanation: allow pre-approves so Claude never asks; ask forces a confirmation even when a broader rule would otherwise permit the action silently. Denying push would block it entirely, not just require confirmation.

cards.prompt: A security-conscious lead wants to guarantee Claude can never read the repo's .env file, no matter what allow rules exist elsewhere in project or user settings. Which permission type accomplishes that?
cards.answer: deny for Read(./.env)

choices: deny for Read(./.env)
ask for Read(./.env), so a human confirms each read
allow for Read(./.env), with a comment warning against misuse
Remove .env from the repo so Claude can't see it
metadata.explanation: deny is described as a hard block that overrides any allow rule — the exact tool for guaranteeing secrets can never be read regardless of other scopes.

cards.prompt: Which rule-shape syntax correctly scopes a permission entry to only npm test invocations?
cards.answer: Bash(npm run test:*)

choices: npm(test:*)
Bash(npm run test:*)
Tool:Bash=npm-test-only, a shorthand a few teams mistakenly expect to work
allow-bash npm test
metadata.explanation: The documented rule shape is Tool(specifier:pattern), matching examples like Bash(npm run test:*).

cards.prompt: A team lead wants Claude to edit files and run safe commands without prompting for each one during a session, while still reviewing a diff before anything is committed. Which permission mode best fits?
cards.answer: acceptEdits, which allows edits without per-file prompts

choices: bypassPermissions, since it lets everything through fastest
acceptEdits, which allows edits without per-file prompts
plan mode, which never lets Claude touch files
default mode, which behaves identically to acceptEdits
metadata.explanation: acceptEdits is the mode built for exactly this: edits proceed without per-file confirmation while other actions still follow normal permission rules, unlike bypassPermissions which skips checks entirely.

cards.prompt: An engineer sets permission mode to bypassPermissions on a shared CI runner just to 'stop the constant prompts.' What's the concern with that choice?
cards.answer: It skips all permission checks, so deny rules protecting secrets and prod stop applying too

choices: There's no concern; bypassPermissions is the recommended CI mode
It skips all permission checks, so deny rules protecting secrets and prod stop applying too
It only affects Read-type tools, not Bash or Write, so it's considered safe for most CI pipelines
It requires a managed policy to be active first, so it will silently no-op
metadata.explanation: bypassPermissions is flagged as dangerous precisely because it skips all checks — including the deny rules meant to protect secrets and production resources.

cards.prompt: A project-level deny rule for Bash(rm:*) and a user-level allow rule for the same pattern both exist, with no managed or local override. Which rule governs, and why?
cards.answer: The project-level deny, reinforced by both deny-over-allow evaluation and project-over-user scope precedence

choices: The project-level deny, reinforced by both deny-over-allow evaluation and project-over-user scope precedence
The user-level allow, since user settings are most specific to the individual
Neither applies; Claude asks the user to pick one interactively
Both apply simultaneously, splitting behavior by read versus write
metadata.explanation: Two independent rules point the same direction here: deny beats allow in evaluation order, and project scope beats user scope in precedence.

cards.prompt: A team wants to guarantee Claude can never modify a specific config file, even if the model 'forgets' an instruction mid-session. Which hook event should this be wired to, and why?
cards.answer: PreToolUse, because it fires before the tool runs and can block the action outright

choices: PostToolUse, because it can inspect the completed write and automatically revert it after the fact
PreToolUse, because it fires before the tool runs and can block the action outright
SessionStart, because it can remind Claude not to touch the file
Notification, because it can alert the team when the file changes
metadata.explanation: PreToolUse is the one event that fires before the tool executes and can block it (exit code 2 or a JSON permissionDecision of deny), making the guarantee real rather than best-effort.

cards.prompt: A hook author writes a PostToolUse hook that detects any change Claude makes to .env and restores the original from git. Why is this weaker than using a PreToolUse hook for the same goal?
cards.answer: PostToolUse fires after the tool already ran, so the write already happened — it's reacting, not preventing

choices: PostToolUse hooks cannot access the file system at all
PostToolUse fires after the tool already ran, so the write already happened — it's reacting, not preventing
PostToolUse hooks are scoped only to Read-type tool calls and never fire for Write, Edit, or similar operations
There's no real difference; both approaches are equally reliable
metadata.explanation: The reference calls this out directly: to hard-block .env edits, use a PreToolUse hook; a PostToolUse 'revert after' is a band-aid because the write already happened by the time it fires.

cards.prompt: Which hook event fires when the user submits a prompt, and is capable of rejecting or modifying that prompt before Claude ever sees it?
cards.answer: UserPromptSubmit

choices: PreToolUse
SessionStart, which only fires once at the very beginning of a session
UserPromptSubmit
Stop
metadata.explanation: UserPromptSubmit fires on prompt submission and can reject or modify it, per the reference table.

cards.prompt: A team wants to inject fresh context, like the current git branch and open PR list, at the very start of every session. Which hook event is designed for this, and can it block anything?
cards.answer: SessionStart — it fires when the session begins but cannot block anything

choices: SessionStart — it fires when the session begins but cannot block anything
PreToolUse — it fires before tools run and can block execution
PreCompact — it fires before compaction and can block context loss
Stop — it fires when the agent finishes a turn and can force continuation
metadata.explanation: SessionStart is used to inject context at session start and is marked as unable to block, unlike PreToolUse or Stop.

cards.prompt: An engineer wants a hook that can force the main agent to keep working instead of ending its turn prematurely. Which event supports that?
cards.answer: Stop / SubagentStop, since it fires when a turn ends and can force continuation

choices: PostToolUse, since it runs after every tool call
Notification, since it can interrupt with a message
Stop / SubagentStop, since it fires when a turn ends and can force continuation
UserPromptSubmit, since it intercepts before the prompt is processed
metadata.explanation: Stop/SubagentStop fires when the main or sub agent ends a turn and is the event marked as able to force continuation.

cards.prompt: Per the hook reference table, which pair of events fires purely to notify or react, with no ability to block or modify anything?
cards.answer: Notification and PreCompact

choices: PreToolUse and UserPromptSubmit
PostToolUse and Stop
Notification and PreCompact
SessionStart and PreToolUse
metadata.explanation: The reference groups Notification and PreCompact together in one row, both marked 'no' for blocking capability.

cards.prompt: What two concrete mechanisms let a PreToolUse hook actually block a tool call, per the reference?
cards.answer: Exiting with code 2, or emitting JSON with "permissionDecision": "deny"

choices: Returning an HTTP 403 status code, or setting a special CLAUDE_BLOCK environment variable
Exiting with code 2, or emitting JSON with "permissionDecision": "deny"
Writing to stderr, or renaming the target file
Raising a Python exception, or calling sys.exit(0)
metadata.explanation: The block mechanism is explicitly documented as exit code 2 or JSON output containing "permissionDecision": "deny".

cards.prompt: A team debates where hook definitions actually live in the config layout. Per the reference, where are hooks configured?
cards.answer: Inside settings.json files — project, user, or local

choices: In a dedicated .claude/hooks.json file, separate from settings
Inside settings.json files — project, user, or local
Inside CLAUDE.md, as a special 'hooks:' frontmatter block
Inside .mcp.json, alongside MCP server definitions
metadata.explanation: Hooks are configured in settings.json, which is why the scope map lists hooks as 'in .claude/settings.json' / '~/.claude/settings.json' / 'settings.local.json' rather than a separate file type.

cards.prompt: A team wants a /release slash command every engineer gets automatically right after cloning the repo. Where should the file live?
cards.answer: .claude/commands/release.md, committed to the repo

choices: ~/.claude/commands/release.md, since committing doesn't matter for personal files
.claude/commands/release.md, committed to the repo
.claude/commands/release.local.md, gitignored per engineer
Inside .mcp.json under a commands section
metadata.explanation: Project command scope is .claude/commands/name.md — committed, so the whole team gets it automatically on clone.

cards.prompt: A slash command author wants to grab the entire string of arguments the user typed after the command name as one blob, rather than splitting it into pieces. Which variable does that?
cards.answer: $ARGUMENTS

choices: $ARGS[] (a JavaScript-style array some authors expect)
$ARGUMENTS
$INPUT
metadata.explanation: $ARGUMENTS represents the whole argument string, while $1, $2, etc. are the positional pieces.

cards.prompt: An author wants their slash command to accept two separate positional values, like /deploy staging v2, and reference them independently in the command body. Which syntax does that?
cards.answer: $1 and $2

choices: $ARGUMENTS, split by a custom parser the author writes
$1 and $2
{{arg1}} and {{arg2}}
process.argv[1] and process.argv[2]
metadata.explanation: $1, $2 are the documented positional-argument variables, distinct from $ARGUMENTS which is the whole string.

cards.prompt: Which frontmatter fields does the reference list as available on a slash command, for restricting tool access, describing it, hinting at expected arguments, and pinning a model?
cards.answer: allowed-tools, description, argument-hint, model

choices: allowed-tools, description, argument-hint, model
tools, summary, usage, engine
permissions, help, args, llm, and a required owner field for attribution
scope, name, params, provider
metadata.explanation: The reference names allowed-tools, description, argument-hint, and model as the slash command frontmatter fields.

cards.prompt: A developer asks how skills structurally differ from slash commands, beyond both having a project/personal scope split. What's the key difference described in the reference?
cards.answer: Skills are a folder with supporting files, invoked by the model based on description rather than typed explicitly

choices: Skills have no frontmatter at all, while commands require it
Skills are a folder with supporting files, invoked by the model based on description rather than typed explicitly
Skills can only ever be personal-scoped, never shared with a team
Skills replace CLAUDE.md entirely and can't coexist with it
metadata.explanation: Skills live at .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md with supporting files and are described as 'model-invocable by description' — the richer evolution of the single-file slash command.

cards.prompt: A teammate insists skills can only live under ~/.claude/skills/ and can never be checked into a repo for the whole team to share. Is that accurate?
cards.answer: No — skills use the same project/personal split as commands: .claude/skills/ for shared, ~/.claude/skills/ for personal

choices: Yes, skills are always personal by design
No — skills use the same project/personal split as commands: .claude/skills/ for shared, ~/.claude/skills/ for personal
No, skills can only be project-scoped, never personal
Yes, but only if the team has disabled MCP servers first
metadata.explanation: The reference states skills have 'the same project/personal scope split' as commands, so a shared, committed version is entirely possible.

cards.prompt: A team wants a Postgres MCP server configuration checked into the repo so no one has to set it up by hand after cloning. Which scope and file achieve that?
cards.answer: project scope, stored in .mcp.json at the repo root

choices: local scope, stored in each engineer's ~/.claude.json
project scope, stored in .mcp.json at the repo root
user scope, stored in ~/.claude.json globally
An mcpServers key inside .claude/settings.json
metadata.explanation: Project scope is the shared, committed MCP scope, and it lives in .mcp.json at the repo root — the file this whole team needs.

cards.prompt: An engineer runs claude mcp add --scope local for a new MCP server. Where does that configuration get stored, and is it shared with teammates?
cards.answer: In ~/.claude.json, scoped to this project; no, it's just for this engineer, here

choices: In .mcp.json at the repo root; yes, since it's committed
In ~/.claude.json, scoped to this project; no, it's just for this engineer, here
In ~/.claude/settings.json; yes, because user settings auto-sync
In .claude/mcp/local.json; no, but it applies across all their repos
metadata.explanation: Local MCP scope stores in ~/.claude.json but scoped to the current project, and is explicitly not shared — just this engineer, just this repo.

cards.prompt: What distinguishes MCP 'user' scope from MCP 'local' scope, given both are stored in ~/.claude.json?
cards.answer: User scope applies across all of the engineer's projects; local scope is scoped to just the current project

choices: User scope applies across all of the engineer's projects; local scope is scoped to just the current project
User scope is shared with the team; local scope is not
There's no real difference; they're aliases for the same behavior
User scope requires a managed policy to be active; local scope does not
metadata.explanation: Both write to ~/.claude.json, but user scope is global across all the engineer's projects while local scope is pinned to the current project only.

cards.prompt: A new hire searches for the shared MCP server list under .claude/, the way they would for skills or subagents, and comes up empty. What should they be told about MCP's location?
cards.answer: The shared MCP file, .mcp.json, sits at the repo root, not inside .claude/

choices: MCP configuration doesn't support a team-shared scope at all
The shared MCP file, .mcp.json, sits at the repo root, not inside .claude/
MCP servers are only ever configured via environment variables
They should look in ~/.claude/mcp.json, the correct shared location
metadata.explanation: MCP is the reference's one named oddball: unlike commands, skills, and agents, its team-shared file lives outside .claude/ entirely, at the repo root.

cards.prompt: Which claude mcp add flag determines whether a new MCP server ends up in the committed .mcp.json versus the personal ~/.claude.json?
cards.answer: --scope project|user|local

choices: --shared vs --private
--scope project|user|local
--team vs --solo
--commit
metadata.explanation: MCP servers are added with claude mcp add --scope project|user|local, and only project scope routes into the committed .mcp.json.

cards.prompt: A contractor argues that since MCP servers are configured via claude mcp add, scope doesn't matter because Claude Code syncs everything to the team automatically. What's wrong with that reasoning?
cards.answer: Scope matters: only project scope writes to the committed .mcp.json; local and user scope stay in the personal ~/.claude.json and are never shared

choices: Nothing — all MCP scopes sync automatically to every collaborator
Scope matters: only project scope writes to the committed .mcp.json; local and user scope stay in the personal ~/.claude.json and are never shared
MCP servers can never be shared with a team under any scope
They should add the server twice, once per scope, to cover all teammates
metadata.explanation: Sharing an MCP server with the team requires deliberately choosing --scope project; the other two scopes are personal by design and never propagate.

cards.prompt: A developer notices their main conversation's context filling up with dozens of grep and file-read results from an exploratory search, degrading later responses. What's the config-level fix?
cards.answer: Delegate the exploration to a subagent, which runs in its own context window

choices: Delegate the exploration to a subagent, which runs in its own context window
Write a much longer CLAUDE.md instructing Claude to summarize its own searches
Manually clear context every few messages during the search
Switch permission mode to plan mode to reduce tool calls
metadata.explanation: Subagents run in their own context window and return just a summary to the main thread — the reference's stated fix for 'main context filling with grep/read noise.'

cards.prompt: A teammate proposes fixing 'main context bloating with search noise' by expanding CLAUDE.md with instructions telling Claude to narrate searches more concisely. Why does the reference treat this as the band-aid rather than the fix?
cards.answer: CLAUDE.md instructions can't purge tokens already in the transcript — it's guidance, not a structural context isolation

choices: CLAUDE.md instructions can't purge tokens already in the transcript — it's guidance, not a structural context isolation
CLAUDE.md is only loaded once per day, so the instruction wouldn't take effect in time
CLAUDE.md cannot contain instructions about tool usage at all
Bigger CLAUDE.md files are actually the reference's recommended fix
metadata.explanation: The reference explicitly names 'bigger CLAUDE.md' as not the fix, since it's just instructions and can't remove tokens already accumulated in the transcript.

cards.prompt: Which subagent frontmatter fields are required, and which are optional, per the reference?
cards.answer: Required: name, description; optional: tools, model

choices: Required: name, description; optional: tools, model
Required: tools, model; optional: name, description
All four fields are required with no optional fields
Only description is required; name is also optional
metadata.explanation: The reference states name and description are required frontmatter, while tools and model are optional.

cards.prompt: A team wants a project-specific 'sql-query-reviewer' subagent available to everyone cloning the repo, plus a personal 'commit-message-polisher' subagent only one engineer uses across all their repos. Where should each live?
cards.answer: sql-query-reviewer in .claude/agents/; commit-message-polisher in ~/.claude/agents/

choices: Both in ~/.claude/agents/, since subagents are always personal
sql-query-reviewer in .claude/agents/; commit-message-polisher in ~/.claude/agents/
sql-query-reviewer in ~/.claude/agents/; commit-message-polisher in .claude/agents/
Both inside .mcp.json, since subagents are treated as MCP tools
metadata.explanation: Project subagents (.claude/agents/) are for the shared, committed reviewer; personal subagents (~/.claude/agents/) follow one engineer across every repo.

cards.prompt: What's described as 'the whole point' of a subagent running in its own context window with its own system prompt and tool access?
cards.answer: It isolates noisy intermediate work and returns only a distilled summary to the main thread

choices: It runs faster than the main agent because it defaults to a smaller model
It isolates noisy intermediate work and returns only a distilled summary to the main thread
It bypasses every permission check configured for the main agent
It automatically inherits the main agent's entire conversation history
metadata.explanation: The reference states the whole point is that each subagent has its own context and tools and 'returns just a summary to the main thread.'

cards.prompt: A lead claims 'subagents are basically just a way to give a task a different model.' Is that an accurate description of why they exist, per the reference?
cards.answer: No — the stated purpose is context isolation, with model as just an optional field

choices: Yes, model selection is the primary and only stated purpose
No — the stated purpose is context isolation, with model as just an optional field
Yes, and subagents also replace the need for any permission configuration
No, subagents exist solely to enable MCP server access the main agent lacks
metadata.explanation: Model is listed as an optional frontmatter field, but the reference frames the core purpose as separate context/tools that isolate noise and return a summary, not model swapping.

cards.prompt: A solo dev dumps an entire API docs folder into every prompt "just to be safe" and notices Claude's suggestions get worse, not better. What's the root cause and best fix?
cards.answer: Irrelevant context dilutes Claude's attention and decreases performance; curate only the files relevant to the current task instead of including everything.

choices: Switch to a model with a larger context window so all the docs fit comfortably.
Irrelevant context dilutes Claude's attention and decreases performance; curate only the files relevant to the current task instead of including everything.
Split the docs into multiple messages so each request stays under the token limit.
Ask Claude to summarize the docs first, then paste that summary into every request.
metadata.explanation: Too much irrelevant context actively decreases performance, it doesn't just cost tokens. The fix is guiding Claude to relevant files, not dumping everything and hoping it filters.

cards.prompt: A team lead notices that after teammates started attaching the entire design-system folder to every Claude Code session "to be thorough," suggestions started ignoring obvious project conventions. What's the root cause and fix?
cards.answer: Too much irrelevant context decreases performance, not just wastes tokens; guide Claude to the specific files needed for the task rather than the whole folder.

choices: The design-system folder is stale and needs to be regenerated before use.
Claude Code has a hidden context ceiling that silently drops the oldest files once a session grows large, so reorder the attachments to put the newest ones first before sending.
Too much irrelevant context decreases performance, not just wastes tokens; guide Claude to the specific files needed for the task rather than the whole folder.
Enable extended thinking mode so Claude can process the larger context more carefully.
metadata.explanation: The framing is explicit: too much irrelevant context decreases performance. Attaching an entire folder buries the relevant files rather than helping Claude reach them.

cards.prompt: A new teammate pastes the entire git log and every open file's contents into their first Claude Code prompt of the day, then complains answers feel generic and slow. What should they do instead?
cards.answer: Include only the specific files and context relevant to the current task, since more context isn't automatically better and can decrease performance.

choices: Run /init first so Claude re-learns the whole repo before every session.
Compress the git log with a summarizer tool before pasting it in.
Increase the max_tokens setting and enable a larger context window so Claude has more room to reason through the full git log and every open file without truncation.
Include only the specific files and context relevant to the current task, since more context isn't automatically better and can decrease performance.
metadata.explanation: Context management means guiding Claude toward the right files, not dumping everything. Pasting logs and unrelated open files is the exact anti-pattern the framing warns against.

cards.prompt: A CI script feeds Claude Code the full contents of every file changed in the last 30 days as background for a code review task, and review quality drops noticeably. What's the fix?
cards.answer: Guide Claude to only the files actually relevant to the review, since irrelevant context degrades output quality.

choices: Run the review twice and merge the two outputs for better coverage.
Increase the CI job's timeout so Claude has more time to process everything.
Add a system prompt telling Claude to "focus only on the important parts" while still attaching all 30 days of unrelated file history unchanged.
Guide Claude to only the files actually relevant to the review, since irrelevant context degrades output quality.
metadata.explanation: The unrelated 30-day history is irrelevant context, and irrelevant context decreases performance regardless of how it's framed to Claude. Scoping the input is the actual fix.

cards.prompt: A solo developer starting a brand-new repo wants Claude Code to have a baseline understanding of the project's architecture and commands, without hand-writing that documentation. What should they run?
cards.answer: /init, which analyzes the codebase and writes a summary to CLAUDE.md.

choices: /init, which analyzes the codebase and writes a summary to CLAUDE.md.
/memory, which opens a blank file for manual notes.
@path on the repo root to include every file in the next prompt.
CLAUDE.local.md, since Claude auto-populates personal files on first use.
metadata.explanation: /init analyzes the whole codebase — purpose, architecture, key commands, coding patterns — and writes the resulting summary into CLAUDE.md.

cards.prompt: A team lead onboarding a new project to Claude Code wants an automated first pass at documenting purpose, architecture, key commands, and coding patterns, before anyone hand-writes anything. Which command produces that?
cards.answer: /init, which analyzes the whole codebase and writes the summary to CLAUDE.md.

choices: /memory, which opens CLAUDE.md for editing but performs no analysis.
@init as an inline file mention placed in the first prompt.
/init, which analyzes the whole codebase and writes the summary to CLAUDE.md.
A shell script that greps for README files and concatenates them.
metadata.explanation: This is exactly what /init does: it analyzes the codebase and writes the resulting summary to CLAUDE.md. /memory only opens an existing file for editing.

cards.prompt: A maintainer wants to regenerate a project's CLAUDE.md from scratch after a major refactor, capturing the new architecture and commands automatically rather than editing it by hand. What accomplishes that?
cards.answer: Re-running /init, which re-analyzes the codebase and rewrites the CLAUDE.md summary.

choices: Deleting CLAUDE.md and waiting for Claude Code to recreate it automatically on the next request.
Re-running /init, which re-analyzes the codebase and rewrites the CLAUDE.md summary.
Running /memory, since it regenerates the file from the current codebase state.
Editing CLAUDE.local.md, which syncs its contents back into CLAUDE.md on save.
metadata.explanation: /init is the analysis-and-write command; running it again re-scans the refactored codebase and produces a fresh CLAUDE.md summary. /memory just opens the file, it doesn't analyze anything.

cards.prompt: A new teammate joins a project with no CLAUDE.md yet and asks how Claude Code would typically produce a first draft covering the project's key commands and file layout. What's the answer?
cards.answer: Run /init, which analyzes the codebase and writes that summary into CLAUDE.md.

choices: Manually write CLAUDE.local.md first, since /init only edits files that already exist.
Use @AGENTS.md to import an existing agent config as a starting point.
Start a conversation and ask Claude to "remember" the codebase for future sessions.
Run /init, which analyzes the codebase and writes that summary into CLAUDE.md.
metadata.explanation: /init is designed for exactly this: a first automated pass over an undocumented codebase that produces the initial CLAUDE.md.

cards.prompt: While running /init on a large monorepo, a solo dev is interrupted by a write-permission prompt for every file and wants Claude to write freely through the rest of the codebase without stopping each time, just for this session. What should they do?
cards.answer: Press Shift+Tab to enter accept-edits mode, letting Claude write freely for the rest of the session.

choices: Press Enter repeatedly until all prompts clear, since that's the only way to batch-approve.
Cancel /init and hand-write CLAUDE.md instead, to avoid the prompts entirely.
Press Shift+Tab to enter accept-edits mode, letting Claude write freely for the rest of the session.
Set USE_SQLITE=1 in .env, which also disables write-confirmation prompts.
metadata.explanation: Shift+Tab switches into accept-edits mode so writes are approved automatically for the session, while Enter only approves the single write currently being confirmed.

cards.prompt: A team lead reviewing a junior engineer's /init session sees they pressed Enter on each individual write-permission prompt instead of using the accept-edits shortcut. What did that choice actually do?
cards.answer: It approved each file write one at a time, giving fine-grained control rather than letting Claude write freely.

choices: It silently discarded any write Claude proposed after the first one, requiring the engineer to restart /init from scratch to recover the lost files.
It saved the changes to CLAUDE.local.md instead of CLAUDE.md.
It disabled /init for the remainder of the session.
It approved each file write one at a time, giving fine-grained control rather than letting Claude write freely.
metadata.explanation: Enter approves the current write only; it neither discards further writes nor changes which file gets updated. Shift+Tab is the shortcut for approving everything at once.

cards.prompt: A CI script that shells out to Claude Code's /init in a fresh container hangs, apparently waiting on a per-file write-confirmation prompt with no human present to answer it. What's the fix for unattended runs like this?
cards.answer: Trigger accept-edits mode (the Shift+Tab behavior) so writes are approved automatically instead of waiting on a per-file prompt.

choices: Add a longer timeout so the CI job waits until someone approves manually.
Switch the CI job to use CLAUDE.local.md instead of CLAUDE.md, since that personal file is documented as skipping write confirmation entirely in headless environments.
Pipe "y" into stdin repeatedly to satisfy the picker prompt.
Trigger accept-edits mode (the Shift+Tab behavior) so writes are approved automatically instead of waiting on a per-file prompt.
metadata.explanation: The hang is caused by the per-write confirmation gate; accept-edits mode is the built-in way to let Claude write freely without a human approving each file, which is what an unattended job needs.

cards.prompt: A new teammate is confused about pressing Enter versus Shift+Tab during /init's write prompts, and asks which one lets Claude write every remaining file without asking again. What's the correct explanation?
cards.answer: Shift+Tab switches into accept-edits mode, approving all subsequent writes for the session; Enter only approves the single file currently being confirmed.

choices: Enter approves all remaining files at once for the rest of the entire session, while Shift+Tab only steps through and approves the single current one each time it appears.

metadata.explanation: This is the intended reuse pattern: @AGENTS.md on the first line pulls in the shared file, and the bullet points below add Claude-specific instructions on top of it.

cards.prompt: A CI maintainer wants Claude Code sessions to inherit the same conventions already encoded in AGENTS.md for a different tool, but is worried about keeping two files in sync as the project evolves. What single-line fix addresses this?
cards.answer: Adding @AGENTS.md as the first line of CLAUDE.md, so Claude always loads the shared file directly instead of a copy that can drift out of date.

choices: Adding a comment at the top of AGENTS.md noting that CLAUDE.md is a duplicate that must be updated in tandem.
Scheduling a nightly job that diffs the two files and alerts on mismatch.
Adding @AGENTS.md as the first line of CLAUDE.md, so Claude always loads the shared file directly instead of a copy that can drift out of date.
Renaming both files to a shared CONVENTIONS.md and hoping both tools pick it up.
metadata.explanation: The @AGENTS.md first-line pattern is a one-time wiring change that removes the possibility of drift entirely, rather than adding process (diffing, comments) to manage two copies.

cards.prompt: To shrink a bloated CLAUDE.md, a solo dev moves most of its content into a separate 600-line doc and adds a single @-mention line pointing to it, expecting the per-turn cost to drop. Does this work?
cards.answer: No, the @-mentioned doc is still auto-included in every request, so the per-turn context tax is unchanged; loaded is loaded regardless of which file the content lives in.

choices: Yes, @-mentioning a file defers loading until Claude actually needs it, so idle turns get cheaper.
No, the @-mentioned doc is still auto-included in every request, so the per-turn context tax is unchanged; loaded is loaded regardless of which file the content lives in.
Yes, moving content out of CLAUDE.md itself always reduces the tax, since only CLAUDE.md's own line count counts against the budget.
Partially, it halves the tax because @-mentioned files load at half the token cost of inline text.
metadata.explanation: @-mentioning a file inside CLAUDE.md still auto-includes its full contents on every request; relocating text behind an @-mention doesn't reduce what actually gets loaded each turn.

cards.prompt: A team lead proposes "just @import the whole style guide into CLAUDE.md instead of pasting it inline" as a fix for a CLAUDE.md that's making every request slower. Will that actually solve the problem?
cards.answer: No, an @-mention in CLAUDE.md still gets auto-included on every request just as fully as inline text; the real fix is to trim the content, not relocate it.

choices: Yes, @import only loads the referenced file lazily, on the turns where it's actually relevant.
Yes, @-mentioned content is documented as not counting toward CLAUDE.md's line-count guidance at all, since only literal inline text counts against the budget.
No, an @-mention in CLAUDE.md still gets auto-included on every request just as fully as inline text; the real fix is to trim the content, not relocate it.
Partially, it removes the tax for the first message of each conversation only.
metadata.explanation: This is the documented trap: @-importing a huge doc into CLAUDE.md doesn't reduce the per-turn tax, because the imported content is still loaded on every request.

cards.prompt: A new teammate suggests replacing a large inline coding-conventions section in CLAUDE.md with an @-mention to a separate CONVENTIONS.md file, believing this will make sessions faster. What should a more experienced teammate point out?
cards.answer: The @-mention still auto-loads CONVENTIONS.md's full contents on every request, so nothing is actually saved; the tax comes from what's loaded, not from which file it originated in.

choices: The suggestion is correct, since @-mentioned files are compressed automatically before being sent.
The suggestion is correct, but only if CONVENTIONS.md is placed in CLAUDE.local.md instead.
The suggestion only works if CONVENTIONS.md itself stays under roughly 200 lines total, in which case @-mentioning it instead of pasting it inline does measurably reduce the per-turn tax.
The @-mention still auto-loads CONVENTIONS.md's full contents on every request, so nothing is actually saved; the tax comes from what's loaded, not from which file it originated in.
metadata.explanation: Splitting content into a second file doesn't help if that file is still @-mentioned inside CLAUDE.md, since it's loaded in full on every request either way.

cards.prompt: A solo dev, chasing sluggish responses, discovers their CLAUDE.md is short but @-mentions three separate multi-hundred-line reference docs at the bottom. They assume the file "counts" as small since it's technically only 15 lines. Where's the flaw in that reasoning?
cards.answer: CLAUDE.md's own line count isn't what matters; every request loads the full contents of anything it @-mentions too, so those three large docs are just as much a per-turn tax as pasted inline text.

choices: There's no flaw; only the literal CLAUDE.md file counts toward the per-turn budget, so those docs are effectively free.

The flaw is that @-mentions technically require every single referenced file to individually stay under 200 lines each, so at least one of the three large reference docs is silently being truncated without warning.
The flaw is that @-mentioned docs load twice per request, doubling their effective cost compared to inline text.
CLAUDE.md's own line count isn't what matters; every request loads the full contents of anything it @-mentions too, so those three large docs are just as much a per-turn tax as pasted inline text.
metadata.explanation: The 15-line CLAUDE.md file is misleading: what actually loads every turn includes everything it @-mentions, so the real per-turn payload is the sum of all three reference docs, not the file's own line count.

cards.prompt: A team building an internal HR bot knows every request will be one of three types: PTO balance lookup, benefits FAQ, or payroll date lookup, and the UX only exposes three buttons for these. What architecture best fits?
cards.answer: A workflow with a fixed step for each of the three known request types

choices: An orchestrator-workers pattern that decomposes each request at runtime
An autonomous agent that infers the user's need from open-ended text
An evaluator-optimizer loop that scores each answer before returning it
A workflow with a fixed step for each of the three known request types
metadata.explanation: Enumerable steps plus a UX that constrains users to known tasks is a strong workflow signal; reaching for a fancier pattern here is the classic over-engineering trap.

cards.prompt: According to the reliability-first heuristic for choosing between workflows and agents, when should you actually reach for an agent?
cards.answer: Only when the task's steps genuinely cannot be predetermined

choices: Whenever a workflow's classification step becomes a minor bottleneck
Whenever users would prefer a more sophisticated-sounding system
Only when the task's steps genuinely cannot be predetermined
Whenever the budget allows for a few extra API calls
metadata.explanation: The rule is to use the least agency that reliably solves the task; agents are for tasks that can't be predetermined, not for impressing users or working around minor bottlenecks.

cards.prompt: A support tool must handle a genuinely unpredictable range of requests, some needing multi-step tool combinations nobody anticipated at design time. Which structural signal correctly points to using an agent here?
cards.answer: The model must decide the next step and when to stop, at runtime

choices: The steps must run in a fixed sequential order every single time
A separate judge model scores the output and loops until it passes
One classification call routes the input to a single specialized pipeline
The model must decide the next step and when to stop, at runtime
metadata.explanation: That's the structural definition of an agent — a loop the model drives. The other options describe chaining, routing, and evaluator-optimizer respectively, not an agent.

cards.prompt: A junior engineer argues for building an autonomous agent for a report generator because 'agents are more powerful,' even though the steps (pull data, format, email) are fully known and fixed. What's the correct pushback?
cards.answer: Reliability matters most; use the workflow since the steps are enumerable

choices: Agents are always more powerful, so the agent should ship as proposed
Use an orchestrator instead so the model can add report sections dynamically
Reliability matters most; use the workflow since the steps are enumerable
Use an evaluator-optimizer instead to grade the report before sending it
metadata.explanation: Default to workflows whenever steps are enumerable; a fancier pattern that isn't needed just adds cost and unpredictability without benefit.

cards.prompt: What is the primary downside of choosing an agent over a workflow, per the course material?
cards.answer: A lower successful-completion rate, and it's harder to test since the step sequence isn't known ahead of time

choices: A hard technical inability to call any external tools or APIs
A mandatory requirement to run a separate evaluator model alongside every single agent call, even for the simplest possible single-step task
Guaranteed higher token cost on every single API call it makes
A lower successful-completion rate, and it's harder to test since the step sequence isn't known ahead of time
metadata.explanation: Agents are documented as less predictable and harder to instrument/test than workflows, since you don't know the step sequence in advance.

cards.prompt: A team's UX intentionally constrains users to a fixed dropdown of three known operations, but a teammate insists on building an agent 'in case future needs get more open-ended.' What should guide today's decision?
cards.answer: Build for today's constrained, known operations; a workflow fits the current UX

choices: Build the agent now to future-proof against any conceivable future request the product might eventually receive
Build an evaluator-optimizer now to future-proof the output's quality bar
Build an orchestrator now to future-proof against dynamic subtask needs
Build for today's constrained, known operations; a workflow fits the current UX
metadata.explanation: UX constraining users to known tasks is a strong workflow signal; over-building for a hypothetical future need trades away today's reliability and simplicity for nothing.

cards.prompt: Which pairing correctly matches the two documented angles used to decide between a workflow and an agent?
cards.answer: Structural: who drives the control flow; practical: can you picture the exact steps in advance

choices: Structural: how many tools are registered in the system; practical: how much each individual API call ends up costing
Structural: which cloud provider hosts it; practical: how many tokens the task consumes
Structural: who drives the control flow; practical: can you picture the exact steps in advance
Structural: which model version is used; practical: how fast the response returns
metadata.explanation: The material explicitly frames the decision as two angles on one line: who controls the flow (structural), and whether you can enumerate the steps up front (practical).

cards.prompt: During an earlier lesson, a team used tools and let Claude figure out how to complete a task on its own. According to the course framing, what was that, even though it wasn't labeled this way at the time?
cards.answer: An agent, even though it wasn't explicitly labeled that way at the time

choices: A workflow, since only a single Claude call was ever involved
An evaluator-optimizer, since the tool outputs were validated afterward
An agent, even though it wasn't explicitly labeled that way at the time
A router, since a classification step selected which tools to use
metadata.explanation: The course explicitly notes that letting Claude figure out how to complete a task with tools is an agent, regardless of whether it was called one at the time.

cards.prompt: A product team can picture every step Claude should take for a fixed onboarding flow, but worries that shipping a workflow feels 'less impressive' than shipping an agent. Which statement best resolves the tension?
cards.answer: Users care about reliable results, not whether the system is labeled an agent

choices: Users care about reliable results, not whether the system is labeled an agent
Agents are inherently more impressive, so they should always be preferred
Impressiveness should be weighted equally with reliability when making this kind of architecture decision
The workflow should simply be renamed an agent to satisfy stakeholders
metadata.explanation: The core rule is that your job is to solve problems reliably — users don't care that it's a fancy agent, they want consistent results.

cards.prompt: Which of these is a genuine, documented downside of choosing a workflow over an agent?
cards.answer: It is less flexible and typically requires more upfront design work to enumerate the steps

choices: It always costs more per request to run than an equivalent agent would
It is completely incapable of interleaving any non-LLM steps like external API calls or validation logic
It is less flexible and typically requires more upfront design work to enumerate the steps
It cannot be tested or evaluated step by step during development
metadata.explanation: Workflows trade flexibility and upfront design effort for predictability; the distractors invert real workflow strengths (easier testing, and the ability to interleave non-LLM steps).

cards.prompt: A pipeline pulls trending topics via a Twitter API call, has Claude pick the best one, has Claude research it, has Claude draft a script, and then a non-LLM step renders the video. What best describes this pipeline?
cards.answer: A chain that interleaves non-LLM steps between Claude calls

choices: A parallelization pattern, since the steps happen concurrently
An agent loop, since tools are technically involved at every step
An orchestrator, since Claude decides the pipeline's stages at runtime
A chain that interleaves non-LLM steps between Claude calls
metadata.explanation: This is a fixed, developer-defined ordered sequence mixing API/rendering steps with Claude calls — the textbook shape of a chain, not a model-driven agent loop.

cards.prompt: A team writes one long prompt asking Claude to draft a technical article that avoids revealing AI authorship, avoids emojis, and stays professional. Claude still slips in emojis and a casual tone. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Split into two steps: draft the article, then a focused revision pass targeting only the violations

choices: Lower the temperature setting to make the output more predictable
Increase max_tokens so Claude has more room to satisfy every constraint
Repeat the same constraints three or four times over using stronger, more emphatic and forceful wording
Split into two steps: draft the article, then a focused revision pass targeting only the violations
metadata.explanation: The documented long-prompt fix is a two-step chain — generate, then a revision pass focused only on fixing violations — not sterner wording, more room, or a temperature tweak.

cards.prompt: What is the primary reason to chain multiple Claude calls instead of using one massive prompt?
cards.answer: Focus — handling one task at a time lets Claude do it well instead of juggling many requirements

choices: Chains guarantee a strictly lower total token cost than an equivalent single big call would ever achieve
Chains always complete faster than an equivalent single call would
Focus — handling one task at a time lets Claude do it well instead of juggling many requirements
Chains eliminate any need to validate or process outputs between steps
metadata.explanation: Chaining's core benefit is focus, not speed; chains actually add latency since steps run in series, and cost isn't guaranteed to be lower.

cards.prompt: A workflow extracts numbers from a report, converts them to percentages, sorts them, and then formats a markdown table across four sequential Claude calls, each feeding the next. What trade-off does this design explicitly accept?
cards.answer: Added latency, since the four calls run one after another in series

choices: Reduced accuracy, since Claude only ever sees one step at a time
Reduced testability, since the individual steps can't be evaluated on their own
Added cost, since many calls run all at once against the same input
Added latency, since the four calls run one after another in series
metadata.explanation: Chaining trades latency for improved focus, since the calls are serial; it doesn't trade away cost, accuracy, or testability the way the distractors claim.

cards.prompt: A developer adds a 'revise for tone' Claude step after drafting an email, followed by a separate step that validates the email against a spam-filter API before sending. Is this still an example of chaining?
cards.answer: Yes — chaining can interleave non-LLM validation steps between Claude calls

choices: Yes — chaining can interleave non-LLM validation steps between Claude calls
No — once a non-LLM step is added, the pipeline becomes an agent
No — adding a validation step converts the pipeline into evaluator-optimizer
No — that pattern is only valid as an orchestrator-workers design
metadata.explanation: Chains can interleave API calls, code, and validation between Claude calls; the defining trait isn't that every step is an LLM call.

cards.prompt: Two subtasks are being considered for a data pipeline: subtask B needs subtask A's finished output before it can run at all. Which pattern fits, and why?
cards.answer: Prompt chaining, because subtask B depends on subtask A's output

choices: Routing, because subtask A classifies the input that subtask B then handles
Prompt chaining, because subtask B depends on subtask A's output
Orchestrator-workers, because the dependency is unknown until runtime
Parallelization, because both subtasks can run concurrently regardless
metadata.explanation: Dependent, ordered subtasks fail the independence test for parallelization and must be chained instead — the dependency here is known up front, not runtime-discovered.

cards.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes a 'generate, then focused revision' chain from an evaluator-optimizer loop?
cards.answer: The chain runs a fixed number of steps with no judge; evaluator-optimizer loops an unknown number of times until a judge passes it

choices: The chain always uses more total Claude calls than an equivalent evaluator-optimizer loop would across any comparable task
The chain can only ever be used for creative writing style tasks
The chain requires its own dedicated grader model while evaluator-optimizer does not
The chain runs a fixed number of steps with no judge; evaluator-optimizer loops an unknown number of times until a judge passes it
metadata.explanation: A fixed two-step revision chain has no judge and a known step count, while evaluator-optimizer loops back an unknown number of times until a grader says PASS.

cards.prompt: A pipeline decomposes a report into 'clean sentences' in one step, then summarizes those clean sentences in a second step. A reviewer suggests running both steps in parallel to save time. What's wrong with that suggestion?
cards.answer: The summarization step needs the cleaning step's output, so it can't run independently

choices: Parallel calls are always more expensive than sequential ones, regardless of dependency
Summarization always requires an evaluator loop instead of parallel execution
The summarization step needs the cleaning step's output, so it can't run independently
Claude is technically incapable of summarizing text that has already been cleaned
metadata.explanation: The independence test fails here — step two needs step one's output, so this must remain a chain rather than being parallelized.

cards.prompt: Why might chaining be preferred over a single mega-prompt even when that mega-prompt's constraints are all clearly listed?
cards.answer: Claude can still ignore some constraints when juggling many at once, so narrowing scope per step improves reliability

choices: A single prompt will always exceed the available context window on any realistically sized production task
Chaining is required any time the desired output is longer than one sentence
A single prompt is technically incapable of expressing more than one constraint
Claude can still ignore some constraints when juggling many at once, so narrowing scope per step improves reliability
metadata.explanation: This matches the documented long-prompt problem: constraint adherence degrades when Claude juggles many requirements at once, even when they're clearly stated.

cards.prompt: A video-script tool serves both 'programming' topics, which want clear technical explanations, and 'surfing' topics, which want exciting, visual language. One generic prompt underperforms on both. What should the team add?
cards.answer: A classification step that routes each topic to its own specialized prompt template

choices: A classification step that routes each topic to its own specialized prompt template
A single, more detailed prompt that tries to cover both audiences at once
A parallel call that runs both the technical and entertainment prompts every time
An orchestrator that invents entirely new topic categories for itself at runtime with every incoming request
metadata.explanation: This is the textbook routing setup: one classify call, then dispatch to exactly one specialized pipeline — not run everything, and not invent categories dynamically.

cards.prompt: A support-ticket classifier occasionally mis-routes a password-reset ticket to 'account' instead of 'technical,' producing an unhelpful reply. What is the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Improve the classifier's selector prompt or its category descriptions

choices: Improve the classifier's selector prompt or its category descriptions
Increase the max_tokens on the specialist prompt's generated response
Switch the specialist prompt over to running on a larger model
Add a brand-new fourth pipeline that only handles password resets
metadata.explanation: Routing quality is entirely classifier quality; a mis-route poisons the downstream specialist, so the fix is improving the selector prompt or category descriptions before adding routes.

cards.prompt: What is the specific efficiency win that routing provides over sending every input through every specialized pipeline?
cards.answer: Each input reaches exactly one pipeline, so every pipeline stays optimized for its own category

choices: Each input reaches exactly one pipeline, so every pipeline stays optimized for its own category
Routing removes the need for any prompt engineering work entirely
Routing guarantees a strictly lower latency than a single unified prompt would achieve in every possible case
Routing eliminates the need for any classification step whatsoever
metadata.explanation: The documented efficiency win is that input goes to exactly one pipeline, letting each stay highly optimized — not that classification, latency, or prompt engineering disappear.

cards.prompt: A team is deciding whether to add routing to a content tool that already serves all its request types well with a single prompt. What should guide the decision?
cards.answer: Whether the specialization benefit outweighs the added overhead of the classify call

choices: Whether the team currently has enough available engineers on staff to build and maintain multiple separate prompts
Whether Claude's output format supports XML tags for the classify step
Whether the existing single prompt has ever produced a visible error
Whether the specialization benefit outweighs the added overhead of the classify call
metadata.explanation: Routing adds an overhead call; it's only worth it when specialization beats that cost. If a single prompt already serves all inputs, adding routing is over-engineering.

cards.prompt: A routed customer-service pipeline sends 'billing' tickets to a billing specialist prompt, which itself runs a three-step chain: verify account, calculate refund, draft response. Is this a valid design?
cards.answer: Yes — a routed pipeline can itself be a chain, since patterns nest and compose

choices: No — nesting a chain inside a route automatically turns the whole system into an agent
No — a routed pipeline is only allowed to call an orchestrator, never a chain
Yes — a routed pipeline can itself be a chain, since patterns nest and compose
No — routing must always dispatch to a single flat prompt with no further structure
metadata.explanation: Patterns nest: a routed pipeline can be its own chain, orchestrator, or agent. Routing is a composition point, not just a switch statement.

cards.prompt: Which factor determines whether the categorization step in a routing design will actually work well in production?
cards.answer: Whether Claude can reliably classify inputs into the predefined categories

choices: Whether every category receives an equal volume of incoming requests
Whether Claude can reliably classify inputs into the predefined categories
Whether the categories happen to be listed in alphabetical order
Whether the specialist prompts are shorter in length than the classify prompt
metadata.explanation: Reliable categorization is explicitly listed as a precondition for routing to work well; ordering, volume balance, and relative prompt length aren't relevant factors.

cards.prompt: An e-commerce chatbot handles both 'where's my order' and 'I want a refund' requests acceptably with one shared prompt. A teammate proposes adding a classify-then-route layer anyway. What's the concern?
cards.answer: The added classify call is overhead that isn't justified if one prompt already serves both cases well

choices: Routing would make the chatbot completely unable to handle refund requests
Routing cannot technically be applied to customer-service use cases at all
The added classify call is overhead that isn't justified if one prompt already serves both cases well
Routing always exactly doubles the total number of Claude calls made per incoming request, no matter how it's designed
metadata.explanation: This is the over-engineering trap: routing only pays off when specialization outweighs the classify-call overhead, which isn't the case if one prompt already works.

cards.prompt: A router classifies inbound emails into 'sales,' 'support,' or 'spam,' then hands each to its own pipeline. A reviewer asks whether the router should instead send every email to all three pipelines and average the results. Why is that the wrong move?
cards.answer: It defeats the purpose of routing, since routing's efficiency win comes from sending each input to exactly one specialized pipeline

choices: It would actually be correct, since averaging outputs from multiple independent pipelines always improves accuracy regardless of cost
It is only wrong because spam emails must never be sent to a Claude call
It would violate rate limits on the initial classification call itself
It defeats the purpose of routing, since routing's efficiency win comes from sending each input to exactly one specialized pipeline
metadata.explanation: Running input through every pipeline is the documented anti-pattern that routing exists to avoid — it wastes cost and defeats the specialization win.

cards.prompt: In a routing design, what two things happen in order?
cards.answer: An LLM classifies the input into a predefined category, then the matching specialized pipeline runs

choices: An LLM drafts a full response, then a second LLM verifies the category after the fact
A generator drafts an output, then a separate evaluator grades it and loops back until it finally passes
An LLM classifies the input into a predefined category, then the matching specialized pipeline runs
A worker LLM generates several subtasks, then an orchestrator combines their outputs
metadata.explanation: This is the two-step routing structure (categorize, then dispatch); the distractors describe orchestrator-workers and evaluator-optimizer instead.

cards.prompt: A market-research tool analyzes how a new policy would affect four independent audiences — customers, employees, investors, suppliers — sending the same 'assess market impact' prompt to each group concurrently. What pattern and flavor is this?
cards.answer: Parallelization, specifically the sectioning flavor, since each call covers a different independent sub-task

choices: Orchestrator-workers, since the four audience groups are decided by Claude at runtime
Parallelization, specifically the voting flavor, since the exact same call is simply repeated four separate times over
Parallelization, specifically the sectioning flavor, since each call covers a different independent sub-task
Prompt chaining, since each group's analysis feeds directly into the next group's analysis
metadata.explanation: Different independent sub-tasks aggregated together is the sectioning flavor of parallelization; it isn't voting (that's identical repeated tasks), chaining (no dependency exists), or orchestrator-workers (the four groups are fixed by the developer, not generated at runtime).

cards.prompt: To increase confidence in a risky legal-clause interpretation, a team sends the exact same clause and prompt to Claude five times concurrently, then runs a final call that picks the most common interpretation. What flavor of parallelization is this?
cards.answer: Voting — the same task run N times, then aggregated

choices: Voting — the same task run N times, then aggregated
Sectioning — different independent sub-tasks aggregated together into one result
Routing — five specialist prompts, each handling one predefined category
Chaining — five sequential passes that each refine the previous answer
metadata.explanation: Running the identical task N times and then aggregating the results is the documented voting flavor of parallelization, distinct from sectioning's different-sub-tasks approach.

cards.prompt: A translation tool fires five concurrent Claude calls, one per target language, and delivers each translation standalone to its own audience without combining them afterward. Does this design still need an aggregation step?
cards.answer: No — aggregation is for synthesizing sub-results into one decision; five independent standalone outputs don't need to be combined

choices: Yes — without aggregation, the five concurrent calls default to becoming an orchestrator-workers pattern
No — aggregation is for synthesizing sub-results into one decision; five independent standalone outputs don't need to be combined
Yes — omitting aggregation will always cause the five translations to contradict each other
Yes — every parallelization design must always end with a dedicated aggregation call, with absolutely no exceptions allowed
metadata.explanation: Aggregation exists to synthesize parallel results into a single output; when each result is consumed independently rather than merged into one decision, there's nothing to aggregate.

cards.prompt: What is the cost/latency trade-off of parallelization compared to running the same total work sequentially?
cards.answer: You pay for N concurrent calls, but wall-clock time drops to roughly the slowest single sub-task rather than the sum of all of them
