choices: You pay for N concurrent calls, but wall-clock time drops to roughly the slowest single sub-task rather than the sum of all of them
Cost stays completely flat while latency increases linearly with the sub-task count
Both cost and latency drop simultaneously with no trade-off involved at all
You pay for only a single call total, but wall-clock time still increases steadily with each additional sub-task that gets added in
metadata.explanation: Parallelization buys wall-clock speed (bounded by the slowest sub-task) at the cost of N concurrent calls — the opposite of the sequential trade-off the distractors describe.

cards.prompt: A customer-feedback tool sends the same review text to four concurrent Claude calls, each scoring one distinct dimension — tone, urgency, product area, and churn risk — using its own tailored prompt. A teammate claims 'that's not real parallelization because the four calls use different prompts.' Is the teammate right?
cards.answer: No — sub-tasks need not be identical; independence and concurrency define parallelization, not sameness

choices: No — sub-tasks need not be identical; independence and concurrency define parallelization, not sameness
Yes — parallelization strictly requires using the exact same identical prompt across every single concurrent call made
Yes — using different prompts per call actually makes this an orchestrator-workers pattern instead
Yes — using different prompts per call actually makes this routing rather than parallelization
metadata.explanation: The documented correction is explicit: sub-tasks need not be identical. The defining trait is independent, concurrent execution, not sameness — the 'identical prompt' claim is the classic trap.

cards.prompt: A team wants to add a fifth independent scoring dimension, such as 'brand safety,' to the four-dimension feedback tool above. What does the parallelization structure make easy about this addition?
cards.answer: Add one more concurrent request with its own prompt, without touching the other four

choices: Rewrite all four of the existing prompts so that each one separately accounts for the new dimension too
Re-run the evaluator loop until all five dimensions pass a shared quality bar
Add one more concurrent request with its own prompt, without touching the other four
Retrain the classifier that decides which dimension each request routes to
metadata.explanation: This is the scalability benefit documented for parallelization: adding a sub-task means adding a request, not rewriting the existing ones.

cards.prompt: A code-quality tool runs three concurrent Claude calls on the same file: one checking security issues, one checking style, one checking performance. Why might tuning the security-check prompt in isolation be safe to do?
cards.answer: Each parallel sub-task can be optimized independently without affecting the others' prompts or results

choices: Because security checks always run strictly before the other two checks in a fixed sequence every single time
Because all three checks actually share one combined prompt under the hood
Each parallel sub-task can be optimized independently without affecting the others' prompts or results
Because the performance and style checks are optional and can be skipped
metadata.explanation: This matches the documented 'easier optimization' benefit of parallelization — each sub-prompt can be tuned or tested independently of the others.

cards.prompt: A team considers parallelizing a workflow where step two needs step one's extracted entities before it can run its own analysis. What should stop them from parallelizing these two steps?
cards.answer: Step two depends on step one's output, which fails the independence test for parallelization

choices: Parallelization can technically never be used with more than a single Claude call total in any valid design
Step one and step two using different prompts disqualifies parallelization by itself
Concurrent execution is only allowed once a router has already classified the input
Step two depends on step one's output, which fails the independence test for parallelization
metadata.explanation: The independence test is the deciding factor: if step B needs step A's output, the steps must chain rather than run in parallel.

cards.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the documented benefits of parallelization over a single mega-prompt covering all criteria?
cards.answer: Reduced total token cost compared to any other pattern

choices: Focused attention on one aspect per call, which improves accuracy
Reduced total token cost compared to any other pattern
Easier optimization, since each sub-task's prompt can be tuned independently
Better scalability, since adding a sub-task doesn't require rewriting the rest
metadata.explanation: Parallelization actually costs N concurrent calls rather than reducing cost; the other three options are the documented benefits (focused attention, easier optimization, scalability).

cards.prompt: A due-diligence tool sends a contract to three concurrent Claude calls — one flags financial risk, one flags legal risk, one flags reputational risk — then a fourth Claude call reads all three flagged-risk lists and produces one prioritized action plan. What is that fourth call?
cards.answer: The aggregation step that synthesizes the parallel sub-results into a final decision

choices: The aggregation step that synthesizes the parallel sub-results into a final decision
An orchestrator that generated the three risk categories at runtime
A router that dispatches the contract to one of three specialist pipelines
An evaluator that grades all three of the risk analyses and requests revisions before anything gets finalized
metadata.explanation: This is the split-then-aggregate structure of parallelization; the fourth call is the synthesis step that combines independent results into one decision, not a router, orchestrator, or evaluator.

cards.prompt: A coding assistant is asked to 'add input validation across the codebase.' It first inspects the repo to figure out which files need changes — this varies completely by codebase and can't be listed ahead of time — then delegates a fix to each file it identifies. Which pattern is this?
cards.answer: Orchestrator-workers, since the set of files to change is generated dynamically at runtime

choices: Routing, since each affected file is simply dispatched to a pipeline that was predefined well ahead of time
Orchestrator-workers, since the set of files to change is generated dynamically at runtime
Prompt chaining, since each file's fix depends on the previous file's fix
Parallelization, since the file list was already divided up before the run even started
metadata.explanation: Subtasks that can't be known in advance and are decided per input by the model define orchestrator-workers — unlike routing's fixed menu or parallelization's pre-divided work.

cards.prompt: What is the key architectural difference between routing and orchestrator-workers?
cards.answer: Routing picks one path from a menu you defined in advance; the orchestrator generates that menu itself at runtime

choices: Routing requires a dedicated grader model while orchestrator-workers does not
Routing always uses strictly more total Claude calls than an equivalent orchestrator-workers design would ever need
Orchestrator-workers is structurally unable to delegate to more than one worker
Routing picks one path from a menu you defined in advance; the orchestrator generates that menu itself at runtime
metadata.explanation: This is the documented 'vs routing' distinction: predetermined menu (routing) versus dynamically generated menu (orchestrator-workers).

cards.prompt: A research assistant is asked an open-ended question requiring searches across an unknown number of sources, decided only after seeing the question. A central Claude call plans which sources to search, delegates a search-and-summarize task to each, then combines the findings. What is that central planning call called in this pattern?
cards.answer: The orchestrator, which dynamically breaks the task into subtasks for the workers

choices: The aggregator, which only combines already-completed worker outputs
The router, which simply classifies the incoming question into one predefined, fixed category
The orchestrator, which dynamically breaks the task into subtasks for the workers
The evaluator, which grades each worker's summary before accepting it
metadata.explanation: The central call that dynamically decomposes the task, delegates, and later synthesizes results is the orchestrator role — distinct from a router, evaluator, or a pure aggregator.

cards.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes orchestrator-workers from parallelization?
cards.answer: Parallelization runs work that's already been divided by the developer; the orchestrator divides the work itself, per input

choices: Orchestrator-workers is structurally unable to run its workers concurrently
Parallelization always uses exactly two concurrent workers while orchestrator-workers always uses three or more workers every time
Parallelization runs work that's already been divided by the developer; the orchestrator divides the work itself, per input
Parallelization requires a synthesis step while orchestrator-workers never includes one
metadata.explanation: This matches the documented 'vs parallelization' distinction: who divides the work, and when — the developer ahead of time, or the orchestrator at runtime.

cards.prompt: A team builds an orchestrator that plans two to three different content variations for a task, spawns a worker to generate each, then collects the results. A reviewer notices the number of workers spawned isn't fixed and varies by task. Is this a problem?
cards.answer: No — that unknown-until-runtime task count is the defining trait of orchestrator-workers

choices: Yes — this variability means the design must add a routing step before it can work
Yes — a valid workflow must always spawn a fixed, predetermined number of workers
Yes — this variability secretly means the design is actually an evaluator-optimizer loop
No — that unknown-until-runtime task count is the defining trait of orchestrator-workers
metadata.explanation: The task list length being unknown until the orchestrator actually runs is explicitly called out as the whole point of the pattern, not a flaw.

cards.prompt: A team is unsure whether their multi-file coding-change tool should use orchestrator-workers or a fixed router with three predefined pipelines: bug-fix, feature-add, refactor. What question should decide it?
cards.answer: Can the specific files and changes needed be predicted before seeing the request, or only decided per input?

choices: Which of the two patterns was published first in the Anthropic Cookbook?
Can the specific files and changes needed be predicted before seeing the request, or only decided per input?
Which of the two patterns is easier to explain to a non-technical stakeholder?
Which of the two patterns uses fewer total Claude calls on average across a large sample of requests?
metadata.explanation: The tested seam between routing and orchestrator-workers is exactly this: are the subtasks knowable in advance, or only decided per input at runtime?

cards.prompt: An orchestrator normally emits an analysis plus a list of two to three parsed tasks, and a worker is called for each. Suppose the analysis step is skipped and workers are instead dispatched immediately using a hardcoded task list every time. What happens to the pattern?
cards.answer: It stops being orchestrator-workers, since the subtask decomposition is no longer generated dynamically

choices: Nothing changes at all, since the worker prompts stay completely identical either way regardless
It stops being orchestrator-workers, since the subtask decomposition is no longer generated dynamically
It becomes parallelization only if the hardcoded tasks happen to run sequentially
It becomes evaluator-optimizer, since the workers now grade each other's output
metadata.explanation: Dynamic decomposition by a central LLM is what defines orchestrator-workers; hardcoding the task list removes that and turns the design into something closer to routing or parallelization.

cards.prompt: Per the blog's definition, what does the central LLM do in an orchestrator-workers system?
cards.answer: Dynamically breaks down the task, delegates pieces to worker LLMs, and synthesizes their results

choices: Generates a response while a second LLM grades it in a repeating feedback loop
Dynamically breaks down the task, delegates pieces to worker LLMs, and synthesizes their results
Runs the identical prompt across every worker without any delegation logic at all
Classifies the input just once and hands the entire request off to a single specialist prompt template
metadata.explanation: This matches the blog's definition of orchestrator-workers; the distractors instead describe routing and evaluator-optimizer.

cards.prompt: A literary-translation tool has one Claude call produce a first-pass translation, then a second Claude call critiques nuance and hands specific feedback back to the first call to retry, repeating until the critique call is satisfied. Which pattern is this?
cards.answer: Evaluator-optimizer, since a judge loops feedback back to a generator until quality is accepted

choices: Routing, since the critique call classifies translation quality into categories
Prompt chaining, since translation always happens strictly before critique
Orchestrator-workers, since the critique call delegates the translation work out to separate translation workers
Evaluator-optimizer, since a judge loops feedback back to a generator until quality is accepted
metadata.explanation: An unknown-count feedback loop where a judge decides when to stop is the defining shape of evaluator-optimizer, not a fixed chain, a decomposition step, or a classifier.

cards.prompt: An engineering team builds an evaluator-optimizer loop for onboarding emails, but nobody on the team can articulate what makes an onboarding email 'good' beyond vague vibes. What's the risk?
cards.answer: Without an articulable quality signal, the evaluator can't meaningfully judge, and the loop becomes theater

choices: The loop will simply run once and skip the evaluation step entirely
The loop will automatically fall back to becoming a plain routing pattern
Without an articulable quality signal, the evaluator can't meaningfully judge, and the loop becomes theater
The loop will always terminate cleanly after exactly two iterations no matter what happens inside it
metadata.explanation: Evaluator-optimizer needs a real, articulable definition of 'good'; without one, the evaluator can't provide a meaningful judgment and the whole loop is theater.

cards.prompt: A code-generation evaluator-optimizer loop only exits when the evaluator returns PASS, and the generator has been stuck on NEEDS_IMPROVEMENT for 40 iterations with no end in sight. What was missing from the original design?
cards.answer: A max-iteration cap to bound the loop as a backstop

choices: A parallel branch that runs several generators at the same time
A max-iteration cap to bound the loop as a backstop
A second evaluator model to double-check the first evaluator's judgment
A higher temperature setting so the generator produces more variety
metadata.explanation: PASS-only exit conditions can loop forever; the documented required fix is adding a max-iteration cap as a safety backstop.

cards.prompt: In the 'producer/grader' vocabulary taught alongside 'generator/evaluator,' what does the producer do?
cards.answer: Takes the input and creates the output that will be judged

choices: Evaluates the output against criteria and issues structured feedback
Classifies the input into one of several predefined categories
Delegates subtasks to multiple workers and then merges their results
Takes the input and creates the output that will be judged
metadata.explanation: Producer maps to generator (it creates); grader maps to evaluator (it judges) — the other options describe routing and orchestrator-workers roles instead.

cards.prompt: An image-to-CAD workflow renders a 3D model from a description, then asks Claude to grade the rendering against the original image and fix issues if it falls short, repeating as needed. What specifically makes this step an evaluator-optimizer rather than plain chaining?
cards.answer: The grading step can loop back and revise an unknown number of times until it's judged acceptable

choices: The step first converts an image into a textual description of the object
The step uses the CadQuery library specifically to generate the underlying 3D model geometry itself
The step happens to be the fourth step in an otherwise sequential pipeline
The grading step can loop back and revise an unknown number of times until it's judged acceptable
metadata.explanation: The judge-driven loop-back with an unknown iteration count is what makes this evaluator-optimizer; the tooling used and its position in the pipeline are incidental.

cards.prompt: Which condition, if absent, makes evaluator-optimizer the wrong pattern even though iterative revision sounds appealing?
cards.answer: Clear evaluation criteria that make the evaluator's feedback genuinely useful

choices: A generator model that is strictly larger in size than the evaluator model
A routing step that classifies the task before generation even begins
A fixed, predetermined number of exactly three separate revision passes every single time it runs
Clear evaluation criteria that make the evaluator's feedback genuinely useful
metadata.explanation: Evaluator-optimizer requires clear evaluation criteria and feedback that's demonstrably useful; without that, revision doesn't reliably improve the output.

cards.prompt: A complex search task might need multiple rounds of refinement based on how relevant early results turn out to be, and the number of rounds can't be known ahead of time. Which pattern best fits this, and why?
cards.answer: Evaluator-optimizer, because a judge determines dynamically when the search results are good enough to stop

choices: Evaluator-optimizer, because a judge determines dynamically when the search results are good enough to stop
Routing, because the search query gets classified before searching even begins
Parallelization, because multiple search rounds can simply run at the same time
Prompt chaining, because the search process always runs through the exact same fixed number of rounds
metadata.explanation: An unknown iteration count driven by a quality judgment is exactly evaluator-optimizer's shape; chaining is fixed-step, parallelization needs independent sub-tasks, and routing is a one-time classify.

cards.prompt: A team replaces their evaluator-optimizer's PASS/FAIL judge with a fixed two-step 'draft then revise' chain, because their quality bar turned out to be simple and well-defined after all. What did they lose, and was it the right call?
cards.answer: They lost the unbounded feedback loop, but that's appropriate since a fixed two-step revision is simpler and sufficient when the quality bar doesn't need iterative judging

choices: They lost the unbounded feedback loop, but that's appropriate since a fixed two-step revision is simpler and sufficient when the quality bar doesn't need iterative judging
They lost the ability to interleave any non-LLM steps anywhere in the pipeline
They lost the ability to ever revise the draft more than once, which is always strictly worse
They lost nothing at all, since chaining and evaluator-optimizer are functionally identical patterns in every practical situation that matters
metadata.explanation: Use the least agency that reliably solves the task: if a fixed revision pass is sufficient, a chain is the simpler, appropriate choice over an unbounded judged loop.

cards.prompt: An agent's harness checks 'if stop_reason is not tool_use, mark done.' During a long response, the model hits its token cap mid-sentence and the harness marks it done anyway. What's the bug?
cards.answer: max_tokens truncation was treated the same as end_turn, but truncated output only looks finished

choices: stop_sequence responses were incorrectly excluded from being marked done
max_tokens truncation was treated the same as end_turn, but truncated output only looks finished
The harness needs to parse the response text itself for the literal word 'done' somewhere in it
tool_use responses should have been treated as done instead of continuing
metadata.explanation: Lumping max_tokens truncation together with a clean end_turn finish ships cut-off output; the fix is branching explicitly on each stop_reason value.

cards.prompt: What should an agent harness do when it receives a stop_reason of pause_turn from a long-running server tool?
cards.answer: Pass the response straight back to continue, rather than treating it as finished

choices: Pass the response straight back to continue, rather than treating it as finished
Retry the exact same tool call again but with a lower temperature setting
Immediately terminate the loop entirely and return whatever partial text happens to exist so far
Treat it identically to a refusal and surface an error message to the user
metadata.explanation: pause_turn indicates a long server tool paused mid-flight; the correct handling is passing the response straight back to let it continue, not stopping or erroring out.

cards.prompt: A harness assumes every response contains a text block, but a request returns stop_reason refusal with no text block at all, and the parser crashes. What should the harness have done?
cards.answer: Handle the refusal case explicitly, since a refusal may have no text block at all

choices: Handle the refusal case explicitly, since a refusal may have no text block at all
Assumed every stop_reason always guarantees a text block is present, per the API's contract
Retried the request with a stronger system prompt until it stopped refusing
Increased max_tokens so the refusal would include more explanatory text
metadata.explanation: Refusals may come back with no text block, so the harness needs to check for and handle that case explicitly instead of assuming text is always present.

cards.prompt: A tool is supposed to return a JSON array, but the harness receives a JSON string instead, and downstream code that assumes it's already an array throws a confusing error. What's the root-cause fix?
cards.answer: Validate and normalize the tool's output at the parse boundary instead of trusting its type

choices: Retry the exact same tool call repeatedly until the returned type eventually happens to be correct
Lower the temperature on the tool call to make its output more consistent
Switch to a larger model that is less likely to make formatting mistakes
Validate and normalize the tool's output at the parse boundary instead of trusting its type
metadata.explanation: Tool and structured output isn't guaranteed-typed; the documented fix is validating and normalizing at the parse boundary, not retrying, tuning temperature, or upgrading models.

cards.prompt: Which stop_reason indicates the model completed its response naturally, meaning the harness should simply return the text as-is?
cards.answer: end_turn

choices: max_tokens
pause_turn
tool_use
end_turn
metadata.explanation: end_turn means a clean, natural finish. tool_use requires running a tool, max_tokens signals truncation requiring continuation, and pause_turn means a paused server tool needs to be resumed.

cards.prompt: A deterministic evaluation harness for an agent needs reproducible outputs from Sonnet 5. A teammate suggests setting temperature to 0 to get consistency. What's the actual issue with that plan?
cards.answer: temperature is deprecated on Sonnet 5 and Opus 4.8 and returns a 400 error; disable thinking instead for a deterministic harness

choices: temperature only has an effect when it's combined with a routing step
temperature is deprecated on Sonnet 5 and Opus 4.8 and returns a 400 error; disable thinking instead for a deterministic harness
Setting temperature to 0 will simply be silently ignored by the API without producing any visible error at all
temperature has no effect whatsoever on any Claude model regardless of version
metadata.explanation: This matches the documented reliability gotcha: temperature is deprecated (400 error) on Sonnet 5/Opus 4.8, though still usable on Haiku 4.5; disabling thinking is the recommended path to determinism instead.

cards.prompt: An agent loop stops after exactly 10 tool calls regardless of what the model was doing, because a developer hardcoded that iteration cap as the primary stop condition. What's wrong with relying only on that as the stop signal?
cards.answer: An arbitrary iteration count isn't the real completion signal; the loop should stop on end_turn or a genuine grader PASS, with the cap only as a backstop

choices: Nothing is wrong; a hardcoded iteration cap is always sufficient on its own as the sole stop condition for any agent loop
The cap should simply be raised to 100 tool calls to resolve the issue
The cap should be removed entirely so the loop is never allowed to terminate early
An arbitrary iteration count isn't the real completion signal; the loop should stop on end_turn or a genuine grader PASS, with the cap only as a backstop
metadata.explanation: Stopping after an arbitrary iteration count is a documented anti-pattern; the real signal (end_turn or a grader PASS) should drive completion, with an iteration cap only as a safety backstop.

cards.prompt: A team keeps adding new emphatic instructions, like 'YOU MUST NOT' and 'THIS IS CRITICAL,' to a single sprawling prompt every time Claude violates a constraint, and the violations keep recurring anyway. What's the actual anti-pattern here?
cards.answer: Treating a focus problem as a wording problem instead of splitting into a chain with a dedicated revision step

choices: Failing to raise the temperature so Claude has more creative freedom
Using a workflow at all here instead of switching straight over to a more flexible autonomous agent
Treating a focus problem as a wording problem instead of splitting into a chain with a dedicated revision step
Using too few emphatic, all-capitalized words in the instructions given
metadata.explanation: The root-cause fix for recurring constraint violations is splitting into a generate-then-revise chain, not escalating the wording of a single mega-prompt.

cards.prompt: Which scenario correctly illustrates reaching for an orchestrator when a simpler pattern would have sufficed?
cards.answer: A team builds runtime task decomposition for a request type that, in practice, always breaks into the same three known steps

choices: A team builds runtime task decomposition for a request type where the needed steps vary unpredictably from one input to the next every time
A team uses a single prompt because every input is the same type and needs one output
A team builds runtime task decomposition for a request type that, in practice, always breaks into the same three known steps
A team uses a fixed router because every request maps cleanly to one of four known categories
metadata.explanation: If the steps are actually always the same three, a router or even a simple chain would serve just as well — paying for runtime decomposition there is the over-engineering trap; the other options correctly match pattern to situation.

cards.prompt: What aliases might the exam use in place of 'orchestrator-workers,' per the documented vocabulary table?
cards.answer: Lead or central LLM plus workers, or 'dynamic task decomposition'

choices: Lead or central LLM plus workers, or 'dynamic task decomposition'
Producer and grader, terms used specifically for the generator-and-judge roles inside a completely different pattern
Sectioning and voting
Tool-use loop or ReAct-style loop
metadata.explanation: The vocabulary table maps 'lead/central LLM plus workers' and 'dynamic task decomposition' to orchestrator-workers; the distractors belong to evaluator-optimizer, the agent loop, and parallelization respectively.

cards.prompt: A support system runs every incoming ticket through all four of its specialist pipelines — billing, technical, account, product — and then has a human pick the best-sounding response. What anti-pattern does this reproduce, and what's the fix?
cards.answer: It reproduces the 'route through everything' distractor; the fix is classifying once and dispatching to exactly one matching pipeline

choices: It reproduces the parallelization anti-pattern; the fix is making all four pipelines share one identical prompt
It reproduces the 'route through everything' distractor; the fix is classifying once and dispatching to exactly one matching pipeline
It reproduces the chaining anti-pattern; the fix is making the pipelines run sequentially instead
It reproduces the evaluator-optimizer anti-pattern; the fix is adding a max-iteration cap to bound how long the loop can run
metadata.explanation: Running input through every pipeline is the classic routing-defeating anti-pattern; genuine one-path routing after a single classify call is the documented fix.

cards.prompt: Which of these is explicitly called out as a reliability gotcha from live agent runs, separate from the stop_reason handling issue?
cards.answer: A tool's array can come back as a JSON string, and truncated tool-call JSON can throw a cryptic KeyError

choices: Every Claude model requires a routing step to run before it can call any tool
Claude is technically incapable of calling more than a single tool across an entire multi-turn conversation
A tool's array can come back as a JSON string, and truncated tool-call JSON can throw a cryptic KeyError
Evaluator-optimizer loops are fundamentally incompatible with any tool use
metadata.explanation: This matches the documented structured-output reliability gotcha: tool output isn't guaranteed-typed, and a truncated tool-call JSON can throw a confusing KeyError.

cards.prompt: A team debates whether a new multi-step feature counts as 'agentic' for architecture-review purposes. It has a router that dispatches to one of three fixed pipelines, one of which is itself a two-step chain, and no step's next action is ever decided by the model at runtime. What's the correct classification?
cards.answer: It's a workflow — control flow is fully wired by the developer in code, even though patterns nest inside each other

choices: It's an agent, because it has more than one architectural pattern layered together inside it
It's an agent, because routing involves an LLM making a classification decision
It's a workflow — control flow is fully wired by the developer in code, even though patterns nest inside each other
It's an orchestrator-workers pattern, because it delegates work to sub-pipelines
metadata.explanation: The anchor test is who drives control flow; here it's entirely developer-wired, so nesting patterns doesn't make it agentic, and the classify step still picks from a fixed menu rather than deciding control flow itself.

cards.prompt: Which pairing correctly matches a workflow pattern to the primary resource it trades away to get its main benefit?
cards.answer: Prompt chaining trades latency for focus; parallelization trades cost, meaning N concurrent calls, for wall-clock speed

choices: Evaluator-optimizer trades focus for a fixed iteration count; chaining trades wall-clock speed for cost
Prompt chaining trades cost for focus; parallelization instead trades latency for wall-clock speed in every design
Routing trades latency for zero added cost; orchestrator-workers trades cost for a fixed subtask menu
Prompt chaining trades latency for focus; parallelization trades cost, meaning N concurrent calls, for wall-clock speed
metadata.explanation: This matches the documented trade-offs table: chaining pays with latency since steps run in series, and parallelization pays with cost (N calls at once) to buy wall-clock speed.

cards.prompt: A developer needs Claude to make a change spanning many files across an unfamiliar module, and wants to review a plan before anything is edited. Which control should they reach for?
cards.answer: Type /plan to enter Planning Mode before describing the task

choices: Type /plan to enter Planning Mode before describing the task
Add the word ultrathink to the prompt
Run /effort and set the level to max
Press Ctrl+O to expand the reasoning steps
metadata.explanation: Planning Mode addresses breadth — spanning the codebase — and holds all edits until you approve a shown plan; effort/ultrathink increase depth of reasoning on a single problem but don't gate edits behind approval.

cards.prompt: A developer is stuck on a single gnarly recursive algorithm that keeps producing subtly wrong output, in one file they already understand well. What's the appropriate lever?
cards.answer: Raise /effort to a higher level for deeper reasoning on the one hard problem

choices: Enter Planning Mode with /plan
Raise /effort to a higher level for deeper reasoning on the one hard problem
Paste a screenshot with Ctrl+V of the failing test output
Press Shift+Tab twice to cycle permission modes
metadata.explanation: The difficulty here is depth (one hard problem in a file already understood), which effort level addresses; Planning Mode is for breadth across many files, not needed here.

cards.prompt: A developer types "ultrathink, please refactor this regex parser" for one particularly tricky prompt, but expects every later prompt in the session to keep running at that same deeper reasoning level. What actually happens?
cards.answer: Only that single turn gets the extra thinking boost; later prompts return to the session's normal effort level

choices: Only that single turn gets the extra thinking boost; later prompts return to the session's normal effort level
The session's /effort level is permanently raised to max after using ultrathink once
ultrathink has no effect unless Planning Mode is also active
ultrathink and /effort are synonyms, so both change permanently together
metadata.explanation: ultrathink is a per-prompt, one-shot boost; it does not change the session's persistent /effort setting, which is what the developer would need to check or set with /effort instead.

cards.prompt: A designer wants Claude to fix the padding on one specific button shown in a mockup, rather than describing its location in words. What is the correct way to give Claude that reference inside Claude Code on macOS?
cards.answer: Paste the screenshot with Ctrl+V

choices: Paste the screenshot with Ctrl+V
Paste the screenshot with Cmd+V, the standard macOS paste shortcut
Drag the image into the terminal and type /image
Reference the file path of a saved screenshot using @-mention only
metadata.explanation: Claude Code deliberately uses Ctrl+V for pasting screenshots even on macOS, not the OS-standard Cmd+V; a precise visual reference beats prose for pointing at an exact UI region.

cards.prompt: While reviewing a plan Claude produced in Planning Mode, a developer wants to make several precise line-level edits to the plan text itself before approving it. What should they do?
cards.answer: Press Ctrl+G to open the plan in a text editor, edit it, and submit; Claude uses that final version

choices: Press Ctrl+G to open the plan in a text editor, edit it, and submit; Claude uses that final version
Type /rewind to jump back and restate the plan from scratch
Press Escape to stop Claude, then retype the entire plan as a new prompt
Run /compact to summarize the plan into a shorter version
metadata.explanation: Ctrl+G opens the plan in your text editor for precise edits, and Claude proceeds with the final version you submit; the other options either discard the plan or aren't designed for editing it.

cards.prompt: Which two actions both enter Planning Mode in Claude Code?
cards.answer: Typing /plan, or pressing Shift+Tab twice (once if edits are already auto-accepting)

choices: Typing /plan, or pressing Shift+Tab twice (once if edits are already auto-accepting)
Typing /plan, or pressing Ctrl+O
Pressing Escape twice, or typing /rewind
Typing /effort max, or pressing Ctrl+G
metadata.explanation: Planning Mode is entered by typing /plan or by pressing Shift+Tab twice (just once if auto-accept edits is already on); Ctrl+O instead expands reasoning steps, and Escape-twice/rewind is unrelated conversation-history control.

cards.prompt: A team is about to attempt a multi-file migration that also involves a genuinely hard concurrency bug in one of the touched files. They're also mindful of API spend. What's the most accurate advice?
cards.answer: Use Planning Mode and a higher effort level together, understanding both spend extra tokens

choices: Use Planning Mode and a higher effort level together, understanding both spend extra tokens
Use only Planning Mode, since effort level doesn't apply to bug fixes
Use only a higher effort level, since Planning Mode is exclusively for brand-new features
Avoid both and rely on the default settings to save tokens, since combining them provides no benefit at all for a task like this
metadata.explanation: The task needs both breadth (multi-file migration) and depth (a hard concurrency bug), so combining Planning Mode and higher effort is appropriate — with the tradeoff that both cost extra tokens.

cards.prompt: A developer runs /effort with no arguments in a fresh session. What does Claude Code report?
cards.answer: The currently set effort level, whose default depends on the developer's model and plan

choices: The currently set effort level, whose default depends on the developer's model and plan
An error, because /effort requires an argument like low or max
Always "low" until manually changed, regardless of model or plan
The number of tokens spent on reasoning so far this session
metadata.explanation: /effort shows and sets the level (low through max); the default depends on your model and plan, and running it with no argument reports your current setting rather than erroring.

cards.prompt: While Claude is mid-response, a developer notices it has started drafting a plan to modify every function in a file when they only wanted one function changed. What's the fastest way to stop it and redirect?
cards.answer: Press Escape to interrupt, then clarify that only the one function should change

choices: Press Escape to interrupt, then clarify that only the one function should change
Type /clear to wipe the conversation and start over
Type /rewind to jump back to the very first message
Wait for Claude to finish, then run /compact to shrink the response
metadata.explanation: Escape interrupts Claude mid-response so you can redirect immediately, exactly for cases where it's heading too broad; /clear and /rewind discard more context than needed, and waiting doesn't stop the overreach.

cards.prompt: Across several separate conversations on the same project, Claude keeps using a deprecated API call that the developer has corrected in-chat each time, but it keeps happening in new sessions. What actually fixes this for good?
cards.answer: Press Escape, then use /memory to write down the correct approach so it persists across conversations

choices: Press Escape, then use /memory to write down the correct approach so it persists across conversations
Type /compact at the end of every session so the correction is summarized forward
Repeat the correction in-chat each time it happens, since Claude eventually learns and generalizes the fix across every future project conversation automatically
Use /rewind to remove the mistaken response from history each time
metadata.explanation: A mistake repeating across separate conversations needs a durable fix — Escape to stop it, then /memory (or CLAUDE.md) to record the correct approach — since /compact, in-chat correction, and rewind only affect the current conversation's history, not future sessions.

cards.prompt: A developer had a long, messy debugging back-and-forth that's now irrelevant, but doesn't want to lose Claude's accumulated understanding of the codebase built up before that detour. Which tool fits, and how does it work?
cards.answer: /rewind (or Escape twice) — shows prior messages and lets you jump back to an earlier point, continuing from there

choices: /rewind (or Escape twice) — shows prior messages and lets you jump back to an earlier point, continuing from there
/clear — discards everything and starts a completely fresh conversation
/compact — summarizes the entire conversation including the irrelevant detour
/memory — permanently records the entire debugging detour into CLAUDE.md so future sessions can reference exactly what went wrong and why it doesn't matter anymore
metadata.explanation: Rewind (Escape twice or /rewind) lets you jump back past the irrelevant stretch while keeping the earlier, valuable context intact; /clear wipes everything, and /compact would still fold the messy detour into its summary.

cards.prompt: A developer has spent an hour on a task and Claude has built up detailed understanding of the module. They now want to move to a closely related task in the same module without losing that understanding, but the conversation has gotten long. What should they run?
cards.answer: /compact, since it preserves key learned info while summarizing for a related task

choices: /compact, since it preserves key learned info while summarizing for a related task
/clear, since related tasks always benefit from a completely blank slate with no risk of old assumptions carrying over into the new work
/rewind, to jump back to before the task started
Escape, to stop Claude and immediately begin the new task
metadata.explanation: /compact summarizes the whole conversation while preserving key learned info, which is the right choice when moving to a related task and wanting to keep the module understanding Claude already built.

cards.prompt: A developer runs /clear to start a completely unrelated task, but a week later realizes they need to reference something specific from that earlier cleared conversation. What is true about recovering it?
cards.answer: The old conversation is not deleted; /resume can bring it back

choices: The old conversation is not deleted; /resume can bring it back
/clear permanently deletes conversation history, so the earlier context is gone
Only /rewind, not /resume, can recover a conversation cleared with /clear
The old conversation is recoverable only if /compact was run first
metadata.explanation: /clear starts a fresh conversation but does not delete the prior one from session history — /resume can return to it later; a common misconception is that /clear destroys history permanently.

cards.prompt: Ranking these four conversation-control tools from "sheds the least context" to "sheds the most," which order is correct?
cards.answer: Escape, /rewind, /compact, /clear

choices: Escape, /rewind, /compact, /clear
/clear, /compact, /rewind, Escape
/compact, Escape, /clear, /rewind
/rewind, Escape, /compact, /clear
metadata.explanation: Escape just stops the current response (no history lost), /rewind drops a bad stretch while keeping the rest, /compact keeps a summary for a related task, and /clear wipes everything for an unrelated task — an increasing ladder of how much context is shed.

cards.prompt: A developer recalls an older tutorial video showing a "#" shortcut to quickly add something to memory in Claude Code. Following current guidance, what should they use instead?
cards.answer: /memory

choices: /memory
/compact
Ctrl+M
/rewind
metadata.explanation: The course notes explicitly correct this: the older "#" shortcut for adding memories has been replaced by the /memory command (or editing CLAUDE.md directly).

cards.prompt: A developer wants to add a new custom command /audit that runs a dependency-vulnerability workflow. After creating the .claude/commands directory, what's the correct next step, and does Claude Code require a restart to pick it up?
cards.answer: Add a markdown file named audit.md inside commands; no restart is needed, Claude Code auto-picks it up

choices: Add a markdown file named audit.md inside commands; no restart is needed, Claude Code auto-picks it up
Add a JSON file named audit.json inside commands, then restart Claude Code for it to load
Register the command in CLAUDE.md under a "commands" heading, then run /reload
Add audit.md to the project root instead of inside .claude/commands, then restart the terminal so the new top-level command file is discovered on the next launch
metadata.explanation: The filename becomes the command name (audit.md → /audit) and lives inside .claude/commands; Claude Code auto-picks up new command files with no restart required.

cards.prompt: A developer writes a write_tests.md command containing "Write comprehensive tests for: $ARGUMENTS" plus project testing conventions, then runs /write_tests the use-auth.ts file in the hooks directory. What happens to the text after the command name?
cards.answer: It's substituted in place of $ARGUMENTS in the command's prompt template

choices: It's substituted in place of $ARGUMENTS in the command's prompt template
It's ignored, since custom commands can only ever accept literal file paths as their arguments, never free-form descriptive text
It's appended as a new message after the command's full prompt runs
It triggers a syntax error, since arguments must be quoted
metadata.explanation: $ARGUMENTS is a placeholder that gets replaced with whatever string follows the command invocation — any string, not just a file path — giving Claude direction while still applying the baked-in conventions in the template.

cards.prompt: A developer opens deploy.md expecting to find shell script syntax, since running /deploy triggers actual deployment steps. What will they actually find in the file?
cards.answer: Prose instructions written in markdown that tell Claude what steps to take, which may include running shell commands

choices: Prose instructions written in markdown that tell Claude what steps to take, which may include running shell commands
A bash script with a shebang line that Claude Code executes directly on the shell, completely bypassing the model and any of its usual reasoning about the request
A YAML pipeline definition compatible with GitHub Actions syntax
Compiled JavaScript that Claude Code's runtime interprets
metadata.explanation: A slash command file is a markdown prompt template that Claude reads and acts on — it can instruct Claude to run shell steps, but the file itself is not a script Claude Code executes directly.

cards.prompt: A team keeps re-explaining the same test-writing conventions (Vitest, React Testing Library, file naming, @/ import prefix) at the start of every testing task. Which benefit of custom slash commands most directly solves this?
cards.answer: Context — baking project conventions into the command so they don't need to be repeated

choices: Context — baking project conventions into the command so they don't need to be repeated
Flexibility — via $ARGUMENTS accepting different inputs
Automation — turning what used to be a repetitive multi-step manual workflow into a single command anyone on the team can run consistently
Consistency — running the same steps every time
metadata.explanation: The team's specific pain is re-explaining conventions each time; baking that context into the command file (as in the write_tests.md example) is what eliminates the repetition, distinct from automation (multi-step chaining) or flexibility ($ARGUMENTS).

cards.prompt: The audit.md example command is described as doing three things in sequence. What are they?
cards.answer: Run npm audit, apply npm audit fix, then run tests to verify nothing broke

choices: Run npm audit, apply npm audit fix, then run tests to verify nothing broke
Run npm audit, open a pull request describing the vulnerable packages found, then notify the entire team about it in Slack
Run npm install, run npm audit, then commit the lockfile
Run npm audit fix, run npm audit again to confirm, then deploy
metadata.explanation: The audit.md example finds vulnerable packages with npm audit, applies fixes with npm audit fix, then runs the test suite to confirm the updates didn't break anything — a three-step vulnerability workflow.

cards.prompt: A developer adds a brand new file .claude/commands/deploy.md while a Claude Code session is already running. Without restarting anything, they type /deploy in that same running session. What happens?
cards.answer: Claude Code recognizes the new command immediately, since custom commands are auto-picked up with no restart needed

choices: Claude Code recognizes the new command immediately, since custom commands are auto-picked up with no restart needed
Nothing — the currently running session must be fully restarted before any newly added command file can ever be recognized or invoked
The command works but only after running /reload-commands manually
The command works only in a brand-new session, never in the one already running
metadata.explanation: The notes state custom commands are auto-picked up with no restart needed — the filename-to-command mapping is read live, not cached at session start.

cards.prompt: A developer assumes $ARGUMENTS in a custom command can only be a file path, so they hesitate to run /write_tests "the checkout flow, focusing on discount code edge cases". Is this concern valid?
cards.answer: No — arguments can be any string, used to give Claude context or direction, not just file paths

choices: No — arguments can be any string, used to give Claude context or direction, not just file paths
Yes — $ARGUMENTS strictly validates its input against a list of existing file paths in the project before allowing the command to run
Yes — passing free text instead of a file path throws a template error
No — but only if the string is wrapped in an @-mention first
metadata.explanation: The notes explicitly state arguments can be any string — not just file paths — used to give Claude context or direction, so descriptive free text works fine as $ARGUMENTS.

cards.prompt: A developer is inside an active Claude Code session and types claude mcp add playwright npx @playwright/mcp@latest directly into the chat prompt, expecting it to register a new MCP server. What's wrong?
cards.answer: claude mcp add must be run in the terminal shell, not typed into the Claude Code REPL as a chat message

choices: claude mcp add must be run in the terminal shell, not typed into the Claude Code REPL as a chat message
The command syntax is wrong; it should be claude add-mcp instead
MCP servers can only ever be added by manually editing the .mcp.json configuration file by hand, never through any CLI command at all
Nothing is wrong — this works identically whether typed in the terminal or inside the REPL
metadata.explanation: claude mcp add is a terminal command run outside the Claude Code session, not something typed as a chat prompt inside the REPL.

cards.prompt: After installing a Playwright MCP server, a developer wants to pre-approve it in .claude/settings.local.json so they aren't asked permission on every use. Which entry correctly pre-approves the entire server?
cards.answer: "mcp__playwright" added to the allow array

choices: "mcp__playwright" added to the allow array
"mcp_playwright" added to the allow array
"mcp.playwright.*" added to the allow array, using dot notation instead of underscores
"playwright:*" added to the allow array
metadata.explanation: MCP permission tokens use a double underscore — mcp__<server> pre-approves the whole server (or mcp__<server>__<tool> for one tool) — a single underscore or other separator won't match.

cards.prompt: A developer wants to pre-approve only the browser_click tool from the playwright MCP server, not the entire server. What's the correct permission string?
cards.answer: mcp__playwright__browser_click

choices: mcp__playwright__browser_click
mcp__playwright.browser_click
mcp__playwright__browser__click
playwright__browser_click
metadata.explanation: The single-tool format is mcp__<server>__<tool>, giving mcp__playwright__browser_click — double underscores separate both the mcp__ prefix and the server-from-tool boundary.

cards.prompt: A developer installs a database MCP server. What does this fundamentally give Claude that it didn't have before?
cards.answer: New tools/capabilities it can call, running either locally or remotely, beyond its built-in tool set

choices: New tools/capabilities it can call, running either locally or remotely, beyond its built-in tool set
A faster context window specifically for database schemas
Automatic, unrestricted write access to production data without any permission prompts ever being shown to the user
The ability to bypass CLAUDE.md instructions when querying the database
metadata.explanation: MCP servers run remotely or locally and give Claude new tools it wouldn't otherwise have (e.g., database interactions); they don't grant unrestricted access — permissions still apply — nor do they affect context window speed or CLAUDE.md handling.

cards.prompt: A developer is iterating on a component-generation prompt and wants Claude's feedback loop to include actual rendered visual output, not just source code review. Which MCP integration and workflow addresses this?
cards.answer: A Playwright MCP server: navigate to the running app, generate a component, then have Claude analyze the visual output plus the code before refining the prompt

choices: A Playwright MCP server: navigate to the running app, generate a component, then have Claude analyze the visual output plus the code before refining the prompt
A filesystem MCP server that greps the component's CSS classes for known bad patterns
Raising the /effort level so Claude reasons much harder and longer about likely rendering and styling issues, entirely without ever running the app or seeing any actual visual output
A database MCP server that logs each generated component's props to a table for later review
metadata.explanation: The Playwright example is precisely this workflow — open a browser, navigate to the app, generate a component, and let Claude see the actual rendered output alongside the code, which produces better styling decisions than code review alone.

cards.prompt: A developer just installed a new MCP server and immediately tries one of its tools for the first time. Before adding anything to settings.local.json, what should they expect?
cards.answer: Claude asks for permission on that use, and will keep asking each time until the tool is pre-approved

choices: Claude asks for permission on that use, and will keep asking each time until the tool is pre-approved
The tool runs silently with no prompt, since MCP tools are trusted by default
Claude Code refuses to run any MCP tool whatsoever until every single one is explicitly pre-listed by name inside the .mcp.json configuration file
The tool is blocked permanently until the server is restarted
metadata.explanation: The first use of an MCP tool prompts for permission, and it will keep prompting on each use until pre-approved via the allow array — it isn't trusted by default nor permanently blocked.

cards.prompt: Which of the following best characterizes the range of MCP servers available in the ecosystem, per the course material?
cards.answer: Servers exist for database interactions, API testing/monitoring, filesystem operations, cloud services, and dev-tool automation

choices: Servers exist for database interactions, API testing/monitoring, filesystem operations, cloud services, and dev-tool automation
MCP servers are strictly limited to browser automation use cases like Playwright and cannot be used for anything else in the ecosystem
MCP servers can only wrap read-only tools, never ones that modify state
MCP servers are exclusively for connecting to other instances of Claude Code
metadata.explanation: The notes describe a broad ecosystem spanning databases, API testing/monitoring, filesystem ops, cloud services, and dev-tool automation — Playwright is just one example, not the limit of what's available.

cards.prompt: A team wants to wire Claude Code into their GitHub repo so it can respond to issue mentions and review PRs automatically. What's the setup command, and where do the resulting workflow files land?
cards.answer: /install-github-app, which generates a PR adding workflow files to .github/workflows

choices: /install-github-app, which generates a PR adding workflow files to .github/workflows
/setup-actions, which writes workflow files directly to main without a PR
/install-github-app, which writes workflow files to .claude/workflows
claude mcp add github, run in the terminal exactly like any other MCP server installation, which configures GitHub as an MCP server
metadata.explanation: /install-github-app walks through installing the GitHub app and adding an API key, then auto-generates a PR whose merged workflow files land in .github/workflows — the standard GitHub Actions location.

cards.prompt: A repo has both default Claude GitHub workflows enabled. A contributor opens a new PR without mentioning @claude anywhere, and separately, a maintainer comments "@claude can you add input validation here?" on an open issue. What happens in each case?
cards.answer: The PR automatically gets a review report; the issue comment triggers Claude to analyze the request, plan, and execute with codebase access

choices: The PR automatically gets a review report; the issue comment triggers Claude to analyze the request, plan, and execute with codebase access
Neither triggers anything, since @claude must be in the PR title, not a comment
Both trigger the exact same review-only workflow with absolutely no code changes made in either case, regardless of whether @claude is mentioned anywhere in the PR or issue
Only the issue comment triggers anything; new PRs require a manual /review-pr command
metadata.explanation: The Pull Request action automatically reviews every new PR regardless of mentions, while the Mention action (@claude) triggers task execution with full codebase access and a direct response — two distinct default workflows.

cards.prompt: A team configures a custom GitHub Actions workflow giving Claude access to a Playwright MCP server, expecting that pre-approving mcp__playwright once (as they do locally in settings.local.json) will cover all its tools in CI. Their first automated run fails silently on tool calls. What's the likely cause?
cards.answer: GitHub Actions has no permission shortcut — every individual tool from the MCP server must be explicitly listed in allowed_tools, unlike local interactive pre-approval

choices: GitHub Actions has no permission shortcut — every individual tool from the MCP server must be explicitly listed in allowed_tools, unlike local interactive pre-approval
mcp_config: only works with database servers, not browser automation servers like Playwright
custom_instructions: must be set before mcp_config: takes effect, and they have the order reversed

GitHub Actions workflows are fundamentally and permanently incapable of using any MCP servers at all under any configuration whatsoever, since MCP integration only exists for local interactive sessions run directly by a developer
metadata.explanation: The notes call this out as the big exam-relevant contrast — headless/non-interactive Actions require every tool from every MCP server individually listed in allowed_tools (e.g., mcp__playwright__browser_click, mcp__playwright__browser_snapshot, …), since there's no interactive "allow" prompt to fall back on.

cards.prompt: A workflow file includes a custom_instructions: block describing that the dev server is already running at localhost:3000, logs are in logs.txt, and the database should be queried with sqlite3. What is this field for?
cards.answer: Giving Claude project-specific context it needs to operate correctly in the CI environment

choices: Giving Claude project-specific context it needs to operate correctly in the CI environment
Declaring which tools Claude is allowed to call during the run
Listing all of the project setup steps, like npm run setup and npm run dev:daemon, that must fully complete running before Claude is allowed to start any work
Configuring which MCP servers are available during the run
metadata.explanation: custom_instructions: supplies project context (server location, log file, how to query the db) — distinct from allowed_tools: (tool permissions), Project Setup steps (pre-run commands), and mcp_config: (MCP server configuration).

cards.prompt: A workflow needs to run npm run setup and npm run dev:daemon before Claude begins working on a task. Where do these belong in a customized workflow file?
cards.answer: As Project Setup steps that run before Claude runs

choices: As Project Setup steps that run before Claude runs
Inside custom_instructions:, since they're project-specific context
Inside allowed_tools:, since they're commands Claude is permitted to run
They can't be automated; a human must run them before triggering the workflow
metadata.explanation: The notes describe Project Setup as steps that run before Claude runs (like npm run setup, npm run dev:daemon), distinct from custom_instructions: (context text) or allowed_tools: (tool permission list).

cards.prompt: A team is setting up Claude in GitHub Actions for the first time. Which approach matches the recommended best practices from the course?
cards.answer: Start with the default workflows, customize gradually, test simple tasks first, and be explicit about MCP tool permissions

choices: Start with the default workflows, customize gradually, test simple tasks first, and be explicit about MCP tool permissions
Immediately write a fully custom workflow with maximal allowed_tools permissions configured for every possible MCP tool before ever running anything at all
Skip the default @claude mention workflow entirely and only use the PR review workflow
Disable Project Setup steps to keep the workflow file as short as possible
metadata.explanation: The recommended path is starting with defaults, customizing gradually, testing simple tasks first, using custom_instructions for context, and being explicit about MCP tool permissions — not jumping straight to a maximal custom config.

cards.prompt: A workflow needs to give Claude a Playwright MCP server configured with specific --allowed-origins restrictions during a GitHub Actions run. Which workflow field configures this?
cards.answer: mcp_config:, a JSON block configuring MCP servers for the run

choices: mcp_config:, a JSON block configuring MCP servers for the run
custom_instructions:, since server config counts as project context
allowed_tools:, since it governs what Claude can access
Project Setup, since installing a server counts as a setup step
metadata.explanation: mcp_config: is the JSON block used to configure MCP servers (such as Playwright with --allowed-origins) for a GitHub Actions run — separate from allowed_tools: which lists which of those servers' tools Claude may actually call.

cards.prompt: A developer wants a hook that can actually prevent a risky Bash command from running, not just react to it afterward. Which hook event and mechanism achieves this?
cards.answer: PreToolUse, exiting with code 2 to block the call and send an error message to Claude via stderr

choices: PreToolUse, exiting with code 2 to block the call and send an error message to Claude via stderr
PostToolUse, exiting with code 2 to attempt to block the call retroactively after it has already fully run and completed
PreToolUse, exiting with code 0 and writing a warning to stdout
SessionStart, since it runs before any tools are available
metadata.explanation: Only PreToolUse can block a tool call, and it does so by exiting with code 2 while writing the reason to stderr, which Claude receives as feedback; PostToolUse fires after the tool already executed and can't block regardless of exit code.

cards.prompt: A developer writes one hook script assuming every PreToolUse invocation will include a file_path field on stdin, since that worked for their Read-matcher hook. They then add the same matcher logic to catch Bash calls, and it stops working. Why?
cards.answer: tool_input's shape depends on the tool — Bash sends a command field, not file_path, so the same field check doesn't apply across tools

choices: tool_input's shape depends on the tool — Bash sends a command field, not file_path, so the same field check doesn't apply across tools
PreToolUse only fires for Read and Write tools, never for Bash
Hooks can only ever inspect a single tool_input field per matcher across the entire settings file, and the file_path field name was already permanently claimed by the earlier Read hook
Bash calls don't emit any stdin JSON, unlike other tools
metadata.explanation: The stdin JSON's tool_input shape varies by tool — Read sends {file_path}, Bash sends {command}, Grep sends {pattern, path} — so logic written for one tool's field names won't match another's, which is exactly the trap the course notes call out.

cards.prompt: A developer isn't sure exactly what fields a PostToolUse hook receives for a given tool and wants to find out empirically rather than guessing from docs. What's the suggested approach?
cards.answer: Add a catch-all hook with matcher "*" that pipes stdin through jq into a log file to inspect the real shape

choices: Add a catch-all hook with matcher "*" that pipes stdin through jq into a log file to inspect the real shape
Read through the entire Claude Code source code repository to manually find and interpret the TypeScript type definitions for every hook payload shape
Set the matcher to "unknown" so Claude Code prints the schema automatically
Enable /effort max, which causes Claude to narrate the hook payload in the transcript
metadata.explanation: The notes' explicit "helper trick" is a catch-all logger — matcher "*" piping stdin JSON through jq into a file — to discover the real shape empirically, since the shape varies by event and tool.

cards.prompt: A PostToolUse hook detects that Claude just wrote a file with a duplicate SQL query and exits with code 2, writing the problem to stderr. What actually happens as a result?
cards.answer: The edit already happened and can't be undone by the hook; the exit-2 feedback surfaces to Claude as a note it can act on, but it doesn't block anything

choices: The edit already happened and can't be undone by the hook; the exit-2 feedback surfaces to Claude as a note it can act on, but it doesn't block anything
The file write is automatically and completely rolled back to its exact prior state the very moment the hook exits with any nonzero status code, undoing the edit entirely
Claude Code halts the entire session until a human intervenes
Nothing happens — PostToolUse hooks ignore exit codes entirely
metadata.explanation: PostToolUse runs after the tool already executed, so it can't block; the notes specify exit 2 there "just surfaces feedback" rather than preventing anything, which Claude can then choose to act on (e.g., remove the duplicate).

cards.prompt: A team writes a PreToolUse hook matched on Read that blocks any file_path containing .env, and considers the secret protected. A teammate then runs grep API_KEY .env via Bash and successfully reads the secret. What's the root-cause fix, per the course material?
cards.answer: Use a permissions.deny rule like "Read(**/.env)" that applies across tools, or add a separate matcher/hook per tool (Read, Grep, Bash) instead of relying on one Read-only hook

choices: Use a permissions.deny rule like "Read(**/.env)" that applies across tools, or add a separate matcher/hook per tool (Read, Grep, Bash) instead of relying on one Read-only hook
Rename the hook script to protect-env.js so Claude recognizes its intent across all tools
Increase /effort so Claude reasons more carefully before running Bash commands near secrets

Move the hook from PreToolUse to PostToolUse entirely, since PostToolUse hooks are able to catch and retroactively block Bash and Grep calls that a Read-only PreToolUse hook would otherwise completely miss
metadata.explanation: A single Read-matched hook only catches Read calls (checking file_path); Grep sends pattern+path and Bash sends command, so blanket protection needs either per-tool matchers or a uniform permissions.deny rule that applies across tools — the course notes flag this as the root-cause vs band-aid lesson. PostToolUse wouldn't help since it can't block by the time Bash has already run.

cards.prompt: A hook script is referenced in settings.json using a relative path like ./hooks/check.js. What security concern does the course material raise about this, and what's the recommended pattern to keep the fix shareable across machines?
cards.answer: Relative paths risk path-interception attacks; use absolute paths, and share configs via a settings.example.json with a $PWD placeholder resolved by a setup script

choices: Relative paths risk path-interception attacks; use absolute paths, and share configs via a settings.example.json with a $PWD placeholder resolved by a setup script
Relative paths are fine as long as the script is checked into version control
The concern is performance, not security — absolute paths simply load faster
Hook scripts should never under any circumstances be committed to the repository at all, whether they are meant to be shared with the team or kept strictly personal and local
metadata.explanation: The notes recommend absolute paths for hook scripts to mitigate path-interception/binary-planting attacks, and address the sharing problem (absolute paths differ per machine) with a settings.example.json containing a $PWD placeholder resolved by a setup script into settings.local.json.

cards.prompt: A developer wants a hook that fires either when Claude needs a tool permission decision, or when the session has been idle for a while. Which event covers both cases?
cards.answer: Notification — fires when Claude needs tool permission, or after 60 seconds idle

choices: Notification — fires when Claude needs tool permission, or after 60 seconds idle
Stop — fires only when Claude has completely finished responding to the user's most recent prompt, with no further action pending
SessionStart — fires when starting or resuming a session
PreCompact — fires before a compact, manual or automatic
metadata.explanation: The Notification event covers exactly these two triggers — Claude needing tool permission, or 60 seconds of idle time — distinct from Stop (response finished), SessionStart (session begins/resumes), and PreCompact (before compaction).

cards.prompt: A developer wants a hook to run specifically when a Task/subagent finishes, separate from when the main Claude response finishes. Which event should the hook be registered on?
cards.answer: SubagentStop

choices: SubagentStop
Stop
PostToolUse
SessionEnd
metadata.explanation: SubagentStop fires specifically when a subagent ("Task" in the UI) finishes, distinct from Stop which fires when the main Claude response finishes — a distinction the full event list draws explicitly.

cards.prompt: A team wants immediate, low-cost feedback whenever Claude edits a TypeScript file, catching cases where Claude changes a function signature but misses updating a call site elsewhere. What hook pattern from the course fits, and why is it described as lightweight?
cards.answer: A PostToolUse hook that runs tsc --noEmit after every edit and feeds any errors back to Claude — lightweight because it's just a compiler check, not a spawned second Claude instance

choices: A PostToolUse hook that runs tsc --noEmit after every edit and feeds any errors back to Claude — lightweight because it's just a compiler check, not a spawned second Claude instance
A PreToolUse hook that blocks all Edit calls to .ts files until a human approves them manually

A PostToolUse hook that spawns an entirely separate second Claude instance via the Agent SDK to painstakingly review every single edit made for full type correctness before allowing anything else to proceed
A SessionStart hook that runs the full test suite once at the beginning of every session
metadata.explanation: The course's TypeScript pattern is a PostToolUse hook running tsc --noEmit after edits and feeding errors back to Claude — described as lightweight/fast compared to the heavier query-duplication pattern that spawns a second Claude instance via the Agent SDK, which the notes say should be reserved for monitoring only critical directories.

cards.prompt: A developer runs npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-code inside their Node script, then tries import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-code", expecting to run Claude's agent loop programmatically. What's wrong?
cards.answer: @anthropic-ai/claude-code is the CLI itself and isn't importable; the correct package is @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk

choices: @anthropic-ai/claude-code is the CLI itself and isn't importable; the correct package is @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk
The import statement should always use require() instead of the import keyword, since the Agent SDK package does not support ES modules in any configuration
Nothing is wrong — this is the correct package and import for the Agent SDK
query must be imported from @anthropic-ai/claude-code/sdk, a named subpath
metadata.explanation: The course notes flag this exact naming gotcha as a likely exam distractor: @anthropic-ai/claude-code is the CLI tool and can't be imported, while @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk is the actual importable SDK package.

cards.prompt: A developer runs a minimal Agent SDK script with for await (const message of query({ prompt })) { console.log(...) }. What should they expect to see printed as the script runs?
cards.answer: A stream of JSON messages representing the same conversation events as the CLI — tool calls, tool results, and Claude's text

choices: A stream of JSON messages representing the same conversation events as the CLI — tool calls, tool results, and Claude's text
A single final JSON object returned only after the entire task completes
Plain, unstructured text output identical in every way to what appears in an interactive terminal chat session, containing no JSON structure whatsoever
Nothing, until allowedTools is explicitly configured
metadata.explanation: query() streams JSON messages representing the same conversation events the CLI produces — tool calls, tool results, and text — as an async iterable, not a single final blob or plain terminal text.

cards.prompt: A developer building an internal tool with the Agent SDK wants Claude to only be able to read and search files, never write, edit, or run shell commands. How do they enforce this?
cards.answer: Pass options: { allowedTools: ["Read", "Glob"] } to query(), narrowing from the full default tool set

choices: Pass options: { allowedTools: ["Read", "Glob"] } to query(), narrowing from the full default tool set
Set a CLAUDE.md instruction telling Claude not to use Write, Edit, or Bash
This can't be restricted in the SDK; only the CLI's --allowedTools flag supports it
Use a PreToolUse hook that blocks every single tool call by default, then painstakingly writes custom logic to manually re-enable just Read and Glob
metadata.explanation: allowedTools is the SDK's explicit tool-restriction option, mirroring the CLI's --allowedTools flag; the default is the full tool set, so narrowing it in query()'s options is the direct enforcement mechanism, not a CLAUDE.md request or a hook workaround.

cards.prompt: A team wants their Agent SDK integration to use a custom system prompt, connect to an MCP server, run hooks, and resume a prior session — all things they already rely on in interactive Claude Code. Is this achievable with the SDK?
cards.answer: Yes — the Agent SDK supports custom system prompts, MCP servers, hooks, subagents, and session resumption, the same as the CLI

choices: Yes — the Agent SDK supports custom system prompts, MCP servers, hooks, subagents, and session resumption, the same as the CLI
No — the SDK only ever supports the most bare-bones query and response loop imaginable, with absolutely no hooks support and no MCP server support of any kind
Only session resumption is supported; system prompts and hooks require the CLI
Only MCP servers are supported; custom system prompts and hooks are CLI-only features
metadata.explanation: The notes state the SDK "supports everything the CLI does" — custom system prompts, MCP servers, hooks, subagents, and session resumption — since it runs the same underlying agent loop.

cards.prompt: The query-duplication-guard hook described in the hooks material launches a second Claude instance to review an edit for duplicate SQL before feeding a fix instruction back to the first instance. What underlying technology makes that second, programmatically-launched Claude instance possible?
cards.answer: The Agent SDK's query() function, run from within the hook's command

choices: The Agent SDK's query() function, run from within the hook's command
A second interactive Claude Code terminal session opened manually by the developer
The GitHub Actions @claude mention workflow, triggered from inside the hook
Planning Mode, entered automatically whenever a PostToolUse hook fires
metadata.explanation: The notes explicitly tie this pattern to the Agent SDK — the hook's command uses the SDK to spawn a second reviewing Claude instance programmatically, not a manual terminal session, GitHub Actions, or Planning Mode.

cards.prompt: A team tests a new prompt three times, patches the one weird output they happened to notice, and ships it. Per the course's 'three paths' framing, what risk have they left unaddressed compared to running an evaluation pipeline?
cards.answer: Real production users will hit edge cases the team never anticipated or tested

choices: Real production users will hit edge cases the team never anticipated or tested
The evaluation pipeline will now be too expensive to ever run
Claude will require a longer system prompt to respond at all
The prompt will exceed the model's maximum context window
metadata.explanation: Path 2 (test a few times, patch a corner case) still leaves real users hitting inputs never considered; only the eval-first path (path 3) systematically surfaces those before production. The other options aren't claims the source makes.

cards.prompt: A PM says 'we don't have time to build a whole eval dataset, let's just eyeball a few outputs and ship.' What does the evaluation-first approach specifically buy that eyeballing a few outputs does not?
cards.answer: Objective, measurable metrics across a broad range of cases that let you compare prompt versions with confidence

choices: Objective, measurable metrics across a broad range of cases that let you compare prompt versions with confidence
Guaranteed elimination of every hallucination the model could ever produce, on any input, without any further testing
A prompt that never needs to be changed again after the first eval run
Automatic removal of the need for any human review of outputs
metadata.explanation: The evaluation-first approach identifies weaknesses across a broad test set and lets you compare versions objectively and iterate with confidence — it doesn't claim to eliminate all errors or remove human review entirely.

cards.prompt: Which pair correctly matches the course's distinction between prompt engineering and prompt evaluation?
cards.answer: Engineering is the toolkit for writing better prompts; evaluation is measuring prompt effectiveness through automated testing
