cards.prompt: An agent's answer quality noticeably drops after about 80 turns of conversation, well before the model's context window limit is reached. What is happening, and what does it imply about the fix?
cards.answer: Context rot is degrading accuracy as tokens accumulate, so the fix is curating fewer high-signal tokens rather than adding more

choices: Context rot is degrading accuracy as tokens accumulate, so the fix is curating fewer high-signal tokens rather than adding more
The session has already overflowed the context window and needs a model with a larger window
A prompt-cache miss is slowing down generation and should be fixed with a longer cache TTL
The model is throttling itself for safety reasons and needs an explicit override in the system prompt
metadata.explanation: Context rot is degradation before the limit is hit, caused by n^2 attention diluting focus as tokens grow; switching to a bigger window doesn't fix dilution and can make it worse.

cards.prompt: A teammate argues that context rot is just a bigger version of running out of context window, so buying a model with a larger window should fix it. Why is this reasoning flawed?
cards.answer: Context rot is a reliability phenomenon where recall degrades as tokens grow even far below the limit, so a bigger window adds more competing tokens and can worsen dilution

choices: Context rot is a reliability phenomenon where recall degrades as tokens grow even far below the limit, so a bigger window adds more competing tokens and can worsen dilution
A larger context window costs strictly more per token, and buying more of it is the standard, complete fix recommended for any accuracy problem
Larger windows are always slower to generate from, which is the real source of the accuracy problem
Context rot only happens once the window limit is exceeded, so a bigger window always fixes it
metadata.explanation: Context rot stems from n^2 attention splitting a fixed focus budget across more tokens, an out-of-distribution length effect, not simple exhaustion — so a bigger window doesn't cure dilution.

cards.prompt: Mechanically, why does adding more tokens to a prompt tend to make it harder for the model to find the one detail it needs, even though the model 'read' every token?
cards.answer: Attention weights are a softmax over all prior tokens summing to a fixed 1.0, so each added token gets a thinner slice of that fixed budget

choices: Attention weights are a softmax over all prior tokens summing to a fixed 1.0, so each added token gets a thinner slice of that fixed budget
The tokenizer starts dropping words silently once the prompt grows past a few thousand tokens
Longer prompts are truncated from the middle automatically by the API before being sent to the model
The model caches only the first few thousand tokens and ignores everything appended after that
metadata.explanation: Softmax attention weights sum to 1.0, so more tokens dilute the share of focus any one token receives; nothing is silently dropped or truncated by the API.

cards.prompt: A long multi-document prompt buries the single most important fact in the middle of the input rather than near the start or end. Based on how long-context attention behaves, what should you expect?
cards.answer: The fact is at higher risk of being missed, since middle-of-context content tends to lose out to primacy and recency effects

choices: The fact is at higher risk of being missed, since middle-of-context content tends to lose out to primacy and recency effects
Position within the prompt has no measurable effect on recall once the whole document fits in the window
The fact becomes easier to recall because the model gives extra weight to whatever sits at the numeric center of the input regardless of surrounding content
The model automatically reorders the input so important facts move toward the middle before generating
metadata.explanation: Long context is subject to a 'lost in the middle' effect where primacy and recency dominate; position materially affects recall, and models don't reorder input.

cards.prompt: In one sentence, what is the single guiding principle behind essentially every context-engineering technique covered in this domain?
cards.answer: Find the smallest set of high-signal tokens that produce the desired outcome

choices: Find the smallest set of high-signal tokens that produce the desired outcome
Always load every piece of potentially relevant data so the model never lacks information
Prefer the model with the largest available context window for any agentic task
Keep the entire conversation history verbatim so nothing is ever forgotten
metadata.explanation: The master principle is curating the smallest high-signal token set; hoarding data or history, or relying on bigger windows, is the band-aid pattern the domain warns against.

cards.prompt: An engineer is designing how an agent should reference a knowledge base of 10,000 internal documents it might need during a task. Which approach best matches the context-engineering guidance in the sources?
cards.answer: Keep lightweight identifiers like file paths or queries and retrieve the actual document content just-in-time at inference

choices: Keep lightweight identifiers like file paths or queries and retrieve the actual document content just-in-time at inference
Pre-load every document's full text into the system prompt so retrieval never adds latency
Summarize all 10,000 documents into one master summary and load only that summary every turn
Pick the largest available context window and load as many documents as will fit up front
metadata.explanation: Just-in-time retrieval keeps lightweight identifiers and loads data on demand, mirroring human working memory; pre-loading everything reintroduces the dilution problem.

cards.prompt: A small, fixed dataset of 20 lookup rows is needed on almost every single call, and the workflow has a very tight latency budget where any extra round trip is unacceptable. Does just-in-time retrieval still win here?
cards.answer: Not necessarily, since JIT retrieval trades latency for context economy and a tiny fixed dataset with tight latency needs can favor pre-loading

choices: Not necessarily, since JIT retrieval trades latency for context economy and a tiny fixed dataset with tight latency needs can favor pre-loading
Yes, JIT retrieval is strictly faster and more context-efficient than pre-loading in absolutely every scenario, no matter how small or latency-sensitive the workload is
Yes, because just-in-time retrieval never adds any latency compared to pre-loading data
No, JIT retrieval should never be used regardless of dataset size or latency requirements
metadata.explanation: JIT retrieval trades latency for context economy; at scale it wins, but a tiny fixed dataset with a tight latency budget can make pre-loading the better call — the rare inverted case.

cards.prompt: An agent has 40 tools registered, several of which overlap in what they can accomplish and have vaguely worded descriptions. Users report the agent frequently picks the wrong tool. What does the context-engineering guidance say is happening?
cards.answer: A bloated tool set with overlapping purposes creates ambiguous decision points that make correct tool selection harder

choices: A bloated tool set with overlapping purposes creates ambiguous decision points that make correct tool selection harder
The model needs a larger context window before it can reliably choose between many similar tools
Tool selection accuracy is unrelated to the number of tools registered and points to a model defect
The agent should be switched to extended thinking mode, which is described as the only reliable fix for any tool-choice or tool-selection ambiguity problem
metadata.explanation: Tool design guidance calls out minimal overlap and unambiguous purpose as the fix; a bloated, overlapping tool set creates ambiguous decision points, which is a curation problem, not a window-size problem.

cards.prompt: A system prompt spells out rigid step-by-step logic for every possible edge case the designer could think of, leaving no room for the model to reason about novel situations. What guidance does this violate?
cards.answer: System prompts should sit at the right altitude, specific enough to steer but flexible enough to let the model reason

choices: System prompts should sit at the right altitude, specific enough to steer but flexible enough to let the model reason
System prompts should always be as exhaustive as possible to eliminate any need for model judgment
System prompts have no bearing on reasoning quality since that is purely a function of model size
System prompts should be left blank so the model relies entirely on few-shot examples for guidance
metadata.explanation: The 'right altitude' principle sits between brittle hardcoded logic and vague hand-waving; exhaustive rigid rules for every edge case is the brittle-logic failure mode, not good steering.

cards.prompt: A team is deciding between writing three paragraphs of exhaustive edge-case prose versus including a handful of diverse worked examples in a prompt. Which does the guidance favor, and why?
cards.answer: Diverse few-shot examples, because they act as canonical communication that conveys intent more efficiently than exhaustive prose

choices: Diverse few-shot examples, because they act as canonical communication that conveys intent more efficiently than exhaustive prose
Exhaustive edge-case prose, because writing out every possible case in full sentences removes any ambiguity the model could otherwise misinterpret during a run
Neither approach matters since only the total token count affects output quality, not its content
A single canonical example repeated many times, since repetition reinforces the model's understanding
metadata.explanation: Few-shot examples are 'canonical communication' and diverse examples beat exhaustive edge-case prose; repeating one example or writing more prose doesn't achieve the same signal density.

cards.prompt: You want long conversations automatically summarized as they approach the context limit, with zero summarization code written on your side. Which mechanism fits, and where does it run?
cards.answer: Compaction, which runs server-side and returns a summarized block the client passes back on the next turn

choices: Compaction, which runs server-side and returns a summarized block the client passes back on the next turn
Context editing, which runs client-side and requires you to write the summarization logic yourself
The memory tool, which requires your application to implement and maintain its own storage layer
Prompt caching, which reduces cost but does not summarize or shorten the conversation history
metadata.explanation: Compaction is the server-side mechanism — the API summarizes and hands back a compaction block with no client bookkeeping; context editing and memory are both client-managed.

cards.prompt: After a compaction event fires mid-session, the very next API call comes back noticeably more expensive than usual even though the conversation is shorter. What is the most likely explanation?
cards.answer: Compaction rewrote the earlier history, so the previously cached prefix no longer matches and the prompt cache is invalidated

choices: Compaction rewrote the earlier history, so the previously cached prefix no longer matches and the prompt cache is invalidated
Compaction always doubles the max_tokens setting for the next call automatically
Compaction silently switches the account to a more expensive model tier for one call
Compaction retries the entire conversation twice internally to verify the summary is accurate, which is why the following call always costs noticeably more than usual
metadata.explanation: Compaction rewrites earlier turns into a summary, which changes the cached prefix and busts the prompt cache for that turn — a known collision between the two mechanisms.

cards.prompt: A reviewer needs to distinguish two context-shrinking mechanisms in a design doc: one that rewrites old turns into a lossy gist, and one that deletes whole stale entries verbatim while keeping the most recent ones intact. Which pairing is correct?
cards.answer: Compaction summarizes and loses detail; context editing clears stale tool results entirely while keeping the most recent ones

choices: Compaction summarizes and loses detail; context editing clears stale tool results entirely while keeping the most recent ones
Context editing summarizes and loses detail; compaction clears stale tool results entirely
Both mechanisms summarize history in exactly the same lossy way, differing only in which API version and beta header supports enabling the feature for a given account
Both mechanisms delete stale entries verbatim, differing only in whether the client or server initiates the delete
metadata.explanation: Compaction rewrites into a lossy summary (keeps gist, loses detail); context editing deletes whole stale tool results verbatim, keeping only the N most recent — the two are not interchangeable.

cards.prompt: A design doc says: 'shrink the whole conversation history into a shorter version, with no custom code required on our side.' Which mechanism does this describe?
cards.answer: Compaction

choices: Compaction
Context editing with tool-result clearing configured by the client
The memory tool backed by a database your team owns and maintains
Prompt caching with a one-hour time-to-live breakpoint
metadata.explanation: Compaction is server-side summarization requiring no client code; context editing and memory both require client-side configuration or storage implementation.

cards.prompt: An agent's tool-heavy loop is bloating the context window with dozens of large, mostly stale tool call results from early in the session. The team wants to keep only the most recent results in context while discarding the old ones outright, not summarize them. What is the targeted fix?
cards.answer: Context editing with tool-result clearing, which removes the oldest tool results at a threshold and keeps a fixed number of recent ones

choices: Context editing with tool-result clearing, which removes the oldest tool results at a threshold and keeps a fixed number of recent ones
Compaction, which summarizes the whole history including the most recent turns into a single condensed block that replaces the detailed tool-call log entirely
Prompt caching, which reduces the cost of resending the same tool results without shrinking the context
Switching to a subagent for every tool call so the parent never accumulates any tool results
metadata.explanation: Context editing's tool-result clearing is the mechanism for discarding old tool results while keeping N recent ones intact; compaction summarizes rather than clears, and caching doesn't reduce token count.

cards.prompt: A team configures both thinking-block clearing and tool-result clearing in the same context-editing edits array. What ordering constraint must they respect?
cards.answer: Thinking-block clearing must be listed first in the edits array

choices: Thinking-block clearing must be listed first in the edits array
Tool-result clearing must be listed first, followed by thinking-block clearing
The two edits cannot be combined in the same request under any configuration
Order does not matter as long as both edits reference the same token threshold
metadata.explanation: The docs specify thinking-block clearing must come first in the edits array; ordering is a real constraint, and the two edits can be combined.

cards.prompt: A developer configures context editing to clear old tool results at a threshold. Right before an old result is cleared, what does Claude receive?
cards.answer: An automatic warning to save any critical information from that result to memory before it is cleared

choices: An automatic warning to save any critical information from that result to memory before it is cleared
A silent removal with no notification at all, since warning the model ahead of time would waste additional tokens that could otherwise be spent on the task
A request to re-run the original tool call so the result can be regenerated later if needed
A forced compaction event that rewrites the entire conversation before the clearing happens
metadata.explanation: Context editing and the memory tool pair together: Claude gets an automatic warning to persist critical info to memory before clearing happens, preventing silent loss.

cards.prompt: Which token-shrinking mechanism preserves the prompt cache when only thinking blocks are cleared while the retained thinking content stays the same?
cards.answer: Context editing's thinking-block clearing, when the kept thinking content is unchanged

choices: Context editing's thinking-block clearing, when the kept thinking content is unchanged
Compaction, since it always preserves the prompt cache regardless of what changes
Tool-result clearing, since removing tool results never affects the cached prefix
Prompt caching itself has no interaction with either clearing mechanism
metadata.explanation: Tool-result clearing invalidates the cache, and compaction always busts it; thinking-block clearing is the one edit that can preserve the cache when the retained thinking is unchanged.

cards.prompt: An architect wants Claude to persist notes across sessions by having it read and write files under a dedicated path, with the client application fully controlling where those files actually live. Which feature matches, and who owns the storage?
cards.answer: The memory tool, where your application implements and owns the actual storage

choices: The memory tool, where your application implements and owns the actual storage
Compaction, where Anthropic stores the summarized history on its servers for you
Context editing, which stores cleared tool results in a hidden Anthropic-managed archive
Prompt caching, which persists the cached prefix indefinitely across all future sessions
metadata.explanation: The memory tool has Claude issue file operations that your application executes against storage you control (filesystem, DB, cloud); Anthropic never sees the files, unlike compaction which is server-managed.

cards.prompt: A security review flags that an agent using the memory tool could theoretically write a file path like '../../etc/passwd' if a handler isn't careful. Whose responsibility is it to prevent this, and how?
cards.answer: The application's responsibility, by enforcing path-traversal protection and keeping all paths under the memory directory

choices: The application's responsibility, by enforcing path-traversal protection and keeping all paths under the memory directory
Anthropic's responsibility, since the API is described as automatically rejecting any path outside the memory root before the request ever reaches the client application
No one's, since the memory tool only accepts numeric identifiers and cannot express file paths at all
The end user's responsibility, since they must manually approve every file operation the model requests
metadata.explanation: Memory security, including path-traversal protection, is explicitly the implementer's job, not Anthropic's — the API just relays the file operations Claude requests.

cards.prompt: Two different sessions of the same agent, run a week apart, both need to pick up where the last one left off using the memory tool. What must be true for this to work?
cards.answer: Both sessions must use the same memory tool entry with a handler and backing store that persist between sessions

choices: Both sessions must use the same memory tool entry with a handler and backing store that persist between sessions
Nothing extra is required, since Anthropic automatically restores memory state on any new session by default
The sessions must share the same conversation ID so the API can reload prior turns automatically
A fresh compaction block must be manually replayed at the start of the second session
metadata.explanation: Persistence with the memory tool comes from using the same tool entry and the same handler/store across sessions — it is entirely client-managed continuity, not something Anthropic restores automatically.

cards.prompt: A high-traffic app reuses the exact same 20,000-token system prompt on every single request throughout the day. The team wants to minimize the input-token cost of resending it. What should they do, and roughly how much does a cache hit save versus the base input rate?
cards.answer: Add a cache_control breakpoint on the static prefix; a cache read costs about 0.1x base input, a 90% discount

choices: Add a cache_control breakpoint on the static prefix; a cache read costs about 0.1x base input, a 90% discount
Shorten the system prompt to under 4,000 tokens, since the documentation states caching provides no measurable discount at any prompt length regardless of reuse frequency
Switch the whole workload to the smallest available model, since caching is unrelated to prompt cost
Raise the temperature parameter, which triggers automatic caching of repeated prefixes
metadata.explanation: Prompt caching via cache_control breakpoints gives roughly a 90% discount (0.1x) on cache reads for the static prefix; caching isn't about model size or temperature.

cards.prompt: A workflow makes calls to the same cached system prompt roughly every 20 minutes throughout an 8-hour agent run, well beyond the default cache refresh window. Which caching configuration best fits this pattern, and what does it cost relative to base input on a write?
cards.answer: A one-hour TTL cache breakpoint, which costs about 2.0x base input on a write

choices: A one-hour TTL cache breakpoint, which costs about 2.0x base input on a write
The default five-minute TTL breakpoint, which will still be valid across 20-minute gaps
No caching at all, since gaps over five minutes make caching worthless in every case
A one-hour TTL breakpoint, which costs the same 0.1x rate as a cache read on every write
metadata.explanation: Calls spaced further apart than the default 5-minute TTL benefit from the 1-hour TTL option, priced at about 2.0x base input on a write; the 5-minute default would expire between 20-minute gaps.

cards.prompt: A prompt caching setup worked fine for weeks, then suddenly every call starts paying full price with no cache hits, right after the team added a new tool to the agent's tool list. What most likely happened?
cards.answer: Changing the tool definitions invalidated the prompt cache, since tool-definition changes are one of the documented cache-busting triggers

choices: Changing the tool definitions invalidated the prompt cache, since tool-definition changes are one of the documented cache-busting triggers
The cache_control breakpoint automatically expires after any single day of continuous use
Adding a tool always forces a compaction event that clears the entire cache permanently
Nothing about tool changes affects caching in any documented way, so a completely separate and unrelated account or billing issue must be the actual cause
metadata.explanation: Tool-definition changes are an explicit cache-invalidation trigger, along with image add/remove and thinking-parameter changes; a bare tool_choice change, by contrast, preserves the cache.

cards.prompt: A very short 200-token system prompt is marked with a cache_control breakpoint, but the team never sees any cache hits or errors in the response. What is the most likely explanation?
cards.answer: The prompt is below the model's minimum cacheable token threshold, so it silently fails to cache without raising an error

choices: The prompt is below the model's minimum cacheable token threshold, so it silently fails to cache without raising an error
Cache_control breakpoints only work when combined with the memory tool enabled on the same request
The request is malformed and the API should have returned a validation error instead of succeeding
Short prompts are cached identically to long ones, so the team must be misreading their billing dashboard
metadata.explanation: Minimum cacheable tokens vary by model, and a prompt below that threshold silently doesn't cache with no error surfaced — a known gotcha distinct from a malformed request.

cards.prompt: Between two consecutive calls, the only change made is switching tool_choice from 'auto' to a specific named tool, with the tool definitions and system prompt otherwise untouched. What happens to the prompt cache?
cards.answer: It is preserved, since a bare tool_choice change does not invalidate the cache

choices: It is preserved, since a bare tool_choice change does not invalidate the cache
It is invalidated, since any change to how tools are invoked busts the cached prefix
It is invalidated only if the named tool was defined after the cache breakpoint
It is preserved only when extended thinking is also disabled for that call
metadata.explanation: The docs explicitly call out that a tool_choice change alone preserves the cache, unlike changes to the tool definitions themselves, which do invalidate it.

cards.prompt: When estimating what counts toward a model's context window for a given API call, which of these should be included in the total?
cards.answer: The system prompt, all messages, tool definitions, tool results, thinking blocks, images, and generated output

choices: The system prompt, all messages, tool definitions, tool results, thinking blocks, images, and generated output
Only the user's messages and the final generated output; system prompts and tools are billed separately
Only the text content of messages; images and tool definitions are excluded from the window entirely
Only whatever content was not served from the prompt cache on that particular call
metadata.explanation: Everything counts toward the window: system prompt, messages, tool definitions, tool results, thinking blocks, images, and output — cached content still occupies window space, it's just billed differently.

cards.prompt: A developer building on Opus assumes the model can see how many tokens of context budget remain via an injected budget tag, the same way they've seen Sonnet behave. Is this assumption correct?
cards.answer: No, context awareness with the injected token-budget tag applies to Sonnet and Haiku models, not Opus

choices: No, context awareness with the injected token-budget tag applies to Sonnet and Haiku models, not Opus
Yes, every current Claude model receives the same injected token-budget tag and running system_warning updates regardless of which model family or version is being used
Yes, but only when extended thinking mode is explicitly enabled on the Opus request
No, none of the current Claude models can see their remaining token budget at all
metadata.explanation: The injected budget tag and running system_warning updates go to Sonnet 5/4.6/4.5 and Haiku 4.5, explicitly not Opus — a frequently tested gotcha.

cards.prompt: A request to a Claude 4.5+ model exceeds the context window mid-generation. What happens, and how does this differ from older model behavior?
cards.answer: The request is accepted but generation stops with stop_reason model_context_window_exceeded, whereas older models returned a validation error

choices: The request is accepted but generation stops with stop_reason model_context_window_exceeded, whereas older models returned a validation error
The request is rejected upfront with a validation error, identical to how all previous model generations have always behaved even on the newest 4.5-and-later releases
The request silently truncates the oldest messages and continues generating without any stop_reason change
The API automatically triggers compaction mid-generation and resumes the same call with a shorter prompt
metadata.explanation: On 4.5+, overflow is accepted but generation halts with stop_reason model_context_window_exceeded; older models instead returned a validation error up front.

cards.prompt: A RAG pipeline feeds Claude five long documents followed by the user's question and instructions at the very end. According to the long-context best practices, is this layout well-chosen, and by how much can it matter?
cards.answer: Yes, placing long documents at the top with the query at the end can improve quality by up to about 30% on complex multi-document inputs

choices: Yes, placing long documents at the top with the query at the end can improve quality by up to about 30% on complex multi-document inputs
No, the query and instructions should always precede the documents for best results, since models are described as reading strictly in top-to-bottom order
No, document order has been shown to have no measurable effect on answer quality at any length
Yes, but only because it reduces token count, not because of where attention concentrates
metadata.explanation: Query-at-the-end (documents at the top) can improve quality up to roughly 30% on complex multi-doc inputs; it doesn't reduce token count, the benefit is about attention placement.

cards.prompt: Before asking Claude to answer a question grounded in several long documents, what technique does the guidance recommend to cut through the noise of a large context?
cards.answer: Ask Claude to first extract the relevant quotes from the documents, then answer using those quotes

choices: Ask Claude to first extract the relevant quotes from the documents, then answer using those quotes
Ask Claude to summarize the entire document set into a single paragraph before reading the question
Instruct Claude to ignore the documents entirely and answer from its own training knowledge
Repeat the question three times at different points in the prompt to reinforce its importance
metadata.explanation: Extracting relevant quotes first, then answering, is the recommended technique for cutting through noise; pre-summarizing everything or repeating the question aren't the documented approach.

cards.prompt: A main agent needs to search across dozens of files for a pattern, a task that would normally generate thousands of tokens of grep output. To keep the main conversation's context clean, what should it do?
cards.answer: Delegate the search to a subagent that works in its own clean window and returns only a condensed summary

choices: Delegate the search to a subagent that works in its own clean window and returns only a condensed summary
Run the search directly in the main thread and rely on context editing to clean it up afterward
Run the search directly in the main thread and let compaction summarize it a few turns later
Increase the main agent's context window so the full search output fits comfortably
metadata.explanation: Subagents provide prevention — a noisy source never enters the main thread at all — versus context editing or compaction, which are cleanup after the noise has already landed and diluted context.

cards.prompt: Roughly how large is the condensed summary a specialist subagent is expected to hand back to the orchestrating agent after finishing its focused work?
cards.answer: About 1,000 to 2,000 tokens

choices: About 1,000 to 2,000 tokens
The subagent's entire raw transcript, unmodified, so nothing is lost
A single sentence with no supporting detail at all
Roughly 50,000 tokens, matched to typical document lengths
metadata.explanation: Sub-agent architectures target condensed summaries of about 1,000-2,000 tokens back to the main agent; returning the full raw transcript would defeat the purpose of isolation.

cards.prompt: A tool call in a heavy agent loop returns a large, noisy payload on essentially every invocation, and this is diluting the main thread over time. Is a subagent or context editing the better-targeted fix, and why?
cards.answer: A subagent, because it prevents the noisy tool from ever entering the main thread, rather than cleaning it up after it has already diluted context

choices: A subagent, because it prevents the noisy tool from ever entering the main thread, rather than cleaning it up after it has already diluted context
Context editing, because clearing tool results after the fact is described as always being strictly better than isolating a chronically noisy tool inside a subagent
Neither, since a bigger context window is the only real fix for a consistently noisy tool
Compaction, because summarizing the noisy payload preserves more detail than a subagent's condensed summary
metadata.explanation: A subagent keeps a noisy source out of the main thread entirely (prevention); context editing only removes noise after it already landed and diluted context (cleanup) — prevention is the better fit here.

cards.prompt: A coding agent runs for many hours across several separate sessions. Each new session starts with no awareness of what the previous session accomplished, and work keeps getting redone. What analogy does the guidance use for this problem, and what is the fix?
cards.answer: Engineers working in shifts with no memory of the previous shift; the fix is explicit state artifacts that carry continuity across sessions

choices: Engineers working in shifts with no memory of the previous shift; the fix is explicit state artifacts that carry continuity across sessions
A relay race with no baton pass; the fix is simply lengthening each session so fewer handoffs occur
A single engineer suffering from amnesia; the documented fix is retraining the underlying model on the project's entire history at the start of every single morning
A broken telephone game; the fix is asking the user to manually repeat instructions every session
metadata.explanation: Sessions are described as 'shifts with no memory of the last shift'; the documented fix is state artifacts (progress log, git commits, JSON feature list), not simply longer sessions or retraining.

cards.prompt: A team wants to give a long-running coding agent continuity across sessions using human-readable and machine-readable artifacts. Which combination matches the guidance?
cards.answer: A claude-progress.txt log of completed work, git commits as a baseline, and an init.sh script to start the dev server

choices: A claude-progress.txt log of completed work, git commits as a baseline, and an init.sh script to start the dev server
A single Markdown file containing the entire raw chat transcript from every prior session, kept as the canonical continuity record between sessions
A larger CLAUDE.md file that repeats the full project history in prose form
Nothing beyond the model's default context window, since it retains state automatically
metadata.explanation: The documented state artifacts are init.sh (start the dev server), claude-progress.txt (human-readable log), and git commits (machine-readable baseline) — not a raw transcript dump or a bigger CLAUDE.md.

cards.prompt: Before a long-running agent begins real feature work, a special first session runs to set up the progress log, the startup script, and the first git commit. What is this first session called?
cards.answer: An initializer agent

choices: An initializer agent
A supervisor agent, distinct from the initializer role described for scaffolding
A compaction agent, since it summarizes the project before work begins
A memory agent, since its only job is writing to the memories directory
metadata.explanation: The initializer agent is the first session that builds the scaffolding (startup script, progress tracking, first git commit) before real feature work starts.
